Women age >= 70 or men >= 80, historical height loss > 4 cm, self-reported prior vertebral fracture, OR glucocorticoid therapy >= 5 mg prednisone equivalent >= 3 months

ISCD 2023 Indications for VFA: T-score < -1.0 AND one or more of: women age >= 70 or men >= 80, historical height loss > 4 cm (1.5 inches), self-reported but undocumented prior vertebral fracture, OR glucocorticoid therapy equivalent to >= 5 mg prednisone per day for >= 3 months. Note the T-score threshold is -1.0, not -2.5 or -1.5.
source: ISCD 2023 Adult Official Positions, Indications for VFA

front: ISCD 2023 accepts which precision study designs?
back:
15 patients scanned 3 times, OR 30 patients scanned 2 times (both give 30 degrees of freedom)

ISCD 2023 accepts EITHER: measure 15 patients 3 times, OR measure 30 patients 2 times. Both yield 30 degrees of freedom for statistical validity. Patients must be repositioned after each scan to capture real-world variability. Calculate root mean square standard deviation (RMS-SD) and derive LSC at 95% confidence interval (multiplier 2.77).
source: ISCD 2023 Adult Official Positions, Precision Assessment

front: Per ISCD 2023, when changing hardware (not the entire system) or replacing with the same technology manufacturer and model, cross-calibration is performed by:
back:
One technologist performs 10 phantom scans with repositioning before and after the hardware change; >1% mean BMD difference triggers service

ISCD 2023: When changing hardware but not the entire system (e.g., tube replacement), or replacing with the same technology manufacturer and model, one technologist performs 10 phantom scans with repositioning BEFORE and AFTER the hardware change. A greater than 1% difference in mean BMD triggers a call to the manufacturer for service.
source: ISCD 2023 Adult Official Positions, Cross-Calibration: Changing Hardware

front: When replacing a DXA system with one made by a different manufacturer using different technology, ISCD 2023 recommends:
back:
Scan 30 patients representative of the facility's population ONCE on the initial system, then TWICE on the new system within 60 days; use the ISCD DXA Machine Cross-Calibration Tool to calculate the intersystem LSC

ISCD 2023 cross-calibration for cross-technology scanner replacement: Scan 30 patients representative of the facility's patient population once on the INITIAL system and then twice on the NEW system within 60 days. Measure spine and proximal femur. Use the ISCD DXA Machine Cross-Calibration Tool (www.ISCD.org) to calculate average BMD relationship and intersystem LSC. This intersystem LSC is used ONLY for comparisons between the old and new machine for the same patient.

front: When ADDING a DXA scanner of the SAME technology (same manufacturer and model) to an existing index scanner, ISCD 2023 cross-calibration requires:
back:
Scan one spine phantom on BOTH scanners on 20 different days; if > 0.5% difference in mean BMD, contact manufacturer

ISCD 2023 position on adding a same-model scanner: scan ONE spine phantom on BOTH the index scanner and the additional scanner on 20 DIFFERENT days to establish respective mean BMD values. If a greater than 0.5% difference in mean BMD is observed between devices, contact the manufacturer for service/correction. The tight 0.5% threshold exists because same-technology machines should match closely and patients may be freely moved between them.
source: ISCD 2023 Adult Official Positions, Cross-Calibration: Adding Hardware or Systems

front: Per ISCD, the preferred terminology for a patient with T-score between -1.0 and -2.5 is:
back:
The term osteopenia is retained, but 'low bone mass' or 'low bone density' is preferred

ISCD 2023: 'The term osteopenia is retained, but low bone mass or low bone density is preferred.' Also: 'People with low bone mass or density are not necessarily at high fracture risk.' In pediatric reports, osteopenia must NOT be used; use 'low bone mineral density for chronologic age.' Use of qualifiers like 'mild' or 'marked' osteopenia is discouraged.
source: ISCD 2023 Adult Official Positions, Use of the Term Osteopenia

front: Per ISCD 2023, the ONLY forearm ROI recommended for diagnosis is:
back:
33% radius (one-third radius) of the non-dominant forearm

ISCD 2023: 'Use 33% radius (sometimes called one-third radius) of the non-dominant forearm for diagnosis. Other forearm ROI are not recommended.' The 33% radius is ~95% cortical bone, matching its preferred use in hyperparathyroidism where cortical loss predominates. Ultra-distal and mid-radius ROIs are NOT recommended for diagnostic classification.
source: ISCD 2023 Adult Official Positions, Forearm ROI

front: Per ISCD 2023, forearm BMD should be measured under which circumstances?
back:
Hip and/or spine cannot be measured or interpreted, OR hyperparathyroidism, OR very obese patients (over the weight limit for DXA table)

ISCD 2023 Skeletal Sites to Measure: Routine measurement is PA spine and hip in ALL patients. Forearm should be measured IF: (1) hip and/or spine cannot be measured or interpreted (e.g., severe scoliosis, bilateral hip prostheses, hardware), OR (2) hyperparathyroidism (cortical loss preference), OR (3) very obese patients over the DXA table weight limit.
source: ISCD 2023 Adult Official Positions, Skeletal sites to measure

front: Per ISCD 2023, an anatomically abnormal vertebra may be excluded from lumbar spine analysis if:
back:
It is clearly abnormal and non-assessable within system resolution, OR there is more than a 1.0 T-score difference between that vertebra and adjacent vertebrae

ISCD 2023 exclusion criteria: anatomically abnormal vertebrae may be excluded IF (1) they are clearly abnormal and non-assessable within the resolution of the system, OR (2) there is MORE THAN A 1.0 T-SCORE DIFFERENCE between the vertebra in question and adjacent vertebrae. The 1.0 rule is applied pairwise between adjacent vertebrae. After exclusion, the BMD of remaining vertebrae derives the T-score.
source: ISCD 2023 Adult Official Positions, Spine ROI

front: Per ISCD 2023, which hip ROIs are specifically NOT to be used for diagnosis?
back:
Ward's area and the greater trochanter

ISCD 2023 explicit footnote: 'Other hip regions of interest, including Ward's area and the greater trochanter, should not be used for diagnosis.' Use femoral neck OR total hip (whichever is lowest). Ward's area has poor precision due to its small size; greater trochanter alone is not a validated diagnostic ROI.
source: ISCD 2023 Adult Official Positions, Central DXA for Diagnosis

front: Per ISCD 2023, the preferred method for diagnosing vertebral fracture on VFA is:
back:
The Genant visual semi-quantitative method (morphometry alone is NOT recommended)

ISCD 2023: 'The Genant visual semi-quantitative method is the current clinical technique of choice for diagnosing vertebral fracture with VFA. Morphometry alone is not recommended because it is unreliable for diagnosis.' Severity may be confirmed by morphometric measurement if desired, but visual grading by a trained reader is primary. Genant grades: 0 normal, 1 mild (20-25% height loss), 2 moderate (25-40%), 3 severe (>40%).
source: ISCD 2023 Adult Official Positions, Methods for Defining and Reporting Fractures on VFA

front: Trabecular Bone Score (TBS) is:
back:
A grey-level texture measure derived from DXA lumbar spine images that reflects bone microarchitecture

TBS is a grey-level texture parameter extracted by post-processing lumbar spine DXA images using TBS iNsight software (Medimaps Group). It estimates trabecular microarchitecture: higher TBS indicates finer, denser trabecular structure; lower TBS indicates degraded architecture. TBS predicts fracture risk independent of BMD and can adjust FRAX output. No additional scan is needed; software analyzes the existing spine DXA image.
source: Goel et al. 2023 ISCD TBS Official Positions, JCD 27(1):101452

front: Per ISCD 2023 TBS Official Positions, the TBS value should be calculated using:
back:
L1-L4 WITHOUT vertebral exclusions, even in the presence of moderate degenerative changes and chronic lumbar compression fractures

A key ISCD 2023 position different from BMD: 'L1-L4 vertebral levels, without exclusions, should be used for TBS measurement and to calculate TBS-adjusted FRAX probabilities even in the presence of moderate degenerative changes and chronic lumbar compression fractures.' TBS is less affected by degenerative changes than BMD. Exception: do NOT report TBS if SEVERE structural or pathological artifact (vertebra plana, laminectomy, hardware, metastatic lesions).
source: Goel et al. 2023 ISCD TBS Official Positions

front: TBS is most likely to alter clinical management in:
back:
Patients aged 40 or older who are close to the pharmacologic intervention threshold by FRAX

ISCD 2023 TBS Position: 'TBS is most likely to alter clinical management in patients aged >= 40 years who are close to the pharmacologic intervention threshold by FRAX.' TBS adjustment is most useful when the FRAX result is near the treatment threshold and the reclassification could change management. Also notable: TBS is associated with major osteoporotic fracture risk in postmenopausal women with type 2 diabetes (who often have preserved BMD despite elevated fracture risk).
source: Goel et al. 2023 ISCD TBS Official Positions

front: When BMD is used in FRAX, only which site's BMD can be entered?
back:
Femoral neck

FRAX accepts ONLY femoral neck BMD. Spine, total hip, and 33% radius BMDs cannot be entered. This is a key exam fact: patients with low spine BMD but normal femoral neck may have underestimated FRAX risk. BHOF explicitly notes: 'FRAX is most useful in patients with low femoral neck BMD. Utilizing FRAX in patients with low BMD at the lumbar spine but a relatively normal BMD at the femoral neck underestimates fracture risk.'

front: Per BHOF, pharmacologic therapy should be considered in postmenopausal women and men age 50+ with a T-score between -1.0 and -2.5 AND:
back:
A FRAX 10-year hip fracture probability >= 3% OR major osteoporosis-related fracture probability > 20%

BHOF 2022 treatment thresholds for low bone mass (osteopenia): 10-year FRAX hip fracture probability >= 3% OR major osteoporotic fracture (MOF) probability > 20%. These thresholds come from cost-effectiveness modeling in the US healthcare context. Patients with T-score <= -2.5 or prior hip/vertebral fragility fracture qualify for treatment regardless of FRAX.

front: Per BHOF 2022, osteoporosis can be clinically diagnosed (without BMD criterion) in patients with:
back:
Hip or vertebral fracture (unless excluded by clinical evaluation), regardless of T-score

BHOF 2022: 'Hip, vertebral, and/or forearm fractures are consistent with osteoporosis (unless excluded by clinical evaluation and imaging).' A hip or vertebral fragility fracture clinically establishes osteoporosis regardless of BMD. BHOF also diagnoses osteoporosis with T-score <= -2.5 at femoral neck, total hip, lumbar spine, or 33% radius; OR fracture of pelvis, proximal humerus, or distal forearm in a person with low bone mass.

front: The FRAX 'previous fracture' input refers specifically to:
back:
Adult fragility fracture (low-trauma from standing height or less)

FRAX 'previous fracture' is an adult fragility fracture (spontaneous or from trauma that in a healthy individual would not result in a fracture). Childhood fractures, high-energy trauma (MVC, fall from height), and pathologic fractures do NOT count. This is a binary yes/no input; FRAX does not distinguish number or recency of prior fractures, which is a known limitation. Patients with multiple prior fragility fractures are at higher risk than FRAX estimates.

front: The FRAX glucocorticoid input is activated at exposure equivalent to:
back:
>= 5 mg of prednisone per day for >= 3 months (binary yes/no, no dose gradation)

FRAX glucocorticoid input = 'yes' if the patient has taken oral glucocorticoids >= 5 mg prednisone equivalent per day for >= 3 months (current OR past exposure). Same binary threshold as ISCD VFA indication. FRAX does not distinguish between 5 mg/d and 40 mg/d, or duration beyond 3 months, so high-dose and long-duration users have risk underestimated by FRAX.

front: Per ISCD 2023, shorter follow-up BMD testing intervals may be indicated in which scenarios?
back:
Glucocorticoids, aromatase inhibitors, androgen deprivation therapy, osteoanabolic therapies, malabsorption, severe systemic inflammation, prolonged immobilization, bariatric surgery, surgical menopause

ISCD 2023 Follow-up BMD Measurements: shorter intervals may be indicated with conditions associated with RAPID change in BMD. These include medications (glucocorticoids, aromatase inhibitors, ADT, osteoanabolic therapies like teriparatide/abaloparatide/romosozumab), medical disorders (malabsorption, severe systemic inflammatory diseases), and other conditions (prolonged immobilization, bariatric surgery, surgical menopause). Intervals must be individualized.
source: ISCD 2023 Adult Official Positions, Follow-up BMD Measurements

front: Per ISCD 2023 BMD Comparison Between Facilities, you should NOT:
back:
Apply an LSC or report BMD change between instruments that are NOT cross-calibrated

ISCD 2023: 'Do not apply an LSC or report BMD change between instruments that are not cross-calibrated.' Patients should return to the same DXA device used for their most recent prior study, provided the facility's in vivo precision and LSC values are known and acceptable. If a patient must be scanned on a different (non-cross-calibrated) machine, establish a new baseline rather than reporting change.
source: ISCD 2023 Adult Official Positions, BMD Comparison Between Facilities

front: The ARRT 2027 Bone Densitometry Content Specifications ADDED which topics?
back:
Trabecular Bone Score (TBS), whole body DXA, record results, reconfigure report settings, informatics section

ARRT 2026 announcement of changes effective Jan 1, 2027: TBS, whole body DXA, record results, reconfigure report settings were ADDED to Image Production. Cross-calibration, equipment replacement, and equipment manufacturer change were REMOVED. An informatics section replaced the file and database management section. Window level and follow-up scans (rate of change and reproducibility) were added to each scan area (lumbar spine, proximal femur, forearm).

front: The ARRT 2027 Bone Densitometry Content Specifications REMOVED which topics?
back:
Cross-calibration, equipment replacement, and equipment manufacturer change

ARRT 2026 announcement: Cross-calibration, equipment replacement, and equipment manufacturer change were REMOVED from the 2027 spec. Rationale: these are now considered manufacturer-managed service events rather than technologist competencies. Note: cross-calibration remains testable on the 2022 spec (in force through Dec 31, 2026); it was removed for the 2027 spec.

front: Per ISCD 2019 Pediatric Positions, a Z-score of -2.0 or lower is defined as:
back:
Below the expected range for age

ISCD: 'A Z-score of -2.0 or lower is defined as BELOW THE EXPECTED RANGE FOR AGE, and a Z-score above -2.0 is WITHIN THE EXPECTED RANGE FOR AGE.' Never use 'osteopenia' or 'osteoporosis' terminology for pediatric BMD alone. Pediatric osteoporosis diagnosis requires Z <= -2.0 PLUS clinically significant fracture history, OR a single vertebral compression fracture alone (without trauma or local disease).

front: Romosozumab (Evenity) is:
back:
A humanized monoclonal antibody against sclerostin, with a dual effect (stimulates bone formation AND decreases bone resorption)

Romosozumab is a humanized monoclonal antibody to SCLEROSTIN, a cytokine produced mostly by osteocytes that normally INHIBITS the Wnt signaling pathway of bone formation. By blocking sclerostin, romosozumab simultaneously STIMULATES bone formation AND DECREASES bone resorption (the 'dual effect'). This differs from teriparatide and abaloparatide which stimulate both formation AND resorption. FDA approved April 2019 for postmenopausal women at high fracture risk.
source: FDA Evenity label; Cosman et al. FRAME study NEJM 2016

front: Romosozumab carries an FDA boxed warning for:
back:
Major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE): MI, stroke, CV death. Should NOT be initiated in patients with MI or stroke in the preceding year

FDA Evenity boxed warning: Romosozumab may increase risk of MI, stroke, and cardiovascular death. The ARCH trial showed higher MACE incidence with romosozumab (2.5%) vs alendronate (1.9%) in the first year. LABEL: 'Evenity should not be initiated in patients who have had a myocardial infarction or stroke in the preceding year.' Other adverse events: osteonecrosis of the jaw and atypical femur fractures (as with antiresorptives).

front: Romosozumab is administered for how long?
back:
Monthly subcutaneous injection (by a provider) for a maximum of 12 doses (12 months)

Romosozumab (Evenity) is given as a MONTHLY subcutaneous injection by a healthcare provider for 12 MONTHS (12 doses). Each dose is 210 mg (two 105 mg injections at a single visit). After 12 months, the effect wanes; antiresorptive therapy (bisphosphonate or denosumab) must follow to maintain BMD gains. Romosozumab carries a Boxed Warning for cardiovascular events (MI, stroke, CV death) and should not be initiated within 1 year of MI or stroke. Unlike abaloparatide (which retains a 2-year cumulative cap), romosozumab is limited to 12 doses total.

front: Teriparatide (Forteo) and abaloparatide (Tymlos) carry what key duration restriction?
back:
Cumulative lifetime use of approximately 2 years is the conventional duration; the original osteosarcoma boxed warning came from rat toxicology studies. Forteo BBW and hard 2-year cap were removed November 2020 (use beyond 2 years may be considered for ongoing high risk). Tymlos BBW was removed December 2021; Tymlos 2-year cumulative limit remains in the current label

Both teriparatide and abaloparatide were originally limited to 2 years cumulative lifetime use due to dose-dependent osteosarcoma in rat toxicology studies. FDA actions: (1) Forteo (teriparatide) boxed warning AND hard 2-year cap were REMOVED November 16, 2020 after real-world data; the current label permits use beyond 2 years 'if a patient remains at or has returned to having a high risk for fracture'. (2) Tymlos (abaloparatide) osteosarcoma boxed warning was removed December 22, 2021, but the 2-year cumulative lifetime limit REMAINS in the current label. Both must be followed by an antiresorptive to preserve BMD gains. Romosozumab does NOT have this lifetime limit (12 monthly doses total, once).

front: Per current guidelines (AACE 2020, BHOF 2022, NOGG 2024), optimal sequential osteoporosis therapy is:
back:
ANABOLIC first (teriparatide, abaloparatide, or romosozumab), followed immediately by an ANTIRESORPTIVE (bisphosphonate or denosumab) to maintain gains

Current evidence (especially ARCH and STRUCTURE trials) supports ANABOLIC FIRST followed by antiresorptive. Prior antiresorptive, especially denosumab, may blunt anabolic response. NOGG 2024: 'Following the approved duration of treatment with teriparatide, abaloparatide, or romosozumab (24, 18 or 12 months respectively), initiate treatment with alendronate, zoledronate, or denosumab without delay.' Anabolic gains are lost rapidly without antiresorptive consolidation.

front: Medication-related osteonecrosis of the jaw (MRONJ) is associated with:
back:
Antiresorptives (bisphosphonates, denosumab) and anti-angiogenic drugs; risk highest in IV zoledronic acid users with dental procedures

MRONJ (medication-related osteonecrosis of the jaw, formerly BRONJ) is associated with antiresorptives (bisphosphonates and denosumab) and anti-angiogenic drugs (bevacizumab, sunitinib). Risk is highest with high-dose IV zoledronic acid in cancer patients (~1-10%) vs low-dose oral bisphosphonates in osteoporosis (~0.001-0.01%). Invasive dental procedures (extractions) are the major trigger. Recommend dental exam BEFORE starting and avoid elective dental surgery during therapy.

front: Per ISCD 2019 Pediatric, a vertebral compression fracture in a child can diagnose osteoporosis:
back:
Alone (without BMD criterion), provided it is not caused by trauma or local disease

ISCD 2019 Pediatric: A single vertebral compression fracture (without high-trauma mechanism or local disease) is ITSELF diagnostic of osteoporosis in children, regardless of BMD. The other diagnostic pathway is clinically significant fracture history (2+ long-bone fractures by age 10 OR 3+ long-bone fractures up to age 19) PLUS BMD Z-score <= -2.0. No BMD-only criterion exists for pediatric diagnosis.

front: Per ISCD 2019 Pediatric, the preferred DXA sites for ambulatory children and adolescents are:
back:
Lumbar spine (L1-L4) and total body less head (TBLH)

ISCD 2019 Pediatric preferred sites: PA spine (L1-L4) and TBLH (total body less head). Hip is avoided in most children due to variability in ossification center fusion (which completes around age 13-15). 33% radius is acceptable in ambulatory children when other sites unavailable. Lateral distal femur is used in non-ambulatory children (cerebral palsy, muscular dystrophy, spina bifida).

front: For a pediatric patient with short stature or pubertal delay, the Z-score should be adjusted by:
back:
Using height-adjusted Z-score, BMAD (bone mineral apparent density), or the CHOP height-adjustment calculator

Pediatric DXA Z-scores are reference-matched to age/sex, but small bones read lower BMD (areal density effect). For children with short stature or delayed puberty, apply height adjustment: height-for-age Z-score adjustment, BMAD (bone mineral apparent density, corrects for bone size), or the CHOP (Children's Hospital of Philadelphia) calculator. Without adjustment, small children appear falsely osteopenic.

front: In pediatric DXA reports, which terminology is PROHIBITED?
back:
'Osteopenia'

ISCD 2019 Pediatric prohibits 'osteopenia' in pediatric reports. Use 'low bone mineral density for chronologic age' or 'Z-score <= -2.0 / below the expected range for age' instead. Also prohibited: using T-scores (use Z-scores only). 'Osteoporosis' is used only with clinically significant fracture history or a vertebral compression fracture.

front: The preferred forearm to scan per ISCD 2023 is:
back:
The NON-DOMINANT forearm (unless contraindicated by prior Colles fracture, hardware, or AV fistula)

ISCD 2023: Use 33% radius of the NON-DOMINANT forearm for diagnosis. Reference databases were built on non-dominant arms. Scan the contralateral arm only if the non-dominant is unusable (prior Colles fracture, hardware, AV fistula for dialysis); document the reason.

front: A patient has bilateral total hip arthroplasties. For diagnostic DXA:
back:
Measure lumbar spine AND 33% radius (both hips not diagnostic-usable)

Bilateral hip prostheses eliminate hip as a diagnostic site. Per ISCD 2023, when hip cannot be measured, use 33% radius. Combined with lumbar spine (if that is also scannable) this provides two diagnostic sites. If the spine is also unusable (scoliosis, fractures, hardware), rely on 33% radius alone. This is a specific ISCD indication for 33% radius measurement.

front: A technologist's lumbar spine precision error is 1.5%. Calculate the LSC at 95% confidence for baseline + 1 follow-up scan:
back:
4.2% (2.77 × 1.5)

LSC = 2.77 × precision error (at 95% confidence, for baseline + 1 follow-up scan). 2.77 × 1.5% = 4.16%, rounded to 4.2%. Any BMD change less than 4.2% is within noise at 95% confidence. Note: for 80% confidence, multiplier is 1.81 (derived from 1.28 × √2). For a single measurement vs fixed reference, multiplier is 1.96.

front: In a paired precision study (30 patients × 2 scans each), the RMS-SD is calculated as:
back:
Square root of (sum of (per-pair SD squared) divided by number of pairs), where each pair's SD = |BMD1 - BMD2| / sqrt(2)

For paired (duplicate) precision: per-pair SD = |BMD_scan1 - BMD_scan2| / √2. Then RMS-SD = √(Σ(per-pair SD)² / n), where n = number of patient pairs. This gives the root mean square standard deviation across the group. %CV = RMS-SD / group mean × 100. LSC = 2.77 × RMS-SD (absolute) or 2.77 × %CV (percent).

front: Why are the 30×2 and 15×3 precision study designs both considered acceptable by ISCD?
back:
Both yield 30 degrees of freedom (30 pairs in the 30×2 design; 30 degrees from 15 triplicates since each triplicate provides 2 df)

Degrees of freedom (df) is the key statistical concept. Paired design: df = number of pairs = 30 (for 30 patients × 2 scans). Triplicate design: df = 2 × number of patients = 30 (for 15 patients × 3 scans). Both give 30 df, which is the ISCD minimum for a valid precision estimate. More df = more reliable LSC estimate.

front: A 68-year-old man has unexplained severe osteoporosis, anemia, renal dysfunction, and hypercalcemia. The most important workup to consider is:
back:
Multiple myeloma workup (SPEP/UPEP, serum free light chains, bone marrow biopsy if indicated)

The tetrad of unexplained osteoporosis + anemia + renal dysfunction + hypercalcemia (CRAB criteria elements) demands multiple myeloma workup. Order SPEP/UPEP (serum and urine protein electrophoresis), serum free light chain assay, skeletal survey or low-dose whole body CT, and bone marrow biopsy if abnormal. Myeloma causes lytic bone lesions, diffuse osteopenia, and vertebral compression fractures. Always exclude myeloma in 'unexplained' osteoporosis.

front: The primary radiation protection strategy for the DXA technologist during scanning is:
back:
Maintain DISTANCE from the scanner (at least 1 meter / 3 feet is typical; scatter drops as inverse square of distance)

Distance is the most effective radiation protection for DXA operators. Scatter radiation from the patient drops as the inverse square of distance. Standing at 1 meter or more from the scanner is standard practice. Lead aprons are not typically required for routine central DXA because operator doses are extremely low at working distance. Time and shielding are the other two ALARA principles.

front: The NRC/US occupational dose limit to the conceptus for a declared pregnant radiation worker is:
back:
5 mSv for the entire gestation (with an effort to avoid substantial variation above a uniform monthly exposure)

US NRC 10 CFR 20.1208 limit to the conceptus for a declared pregnant worker: 5 mSv for the entire gestation, with effort to avoid substantial variation above a uniform monthly exposure rate. ICRP recommends a stricter 1 mSv limit. For DXA operators, this is almost always easily met at normal working distance. Pregnancy must be formally declared in writing to activate these protections.

front: Degenerative osteophytes at a lumbar vertebra typically cause the BMD at that vertebra to:
back:
Increase falsely (added mineralized material)

Osteophytes, facet sclerosis, and anterior longitudinal ligament ossification add mineralized material that falsely ELEVATES measured BMD without reflecting vertebral body strength. This is common in older adults and is a major reason hip DXA is preferred in the elderly. Exclude affected vertebrae per the 1.0 T-score rule.

front: Aortic calcifications visible on a PA lumbar spine DXA image:
back:
Falsely elevate BMD at overlapping levels (most often L3-L4) if the calcification is in the projection path

Aortic calcifications, common in older adults, project onto the spine in PA DXA and can falsely elevate BMD at overlapping levels (typically L3-L4). Lateral spine DXA would avoid this artifact but has its own precision limitations. If substantial aortic calcification is present, consider hip as the primary diagnostic site.

front: A patient has pedicle screws and a posterior fusion rod at L3-L5. Best approach for spine DXA:
back:
Exclude instrumented levels; if fewer than 2 evaluable vertebrae remain, diagnose from another site (hip or 33% radius)

Instrumented vertebrae must be excluded from BMD analysis (hardware elevates BMD). In this case L3-L5 hardware leaves only L1-L2 evaluable. Per ISCD 2023, if at least 2 vertebrae remain, spine can be reported with modified LSC. If fewer than 2 remain, use hip (femoral neck or total hip) or 33% radius for diagnosis.

front: ISCD 2023 guidance on using a SINGLE vertebral body for diagnosis or monitoring:
back:
Not recommended for either diagnostic classification or for monitoring

ISCD 2023: 'We do not recommend using a single vertebral body for diagnostic classification or for monitoring.' Precision worsens progressively with fewer vertebrae. If only one evaluable vertebra remains, diagnose from a different site (hip, 33% radius).
source: ISCD 2023 Adult Official Positions, Reporting Less Than Four Vertebrae

front: Measuring and documenting the patient's current height at each DXA visit is important because:
back:
Height loss > 4 cm (1.5 inches) from peak height or > 2 cm during observation triggers VFA consideration for possible new vertebral compression fracture

Height loss > 4 cm from peak or > 2 cm during follow-up is a specific ISCD VFA indication for possible new or undetected vertebral compression fracture. Measure with a stadiometer (not patient self-report). Document at every visit. Height also affects FRAX calculations.

front: A patient's lumbar vertebrae show L1 T = -2.1, L2 T = -2.0, L3 T = -1.9, L4 T = -0.5. The approach per ISCD 2023:
back:
Consider excluding L4 because it differs by more than 1.0 T-score from the adjacent L3; reanalyze using L1-L3

ISCD 2023 exclusion rule: a vertebra with greater than 1.0 T-score difference from adjacent vertebrae may be excluded. L4 at T = -0.5 differs from L3 (-1.9) by 1.4 T-scores, suggesting degenerative change. Exclude L4 and report L1-L3 with an adjusted LSC. Include a note in the report explaining the exclusion.
source: ISCD 2023 Adult Official Positions, Spine ROI

front: Follow-up DXA exams should ideally be performed:
back:
At the same institution, ideally with the same DXA machine as baseline

Follow-up DXA should be on the SAME machine at the SAME facility when possible. BMD values from different manufacturers cannot be directly compared without formal cross-calibration. Even same-manufacturer machines can differ slightly. ISCD 2023 requires cross-calibration before applying any LSC between different machines.

front: A patient's lumbar spine shows grade 1 spondylolisthesis of L4 on L5 with degenerative changes. Impact on DXA:
back:
Potential false elevation at L4 from adjacent degenerative changes; assess using the 1.0 T-score rule and exclude if appropriate

Degenerative spondylolisthesis often accompanies facet arthropathy and osteophytes, which falsely elevate BMD. Apply the ISCD 1.0 T-score exclusion rule: if L4 differs from L3 by more than 1.0 T-score, exclude L4. If multiple levels show this pattern, consider hip as the primary diagnostic site.

front: To correctly label T12 on a PA lumbar spine DXA image, the technologist should look for:
back:
The 12th rib articulating with the T12 vertebra

T12 is identified by the presence of the 12th rib articulation. The scan field should include enough of the thoracolumbar junction to show the lowest rib-bearing vertebra. In patients with transitional anatomy (rib on L1 or missing T12 rib), labeling requires careful landmark integration (iliac crest position helps).

front: Per ISCD 2023, when reporting spine BMD using L1-L3 (excluding L4 due to degenerative change), the LSC used should be:
back:
Modified (typically higher) based on the facility's precision assessment for the L1-L3 vertebral combination

ISCD 2023 new position: 'The LSC should be modified according to the precision assessment for corresponding combinations of fewer than 4 vertebrae.' Precision worsens progressively with fewer vertebrae. Facilities should reanalyze their precision data to derive LSCs for each possible vertebral combination (L1-L3, L1-L2, L2-L4, etc.). Simplified approaches exist in the published literature.
source: ISCD 2023 Adult Official Positions; Rosen et al. JCD 2024

front: Correct internal rotation of the femur during hip DXA is indicated by:
back:
A small or barely visible lesser trochanter

Correct 15-25 degree internal rotation makes the lesser trochanter BARELY VISIBLE (small or hidden). A prominently visible lesser trochanter indicates insufficient rotation (external rotation or neutral). An invisible or not-at-all-seen lesser trochanter may indicate over-rotation. The lesser trochanter is the primary visual indicator of rotation.

front: The femoral neck ROI on hip DXA must EXCLUDE:
back:
The greater trochanter and the femoral head

The femoral neck ROI must exclude the greater trochanter and the femoral head. Ward's area actually falls WITHIN the femoral neck region historically but is not a diagnostic ROI per ISCD 2023. Total hip (a separate composite ROI) includes femoral neck, greater trochanter, and intertrochanteric area.

front: The total hip ROI includes which anatomic regions?
back:
Femoral neck, greater trochanter, and intertrochanteric region

Total hip is a composite ROI including femoral neck, greater trochanter, and intertrochanteric region. Because it includes more bone and has larger area, total hip has better PRECISION than femoral neck alone. Ward's area and the femoral head are NOT part of total hip. For diagnosis, use the LOWEST of total hip or femoral neck T-score.

front: Ward's area (Ward's triangle) is located:
back:
Within the femoral neck, at the junction of principal trabecular groups; it is a small triangular region of lowest BMD

Ward's area is a small triangular region within the femoral neck where the principal trabecular groups converge. Historically identified as the region of lowest BMD, it has clinical interest but POOR PRECISION due to its small size. ISCD 2023 explicitly says Ward's area should NOT be used for diagnosis. Historical exam content may still test its definition.

front: For a baseline hip DXA, ISCD 2023 allows:
back:
Either hip may be measured; bilateral hip scanning is also appropriate for diagnostic purposes

ISCD 2023: 'BMD may be measured at either hip.' Bilateral hip scanning is also appropriate and may be useful for diagnosis (use lowest T-score) and monitoring (use mean bilateral total hip). Document which hip(s) were scanned and reproduce at follow-up. Avoid the side with prior hip replacement, fracture, or significant hardware.

front: A patient had a left hip fracture with surgical repair 2 years ago. For diagnostic hip DXA:
back:
Scan the contralateral (right) native hip; hardware precludes valid diagnostic BMD

Metal hardware prevents valid BMD measurement. Scan the contralateral native hip for diagnostic purposes. Document the reason clearly. At follow-up, continue scanning the same hip as baseline. If both hips have hardware, use 33% radius and/or spine as diagnostic sites.

front: A patient has a right total hip arthroplasty. Best approach for diagnostic hip DXA:
back:
Scan the contralateral (left) native hip for T-score; periprosthetic DXA is a separate protocol not used for diagnosis

Scan the contralateral native hip for diagnostic BMD. The prosthetic hip is not measurable for diagnosis. Periprosthetic DXA uses different ROIs (Gruen zones around the femoral stem, DeLee-Charnley zones around the acetabular cup) and is used for orthopedic follow-up of implant stability, not for osteoporosis diagnosis.

front: A patient has bilateral total hip arthroplasties. For diagnostic DXA:
back:
Use lumbar spine and 33% radius as diagnostic sites

When both hips cannot be measured, ISCD 2023 allows the 33% radius as a diagnostic site. Combined with spine (if scannable), this provides appropriate diagnostic data. If spine is also compromised (e.g., scoliosis, hardware, multiple fractures), rely on 33% radius alone. This is one of the specific ISCD indications for forearm measurement.

front: Approximate weight limits for common DXA tables are:
back:
GE Prodigy ~350 lb, Lunar iDXA ~450 lb, Hologic Horizon ~500 lb, Norland Elite ~625 lb (manufacturer-specific)

Weight limits vary by manufacturer and model. Common limits: GE Prodigy ~350 lb (159 kg), GE iDXA ~450 lb (204 kg), Hologic Horizon ~500 lb (227 kg), Norland Elite ~625 lb (283 kg). Exceeding the limit risks damage to the table motor and gives unreliable BMD (patient may not fit in scan field). For over-limit patients, scan the 33% radius (forearm, table not used).

front: A patient exceeds the DXA table weight limit. ISCD 2023 recommends:
back:
Measure 33% radius of the non-dominant forearm; this is an explicit ISCD indication for forearm scanning

ISCD 2023 explicitly lists 'very obese patients (over the weight limit for DXA table)' as an indication for measuring the 33% radius. Forearm DXA does not require the patient to be on the table. This allows diagnostic BMD measurement when spine and hip are inaccessible.

front: A patient has a congenitally short femoral neck with crowded trochanters. Challenge for DXA:
back:
Accurate femoral neck ROI placement is difficult; document variant anatomy and reproduce at follow-up

Short femoral neck with crowded anatomy can make femoral neck ROI placement difficult. Manually adjust ROI for best fit, document the variant in the report, and reproduce the exact ROI placement at follow-up. Total hip ROI is less affected by this variant and may be the more reliable diagnostic site in such patients.

front: A morbidly obese patient is scanned on a weight-capable DXA table. Considerations:
back:
Use thick mode, ensure the hip of interest is fully within the scan field, check bone edge detection carefully, and consider measuring the spine and 33% radius also for diagnostic confirmation

Obese patients require thick scan mode (higher flux penetrates thicker soft tissue), careful centering (ensure hip is fully within the scan field), and verification of bone edge detection. Consider multiple sites for diagnostic confirmation because any single site may have edge detection errors. Discordance between sites is common in this population.

front: During hip DXA, the femur should be:
back:
In neutral abduction (approximately parallel to the midline) with internal rotation of 15-25 degrees

Standard hip positioning is neutral abduction (femur roughly parallel to the midline) with 15-25 degrees internal rotation. Abducted or adducted femurs project the neck at unpredictable angles. The foot positioner helps achieve the correct neutral-abduction, internally-rotated pose.

front: A patient's hip DXA shows: Total hip T = -1.8, Femoral neck T = -2.4. For diagnostic classification (WHO criteria):
back:
Use the LOWEST T-score (femoral neck -2.4): osteopenia (still > -2.5)

Per ISCD 2023, use the LOWEST T-score of femoral neck or total hip for diagnostic classification. T = -2.4 is in the osteopenia range (-1.0 to -2.5, not yet osteoporosis). Document the site and side used. For MONITORING of bilateral scans, use mean bilateral total hip (different rule).

front: For bilateral hip scanning for diagnosis, which T-scores are eligible for 'lowest T-score' selection?
back:
Either the right or left femoral neck OR total hip; diagnose from the lowest of those four values

ISCD 2023: 'When both hips have been scanned, the lowest T-score of the right or left femoral neck or total hip should be used for diagnostic classification.' This gives four eligible values (right FN, right TH, left FN, left TH). Use the lowest. For monitoring, different rule: use mean bilateral total hip.

front: A patient has bilateral hip scans for serial monitoring. Best BMD value to track over time:
back:
Mean bilateral total hip BMD (ISCD 2023 position)

ISCD 2023: 'When both hips have been scanned on repeat tests, mean bilateral total hip BMD should be used for monitoring.' Mean bilateral total hip has better precision than any single hip measurement because it averages out unilateral positioning variations. This is a DIFFERENT rule than diagnostic classification (which uses lowest T-score).
source: ISCD 2023 Adult Official Positions, Bilateral Hip Exams

front: If the femur is over-rotated (more than 25 degrees internal), on the image:
back:
The lesser trochanter becomes invisible/not visible at all

Over-rotation (too much internal rotation) hides the lesser trochanter completely (it rotates medially out of view). Under-rotation leaves the lesser trochanter prominently visible. The target is BARELY visible (small), indicating the correct 15-25 degree rotation that places the femoral neck parallel to the table.

front: Full-length femur imaging (FFI) on DXA is used to screen for:
back:
Atypical femur fractures (AFF) and their precursor cortical abnormalities in patients on long-term antiresorptives

FFI (bilateral full-length femur imaging on DXA) screens for localized cortical abnormalities in the spectrum of AFF. Consider in patients on bisphosphonate or denosumab >= 3 years cumulative, especially those also on glucocorticoids. The FFI report states presence or absence of AFF-spectrum abnormalities (beaking, transverse lucency, periosteal reaction).

front: Classic imaging features of atypical femur fracture (AFF) include:
back:
Transverse or short oblique fracture, located subtrochanteric or mid-shaft, with localized cortical thickening (beaking) on the lateral cortex and focal transverse lucency

Classic AFF imaging features: transverse or short oblique fracture orientation (not spiral), subtrochanteric or mid-diaphyseal location (not intertrochanteric), lateral cortical thickening (beaking), focal transverse lucency crossing the cortex (stress reaction line). Often bilateral (asymmetric). FFI on DXA detects early lateral cortical changes before complete fracture.

front: DeLee-Charnley zones divide which component of a hip replacement?
back:
The acetabular component (3 zones)

DeLee-Charnley zones are 3 regions around the acetabular cup (I, II, III) used to monitor periprosthetic bone around the acetabular side of a total hip replacement. Gruen zones (7 regions) are for the femoral stem side. Both are used for orthopedic follow-up of implant stability, not osteoporosis diagnosis.

front: The intertrochanteric ROI on hip DXA is the region:
back:
Between the femoral neck and the line just distal to the lesser trochanter

The intertrochanteric ROI is the region of the proximal femur between the femoral neck and a line drawn just below the lesser trochanter. It's part of the total hip composite ROI but not typically used as a standalone diagnostic ROI per ISCD 2023.

front: In older women (>65) with significant degenerative changes of the spine, the preferred site for fracture risk assessment is:
back:
Proximal femur (total hip or femoral neck)

In older adults with substantial degenerative spine changes, the PA spine BMD is falsely elevated by osteophytes, facet sclerosis, and aortic calcifications. Proximal femur BMD is less affected and is the preferred fracture risk predictor in this population. Femoral neck BMD is also the only BMD accepted by FRAX.

front: For FRAX calculation, only which hip BMD value can be entered?
back:
Femoral neck

FRAX accepts only FEMORAL NECK BMD. Total hip, spine, and 33% radius BMDs cannot be entered. This means a patient with low spine BMD but preserved femoral neck BMD may have FRAX-calculated risk that underestimates true fracture risk. Consider clinical context when spine-hip discordance is present.

front: The femoral head is:
back:
NOT part of the standard diagnostic ROIs; total hip ROI starts below the femoral head

The femoral head is NOT a standard diagnostic ROI. Total hip starts just below the femoral head. The femoral neck ROI also excludes the femoral head. The femoral head sits within the acetabulum and is not used for DXA osteoporosis diagnosis.

topic: hip not affected by spine factors
front: Hip DXA is NOT affected by:
back:
Lumbar laminectomy (spine surgery does not affect hip BMD)

Hip BMD measurement is independent of lumbar spine surgery, degenerative changes, or fractures. This is a key reason hip is used as a diagnostic site when spine is compromised. Hip CAN be affected by hip-specific issues: prior hip fracture, hardware, coxarthritis, prior joint replacement, or metal/dense objects in the abdomen or pelvis overlying the hip.

front: The 33% radius site is composed of approximately what percentage cortical bone?
back:
95% cortical

The 33% radius is approximately 95% CORTICAL bone. This is the highest cortical content among typical DXA sites. It's preferred in hyperparathyroidism (which preferentially damages cortical bone) and when obese patients exceed the DXA table weight limit. The ultra-distal radius is the opposite: mostly trabecular.

front: ISCD 2023 indications for forearm DXA include all EXCEPT:
back:
Routine baseline for all osteopenia patients

Forearm is NOT a routine baseline site for all patients. ISCD 2023 specifically indicates forearm when: (1) hip AND/OR spine cannot be measured or interpreted, (2) hyperparathyroidism (cortical loss preference), or (3) very obese patients over the DXA table weight limit. Routine measurement is spine and hip in all patients.

front: Why is the 33% radius preferred in primary hyperparathyroidism?
back:
Primary hyperparathyroidism (PHPT) preferentially damages cortical bone; the 33% radius is 95% cortical and shows changes earliest

PHPT causes preferential cortical bone loss (parathyroid hormone increases cortical resorption). Spine (trabecular) and even total hip may appear normal while the 33% radius (95% cortical) shows low BMD. This is the ISCD rationale for using 33% radius in hyperparathyroidism. After parathyroidectomy, cortical recovery at 33% radius can be dramatic.

front: The non-dominant forearm should NOT be scanned in a patient with:
back:
Prior Colles fracture, AV fistula for dialysis, or other hardware on that side; scan the contralateral arm and document

Skip the non-dominant arm if it has contraindications: prior Colles fracture (fractures falsely elevate BMD), AV fistula for dialysis (altered vasculature and local bone changes), or hardware. Scan the contralateral (dominant) arm in these cases and document the reason for deviation from standard protocol.

front: Under the Bone Mass Measurement Act (BMMA), forearm DXA is covered by Medicare in patients with:
back:
One of five BMMA-qualifying conditions (e.g., estrogen-deficient women at risk, hyperparathyroidism, individuals on long-term glucocorticoids, etc.)

BMMA (1997, effective July 1998) established Medicare coverage for central DXA every 23 months in patients with qualifying conditions: (1) estrogen-deficient women at clinical risk for osteoporosis, (2) individuals with vertebral abnormalities suggestive of fracture, (3) individuals receiving or planning to receive long-term glucocorticoid therapy, (4) individuals with primary hyperparathyroidism, (5) individuals being monitored for response to FDA-approved osteoporosis therapy. Forearm DXA is covered under the same BMMA rules.

front: Forearm DXA can often be performed with the patient:
back:
Seated in a chair next to the scanner (forearm on the scanner), making this site accessible for patients who cannot lie on the main DXA table

Forearm DXA is often performed with the patient seated next to the scanner, with the forearm placed on the scanning surface. This makes forearm DXA accessible for patients who cannot lie supine (severe kyphosis, orthopnea, immobility) and for patients exceeding the main DXA table weight limit. A key advantage for these populations.

front: Serial forearm DXA for monitoring response to therapy is:
back:
Generally NOT preferred because cortical bone at the 33% radius changes slowly; central DXA (spine and hip) is more sensitive to therapy-induced change

Forearm (33% radius) is mostly cortical, which turns over slowly. Serial forearm DXA is not preferred for monitoring therapy response because changes are too small to detect against precision error within typical follow-up intervals. Spine (66% trabecular) shows the earliest and largest therapy response; total hip is next. Forearm is used for diagnosis when central sites are unavailable, not for routine monitoring.

front: Per ISCD 2023, VFA is indicated when T-score is less than what value AND at least one additional indication is present?
back:
T < -1.0

ISCD 2023: 'Lateral spine imaging with Standard Radiography or Densitometric VFA is indicated when T-score is < -1.0 AND one or more of the following is present...' The T-score threshold is -1.0 (not -2.5). The additional indications are age thresholds (women >= 70, men >= 80), height loss > 4 cm, self-reported prior vertebral fracture, or glucocorticoid therapy >= 5 mg prednisone equivalent for >= 3 months.
source: ISCD 2023 Adult Official Positions, Indications for VFA

front: Per ISCD 2023, VFA age-based indications (in the presence of T < -1.0) are:
back:
Women >= 70 years, men >= 80 years

ISCD 2023 age-based VFA indications: women >= 70 years OR men >= 80 years (combined with T < -1.0). These thresholds reflect the age at which vertebral fractures become common enough to warrant screening. Younger patients need additional risk factors (height loss, prior fracture, glucocorticoids).

front: A VFA indication based on height loss is historical loss of:
back:
Greater than 4 cm (1.5 inches) from peak height, combined with T < -1.0

Historical height loss > 4 cm (1.5 inches) from peak height is a VFA indication (with T < -1.0). This threshold reflects likely unrecognized vertebral compression fracture(s). For follow-up height loss during observation, > 2 cm over the observation period is the action threshold.

front: VFA is indicated in a patient with T < -1.0 and glucocorticoid therapy equivalent to:
back:
>= 5 mg prednisone equivalent per day for >= 3 months

Glucocorticoid threshold for VFA indication: >= 5 mg prednisone equivalent per day for >= 3 months. This is the same threshold used in FRAX. Any equivalent dose of other oral glucocorticoids (prednisolone, methylprednisolone, dexamethasone) counts.

front: Another VFA indication (with T < -1.0) is self-reported:
back:
But undocumented prior vertebral fracture

A specific VFA indication: self-reported BUT UNDOCUMENTED prior vertebral fracture. VFA helps confirm or refute the patient's report. If the patient has a documented prior vertebral fracture, they already meet criteria for osteoporosis regardless of BMD, so VFA is less additive (though useful to establish baseline for serial comparison).

front: The typical range of vertebrae assessed on VFA is:
back:
T4 through L4 (upper thoracic vertebrae are often obscured by the scapulae and shoulders)

VFA typically assesses T4 through L4. Upper thoracic vertebrae (T1-T3) are usually obscured by ribs, scapulae, and shoulders, making them non-assessable on VFA. Cervical spine is out of the scan field. Unidentifiable vertebrae between T7-L4 are an indication for additional imaging per ISCD.

front: Per ISCD 2023, indications for follow-up imaging after VFA include all EXCEPT:
back:
All vertebrae normal with no abnormalities

ISCD 2023 indications for follow-up imaging after VFA: (1) lesions not attributable to benign causes, (2) vertebral deformities with known relevant malignancy, (3) equivocal fractures, (4) unidentifiable vertebrae T7-L4, (5) sclerotic or lytic changes or findings suggestive of other conditions. A completely normal VFA with all vertebrae identifiable does NOT require follow-up imaging.

front: Classic clinical signs and consequences of vertebral compression fractures include:
back:
Kyphosis, height loss, reduced lung function, and back pain

Vertebral compression fractures cause a cluster of findings: progressive kyphosis (forward rounding of upper back), height loss (> 4 cm from peak is a VFA indication), reduced lung function (chest cavity compressed by kyphosis), chronic back pain, and sometimes acute severe pain at the time of fracture. Many VCFs are silent (no acute pain) and only detected retrospectively on imaging.

front: Genant grade 1 (mild) vertebral fracture corresponds to height reduction of approximately:
back:
20-25%

Genant grade 1 (mild) = 20-25% height reduction of any vertebral dimension (anterior, middle, or posterior). Grade 2 (moderate) = 25-40% reduction. Grade 3 (severe) = >40%. Grade 0 = normal (no fracture). Even grade 1 fractures are clinically significant and indicate osteoporosis; a single VCF alone meets the clinical diagnosis of osteoporosis per BHOF regardless of BMD.

front: A single vertebral compression fracture found on VFA in a postmenopausal woman:
back:
Establishes a clinical diagnosis of osteoporosis regardless of BMD per BHOF, and qualifies for pharmacologic treatment

A single vertebral fragility fracture in a postmenopausal woman or man >= 50 establishes clinical osteoporosis regardless of T-score. BHOF 2022 recognizes this as a standalone diagnostic criterion. Treatment initiation is appropriate. This is why detecting silent VCFs via VFA is valuable: it may change treatment decisions even in patients with non-osteoporotic T-scores.

front: Per ISCD 2023, follow-up VFA or radiographic lateral spine imaging is indicated in patients with:
back:
Continued high risk: historical height loss > 4 cm, self-reported but undocumented vertebral fracture, or glucocorticoid therapy >= 5 mg prednisone equivalent for >= 3 months

ISCD 2023: 'Use Follow-up VFA or radiographic lateral spine imaging in patients with continued high risk.' This mirrors the initial VFA indications and applies to serial monitoring: same thresholds (height loss > 4 cm, undocumented prior VCF, glucocorticoids >= 5 mg/day >= 3 months). Follow-up VFA detects new compression fractures that may change treatment decisions.

front: A biconcave fracture on VFA shows:
back:
Depression of both superior and inferior endplates with preserved anterior and posterior height

A biconcave (cupping) fracture shows depression of both the superior and inferior endplates (middle height reduced) with relatively preserved anterior and posterior heights. This pattern is common in osteoporosis and is one of the three Genant fracture types (wedge, biconcave, crush). Grade by the degree of central height reduction.

front: Vertebral Fracture Assessment (VFA) is performed to:
back:
Identify vertebral fractures using lateral DXA imaging without measuring BMD

VFA is performed to identify vertebral fractures using lateral DXA imaging. It does NOT measure BMD; it is an imaging assessment for fracture morphology. VFA uses Genant semi-quantitative grading. ISCD 2023 specifies T7-L4 must be identifiable; T1-T3 are typically not visualized. VFA is indicated for height loss > 4 cm from peak, prospective height loss > 2 cm, T-score below specified thresholds with risk factors, or chronic glucocorticoid use. PA spine DXA still measures BMD; VFA is complementary, not a replacement.

front: A patient presents with T-score -2.0 at the femoral neck, height loss of 5 cm from peak, and no prior known fractures. Appropriate next step:
back:
VFA is indicated (T < -1.0 AND height loss > 4 cm); detecting a silent VCF would reclassify as clinical osteoporosis per BHOF

This patient meets ISCD 2023 VFA criteria: T-score < -1.0 (she is -2.0) AND height loss > 4 cm (she has 5 cm loss). VFA is indicated. If a previously undetected vertebral compression fracture is found, she has clinical osteoporosis regardless of BMD per BHOF, and treatment is clearly warranted. Without VFA, she might be classified as 'osteopenia with high fracture risk' rather than osteoporosis.

front: Common DXA phantom types include:
back:
Manufacturer-specific spine phantoms (aluminum, calcium hydroxyapatite in epoxy, or plastic water-equivalent) and block phantoms for QC

DXA phantoms are typically manufacturer-specific and simulate the spine (with known BMD values). Materials include calcium hydroxyapatite embedded in plastic or epoxy resin, aluminum simulators, or water-equivalent material. Each phantom has a certified BMD value, and daily phantom scanning tracks scanner calibration over time. Phantoms cannot be swapped between manufacturers without cross-calibration.

front: Per ISCD 2023, if cross-calibration is NOT performed after changing DXA systems:
back:
No quantitative comparison to the prior machine can be made; a new baseline BMD and intra-system LSC must be established

ISCD 2023: 'If a cross-calibration assessment is not performed, no quantitative comparison to the prior machine can be made. Consequently, a new baseline BMD and intra-system LSC should be established.' Patients effectively 'restart' their DXA history on the new machine. This is why cross-calibration should be planned before the switchover.

front: ALARA stands for:
back:
As Low As Reasonably Achievable

ALARA = As Low As Reasonably Achievable. The guiding principle for minimizing radiation exposure. Three primary strategies: TIME (minimize exposure duration), DISTANCE (maximize distance from source; scatter drops as inverse square of distance), SHIELDING (use appropriate barriers). For DXA operators, distance is the primary effective strategy.

front: Quality Assurance (QA) and Quality Control (QC) differ in that:
back:
QA is the systematic, planned actions and policies ensuring quality; QC is the specific measurements and tests verifying measurable aspects within specified limits

Quality ASSURANCE (QA) = systematic, planned actions and policies that give confidence in quality outcomes (SOPs, training programs, documentation). Quality CONTROL (QC) = specific measurable checks (phantom scans, cross-calibrations, accuracy tests) verifying products or measurements are within specifications. QA is the management framework; QC is the daily measurement activity.

front: For facility-specific LSC calculation, per ISCD 2023:
back:
Each DXA facility must determine its own precision error and calculate LSC; the manufacturer-supplied precision should NOT be used

ISCD 2023 explicitly: 'The precision error supplied by the manufacturer should not be used.' Manufacturer precision is measured under ideal controlled conditions not representative of real clinical use. Each facility must conduct its own precision assessment (15 patients × 3 scans OR 30 patients × 2 scans) and calculate LSC from that in-house data. Required for accreditation.
source: ISCD 2023 Adult Official Positions, Precision Assessment

front: A T-score represents:
back:
The number of standard deviations the patient's BMD is above or below the mean of a young adult peak bone mass reference population

T-score = (patient BMD - young adult mean BMD) / young adult SD. It expresses how many standard deviations the patient's BMD differs from peak young adult bone mass (ages 20-29, NHANES III reference for hip). Negative T-scores = below young adult peak (lower bone mass). WHO diagnostic thresholds: -1.0 to -2.5 = low bone mass (osteopenia), <= -2.5 = osteoporosis.

front: A Z-score represents:
back:
The number of standard deviations the patient's BMD is above or below the mean of an AGE-MATCHED, sex-matched, and (where appropriate) ethnicity-matched reference population

Z-score = (patient BMD - age-matched mean BMD) / age-matched SD. It compares the patient to people of their own age, sex, and (when appropriate) ethnicity. Z-score is PREFERRED over T-score for premenopausal women, men < 50, and all children. A Z-score <= -2.0 is 'below the expected range for age.'

front: Per WHO criteria (postmenopausal women and men age 50+), BMD T-scores are classified as:
back:
Normal >= -1.0; Low bone mass (osteopenia) between -1.0 and -2.5; Osteoporosis <= -2.5; Severe/established osteoporosis <= -2.5 WITH fragility fracture

WHO T-score categories: Normal = T >= -1.0. Low bone mass (osteopenia, preferred term 'low bone density') = T between -1.0 and -2.5. Osteoporosis = T <= -2.5. Severe (or established) osteoporosis = T <= -2.5 PLUS one or more fragility fractures. Applies at lumbar spine, total hip, or femoral neck in postmenopausal women and men age 50+.

front: The Root Mean Square Standard Deviation (RMS-SD) in a paired precision study (30 × 2) is calculated as:
back:
√(Σ(SD²) / n), where SD for each pair = |BMD1 - BMD2| / √2 and n = number of patient pairs

RMS-SD formula for paired measurements: for each patient's pair, compute SD = |BMD_scan1 - BMD_scan2| / √2. Then RMS-SD = √(Σ(individual SD²) / n). The /√2 adjustment for paired measurements comes from the statistics of paired differences. %CV = RMS-SD / group mean BMD × 100. LSC = 2.77 × RMS-SD (absolute) or 2.77 × %CV (percent).

front: Per ISCD 2023, BMD reporting for premenopausal women and men under age 50 preferentially uses:
back:
Z-scores (not T-scores); osteoporosis cannot be diagnosed in men under 50 on the basis of BMD alone

ISCD 2023: For premenopausal women and men under 50, Z-SCORES are preferred over T-scores. Osteoporosis cannot be diagnosed in men under 50 by BMD alone. Use Z-scores with clinical context and fragility fracture history to guide management. T-scores imply WHO classification that doesn't apply to this younger population.

front: Per ISCD reporting guidance, T-scores are reported to how many decimal places?
back:
1 decimal place (e.g., -2.5)

T-scores are reported to 1 DECIMAL PLACE per ISCD guidance (e.g., T = -2.5, not -2.50 or -2). More precision would imply false accuracy given measurement uncertainty. BMD values (g/cm²) are reported to 3 decimal places (e.g., 0.985 g/cm²).

front: Per ISCD, BMD values are reported in:
back:
g/cm² (grams per square centimeter)

Areal BMD from DXA is reported in g/cm² to 3 decimal places. Volumetric BMD from QCT is reported in mg/cm³. These are different quantities with different clinical reference values; never confuse them.

front: FRAX provides two probability outputs:
back:
10-year probability of major osteoporotic fracture (MOF: hip, clinical spine, wrist, humerus) and 10-year probability of hip fracture

FRAX outputs two values: (1) 10-year probability of MAJOR OSTEOPOROTIC FRACTURE (MOF = clinical vertebral + hip + forearm + proximal humerus), and (2) 10-year probability of HIP FRACTURE specifically. BHOF treatment thresholds: MOF > 20% or hip > 3% (for patients with low bone mass T-score between -1.0 and -2.5).

front: In a normally distributed population, approximately what percentage of values falls within ±2 SD of the mean?
back:
95%

For a normal distribution: 68% within ±1 SD, 95% within ±2 SD, 99.7% within ±3 SD (empirical rule). This is why T-score <= -2.5 (osteoporosis threshold) identifies approximately the bottom 0.6% of young adult BMD distribution (actually 0.62% because -2.5 is slightly beyond -2.5 SD).

front: The three principles of ALARA are:
back:
Time, distance, shielding

ALARA principles: TIME (minimize exposure duration), DISTANCE (maximize distance from source; scatter drops as inverse square of distance), SHIELDING (use appropriate barriers). For DXA operators, distance is most effective because scanner output is low and exposure time is fixed by the scan protocol. Lead aprons are typically unnecessary due to very low scatter doses at working distance.

front: The WHO osteoporosis criteria based on BMD were established in:
back:
1994

In 1994 the WHO selected a BMD cut-point of T-score <= -2.5 to define the prevalence of osteoporosis in postmenopausal Caucasian women. These criteria were later extended with ISCD support to men age 50+ and to lumbar spine and total hip (in addition to the original femoral neck). The foundational reference group: NHANES III white women age 20-29.

front: The Bone Mass Measurement Act (BMMA 1997, effective 1998) provides Medicare coverage for central DXA every 23 months in patients with qualifying conditions. The 5 qualifying conditions are:
back:
(1) estrogen-deficient women at clinical risk, (2) individuals with x-ray findings suggestive of vertebral fracture, (3) long-term glucocorticoid therapy, (4) primary hyperparathyroidism, (5) individuals being monitored for response to FDA-approved osteoporosis therapy

BMMA 1997 (effective July 1998) qualifying conditions: (1) estrogen-deficient women at clinical risk for osteoporosis, (2) individuals with x-ray findings suggestive of vertebral fracture, (3) individuals receiving or planning to receive long-term glucocorticoid therapy, (4) primary hyperparathyroidism, (5) individuals being monitored for response to FDA-approved osteoporosis therapy. Medicare covers central DXA every 23 months with these conditions.

front: FRAX clinical risk factor inputs include:
back:
Age, sex, weight, height, prior fracture, parental hip fracture, current smoking, glucocorticoid use, rheumatoid arthritis, secondary osteoporosis, alcohol (3+ units/day)

FRAX inputs: age, sex, weight, height, prior fragility fracture (yes/no), parental HIP fracture history, current smoking, glucocorticoid use (>= 5 mg prednisone >= 3 months), rheumatoid arthritis, secondary osteoporosis (conditions associated with bone loss), alcohol 3+ units per day, plus optional femoral neck BMD. 11 risk factors total (plus optional BMD and optional TBS adjustment).

front: The FRAX 'previous fracture' input means:
back:
Adult fragility fracture (low-trauma); does NOT distinguish number or recency (binary yes/no)

FRAX 'previous fracture' = adult fragility fracture from low-trauma mechanism. Binary yes/no input; does not distinguish 1 vs 5 prior fractures, or recent vs remote. Patients with multiple or recent fractures have HIGHER risk than FRAX estimates. Childhood fractures, high-energy trauma (MVC), and pathologic fractures do NOT count. Physicians adjust management based on clinical context beyond FRAX.

front: The FRAX glucocorticoid input is activated at:
back:
Current or past exposure to oral glucocorticoid >= 5 mg prednisone equivalent per day for >= 3 months

FRAX glucocorticoid threshold: oral glucocorticoid >= 5 mg/day prednisone equivalent for >= 3 months (current OR past). Binary yes/no; doesn't capture high-dose users (40+ mg) or very long-term exposure (years). Clinical context overrides FRAX in these scenarios. Same threshold as ISCD VFA indication. Equivalent doses: 4 mg methylprednisolone, 0.75 mg dexamethasone.

front: BHOF 2022 treatment thresholds for postmenopausal women and men >= 50 with low bone mass (T between -1.0 and -2.5) are:
back:
10-year FRAX MOF >= 20% OR hip fracture >= 3%

BHOF 2022 pharmacologic treatment thresholds for low bone mass: FRAX 10-year MAJOR OSTEOPOROTIC FRACTURE probability >= 20%, OR 10-year HIP FRACTURE probability >= 3%. Patients with T-score <= -2.5 at femoral neck, total hip, lumbar spine, or 33% radius qualify for treatment regardless of FRAX. Patients with hip or vertebral fragility fracture qualify regardless of BMD.

front: Per BHOF 2022, osteoporosis is diagnosed by T-score <= -2.5 at:
back:
Femoral neck, total hip, lumbar spine, OR 33% radius

BHOF 2022: osteoporosis diagnosed by T-score <= -2.5 at ANY of: femoral neck, total hip, lumbar spine, or 33% radius. ISCD 2023 criteria are similar but exclude 33% radius from routine diagnosis (only allowed in specific circumstances). Use the LOWEST T-score from the eligible sites for diagnostic classification.

front: Per ISCD 2023, osteoporosis may be diagnosed by T-score <= -2.5 at:
back:
Lumbar spine, total hip, femoral neck; 33% radius may be utilized in certain circumstances

ISCD 2023: Osteoporosis may be diagnosed in postmenopausal women and men age 50+ if the T-score of the lumbar spine, total hip, or femoral neck is <= -2.5. The 33% radius may be utilized in certain circumstances (hip/spine not measurable, hyperparathyroidism, obesity over table weight limit). Ward's area and greater trochanter are explicitly NOT for diagnosis.
source: ISCD 2023 Adult Official Positions, Central DXA for Diagnosis

front: The WHO international reference standard for osteoporosis diagnosis is T <= -2.5 at which site?
back:
Femoral neck

The WHO international reference standard is T <= -2.5 at the FEMORAL NECK, calculated from the NHANES III white female age 20-29 database. While osteoporosis may ALSO be diagnosed by T-score <= -2.5 at the lumbar spine or total hip in postmenopausal women or men age 50+, the WHO REFERENCE STANDARD is specifically at the femoral neck.

front: Low bone mass (formerly osteopenia) is defined by T-score:
back:
Between -1.0 and -2.5

Low bone mass (preferred term per ISCD 2023, formerly 'osteopenia') is defined by T-score between -1.0 and -2.5 (i.e., lower than -1.0 but higher than -2.5). 'Osteopenia' is retained but 'low bone mass' or 'low bone density' is preferred. People with low bone mass are not necessarily at high fracture risk; FRAX is used to identify those who would benefit from treatment.

front: 'Severe' or 'established' osteoporosis by WHO criteria is defined as:
back:
T-score <= -2.5 AND one or more fragility fractures

Severe/established osteoporosis (WHO): T-score <= -2.5 PLUS one or more fragility fractures. This category identifies patients with both low bone mass AND demonstrated fracture risk manifestation, typically requiring aggressive treatment. The 'severe' qualifier is one of the few descriptors ISCD 2023 endorses (avoiding terms like 'mild' or 'marked' osteopenia).

front: Per ISCD 2023, patients having serial DXA should return to:
back:
The SAME DXA device that performed their most recent prior study, provided facility precision and LSC are known and acceptable

ISCD 2023: 'Patients should return to the same DXA device that was used to perform their most recent prior study, provided that the facility in vivo precision and LSC values are known and do not exceed established maximum values.' Without this, apparent 'change' could be cross-facility calibration artifact. If forced to use a different scanner, it should be cross-calibrated or a new baseline established.

front: Per ISCD 2023, if local reference data are available, they should be used to:
back:
Calculate only Z-scores, but NOT T-scores

ISCD 2023: 'If local reference data are available they should be used to calculate only Z-scores but not T-scores.' This preserves the international WHO T-score standard (NHANES III white female 20-29 for hip) for consistent diagnostic classification while allowing Z-scores to reflect local age-matched norms.

front: After 3-5 years of alendronate or 3 years of zoledronic acid in lower-risk patients, BHOF 2022 suggests consideration of:
back:
A drug holiday (temporary cessation) with monitoring; can reduce AFF/ONJ risk while retaining some protection due to bone-bound bisphosphonate

BHOF 2022: After 3-5 years of alendronate/risedronate or 3 years of IV zoledronic acid in lower-risk patients, consider a 'drug holiday.' Rationale: bisphosphonate-bound bone retains some protection for 1-5+ years after discontinuation; risk of rare adverse effects (AFF, ONJ) increases with duration. Monitor BMD; resume therapy if bone loss occurs. HIGH-risk patients may continue beyond 5 years.

front: After discontinuing bisphosphonate therapy:
back:
Bisphosphonate remains bound to bone for years; residual effect provides some protection during drug holiday

Bisphosphonates bind tightly to hydroxyapatite in bone and can remain there for YEARS. This provides the rationale for drug holidays after 3-5 years of therapy; residual bone-bound bisphosphonate continues to inhibit osteoclast activity and may reduce AFF/ONJ risk. This is DIFFERENT from denosumab, which does not bind bone and whose effect reverses rapidly upon cessation.

front: Denosumab (Prolia) for osteoporosis is dosed:
back:
60 mg subcutaneous injection every 6 months

Denosumab: 60 mg subcutaneous injection every 6 MONTHS. Administered in clinic by a healthcare provider. Unlike bisphosphonates, no renal dose adjustment required (useful in CKD). Effective for osteoporosis, cancer treatment-induced bone loss, and Giant Cell Tumor of bone (at higher dose). Never miss a dose due to rebound effect.

front: Teriparatide (Forteo) is:
back:
Recombinant human PTH 1-34; given as daily SC injection, it is an ANABOLIC drug that stimulates bone formation

Teriparatide = recombinant human parathyroid hormone fragment (PTH 1-34). PARADOXICAL: continuous high PTH (as in hyperparathyroidism) causes bone loss, but INTERMITTENT daily SC injection stimulates bone FORMATION preferentially over resorption. This is the first anabolic osteoporosis drug. Daily SC injection (20 mcg) for up to 2 years cumulative lifetime.

front: Romosozumab is dosed as:
back:
Monthly subcutaneous (two 105 mg SC injections at a single visit = 210 mg) for a MAXIMUM OF 12 MONTHS (12 doses)

Romosozumab: 210 mg SC monthly (given as two separate 105 mg injections at a single visit), for a maximum of 12 DOSES (12 months). Administered by a healthcare provider. After 12 months, the anabolic effect wanes. An antiresorptive (bisphosphonate or denosumab) should be given after romosozumab to maintain BMD gains.

front: Atypical femur fractures (AFF) are associated with:
back:
LONG-TERM (>= 3-5 years cumulative) bisphosphonate or denosumab therapy; risk increases with duration, especially with concomitant glucocorticoid use

AFF risk INCREASES with duration of antiresorptive therapy, especially beyond 3-5 years. Also increased with concomitant glucocorticoid use. AFF risk is one reason for drug holidays after 3-5 years of alendronate or 3 years of zoledronic acid in lower-risk patients. Absolute risk of AFF is LOW (about 1 per 1000-10000 patient-years) compared to the benefit of fracture prevention.

front: For a typical postmenopausal woman with osteoporosis and no contraindications, first-line therapy usually is:
back:
Oral bisphosphonate (alendronate, risedronate) as first-line; IV zoledronic acid for intolerance/compliance issues; denosumab as alternative

For typical postmenopausal osteoporosis without very high fracture risk: first-line = oral bisphosphonate. IV zoledronic acid if oral not tolerated. Denosumab as alternative (especially useful in CKD). For VERY HIGH fracture risk patients (multiple vertebral fractures, very low T-score, prior hip fracture), anabolic-first approach with teriparatide, abaloparatide, or romosozumab is increasingly preferred.

front: AACE 2020 'very high fracture risk' criteria that favor starting with an anabolic agent include:
back:
Recent fragility fracture (within 12 months), fractures on therapy, multiple vertebral fractures, very low T-score (<= -3.0), high fall risk with injury, very high FRAX (MOF > 30% or hip > 4.5%)

AACE 2020 defines very high fracture risk patients who benefit from anabolic-first therapy: recent fragility fracture (within 12 months), fractures while on osteoporosis therapy, multiple fragility fractures, fractures while on high-risk medications, very low T-score (<= -3.0), high risk for falls or injurious falls, very high FRAX risk (MOF > 30%, hip > 4.5%). These patients may start with teriparatide/abaloparatide/romosozumab, consolidated with antiresorptive.

front: Glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis (GIO) management:
back:
Adequate calcium and vitamin D, PLUS a bone-protective agent for patients on >= 5 mg prednisone equivalent for >= 3 months (bisphosphonates first-line; teriparatide for high risk)

GIO is the most common cause of drug-induced secondary osteoporosis. Bone loss is rapid in the first 3-6 months of therapy. For patients on >= 5 mg prednisone equivalent for >= 3 months: ensure adequate calcium (1000-1200 mg) and vitamin D, plus bone-protective therapy. First-line: oral or IV bisphosphonate. High-risk patients (fracture, very low BMD): consider teriparatide, which has specific evidence in GIO.

front: Romosozumab (Evenity) patient selection considerations:
back:
Postmenopausal women at HIGH fracture risk; avoid in patients with MI or stroke within the past year; cost and monthly visit burden limit routine use

Romosozumab is approved for postmenopausal women with osteoporosis at HIGH risk of fracture (multiple risk factors, failure/intolerance of other therapies). Not recommended initiation in patients with MI or stroke within past year (boxed warning). Cost and burden of monthly provider visits limit use. Must be followed by antiresorptive after 12 months.

front: Teriparatide (Forteo) is administered:
back:
20 mcg subcutaneous DAILY self-injection, rotating sites

Teriparatide: 20 mcg subcutaneous injection DAILY, patient self-administers (rotating sites: thigh, abdomen). Prefilled pen dispenses 28 doses. Refrigerate. For up to 2 years cumulative lifetime. Daily dosing is critical because INTERMITTENT PTH stimulates bone formation; continuous high PTH causes bone loss.

front: Osteocytes are bone cells that:
back:
Are entombed osteoblasts; act as MECHANOSENSORS and signaling cells via RANKL, OPG, and sclerostin production

Osteocytes are former osteoblasts entombed in mineralized bone. Most abundant bone cell (90%+ of cells in mature bone). MECHANOSENSORS detect loading/strain. Signal via canalicular network. Produce SCLEROSTIN (Wnt pathway inhibitor; target of romosozumab) and regulate RANKL/OPG balance controlling osteoclast activity.

front: Peak bone mass is typically achieved:
back:
By the late 20s to early 30s (approximately ages 25-30)

Peak bone mass achieved by late 20s to early 30s. Higher peak provides greater reserve against age-related loss. Factors: genetics (dominant), adequate calcium and vitamin D, weight-bearing exercise, hormone status. After peak, bone mass is relatively stable until age 40-50 when gradual loss begins.

front: Accelerated bone loss in menopause is primarily caused by:
back:
ESTROGEN DEFICIENCY: estrogen normally restrains osteoclast activity; its loss causes rapid turnover with resorption exceeding formation

Menopausal bone loss driven by ESTROGEN DEFICIENCY. Estrogen normally restrains osteoclast activity and promotes osteoclast apoptosis. Its loss increases RANKL and decreases OPG, leading to excessive osteoclast activity. Most rapid loss in first 5-10 years post-menopause (especially trabecular sites). Women can lose ~20% of bone mass in this period.

front: Primary hyperparathyroidism (PHPT) affects bone by:
back:
Preferentially CORTICAL bone loss from chronic PTH elevation; 33% radius (95% cortical) is the recommended DXA site

PHPT causes preferential CORTICAL bone loss because chronic elevated PTH stimulates cortical osteoclast activity. Spine (trabecular) may appear relatively preserved while 33% radius (95% cortical) shows low BMD. ISCD 2023 recommends 33% radius in PHPT. Post-parathyroidectomy, cortical bone recovers substantially. Paradox: teriparatide (INTERMITTENT PTH) stimulates formation; continuous PTH causes resorption.

front: Under the Bone Mass Measurement Act (BMMA), Medicare covers central DXA every:
back:
23 months for qualifying patients; more frequent testing requires documented medical necessity

BMMA 1997 (effective July 1998) covers central DXA every 23 MONTHS for qualifying Medicare patients. More frequent testing requires documented medical necessity (rapid bone loss, therapy change). The 5 qualifying conditions: estrogen-deficient women at risk, x-ray findings suggestive of VCF, long-term glucocorticoids, PHPT, monitoring response to FDA-approved therapy.

front: Smoking affects bone health by:
back:
Decreasing BMD and increasing fracture risk; mechanisms include direct toxicity to bone cells, reduced calcium absorption, lower estrogen levels in women, earlier menopause

Smoking is a FRAX risk factor. Mechanisms: direct toxicity to osteoblasts and osteocytes; reduced calcium absorption; lower estrogen levels and earlier menopause in women; lower peak bone mass if smoking starts young; impaired fracture healing. Smoking cessation is part of osteoporosis prevention and treatment.
