front: Before starting DXA, verify:
back:
Clinical indications are documented (meeting BMMA criteria if Medicare), order is current, patient identity confirmed, correct scan sites ordered

Pre-scan verification: current, valid order; documented clinical indications (for Medicare, verify BMMA criteria met); correct sites ordered (usually bilateral hips and lumbar spine for most patients); correct patient identified with 2 identifiers; pregnancy screened if applicable; metal objects removed; gown provided. Missing documentation can lead to denied Medicare reimbursement.

front: In advanced ankylosing spondylitis (AS), PA spine DXA:
back:
May be FALSELY ELEVATED by syndesmophytes and ligamentous ossification, obscuring true osteoporosis; vertebral fractures are common despite apparently normal BMD

In advanced AS with bamboo spine appearance, syndesmophytes and anterior longitudinal ligament ossification falsely elevate PA spine BMD while the underlying vertebral bodies may be severely osteoporotic. Vertebral fractures are common. Use hip DXA for diagnosis; consider lateral spine DXA (isolates vertebral body) or QCT for the spine. Patients with AS are at increased fracture risk and should be monitored.

front: For pediatric patients, DXA BMD should be reported using:
back:
Z-scores only (age-, sex-, and in some cases ethnicity-matched); T-scores must NOT be used in pediatric reports

ISCD 2019 Pediatric: use Z-SCORES only for pediatric DXA reports. T-scores reference young adult peak bone mass and are meaningless/misleading in growing children. Z-scores are age-, sex-, and ethnicity-matched. Z-score <= -2.0 is defined as 'below the expected range for age.'

front: Clinically significant fracture history for pediatric osteoporosis diagnosis (combined with Z-score <= -2.0) is:
back:
2 or more long-bone fractures by age 10, OR 3 or more long-bone fractures up to age 19

ISCD 2019 Pediatric clinically significant fracture history: 2+ long-bone fractures by age 10, OR 3+ long-bone fractures up to age 19. Combined with Z-score <= -2.0, establishes pediatric osteoporosis diagnosis. Fractures from high-trauma mechanisms may still contribute but are weighed with clinical context.

front: In pediatric patients at risk for vertebral fractures (e.g., long-term glucocorticoids, OI, leukemia), vertebral fracture assessment:
back:
Should be considered; VFA on DXA or lateral spine radiographs can detect silent VCFs that change management

Pediatric VFA is increasingly used in children at risk for vertebral fractures: long-term glucocorticoids, OI, leukemia, chronic inflammatory disease. Silent VCFs are common and impact treatment decisions. Use Genant semi-quantitative method (same as adult). VFA dose is lower than plain film lateral spine. In young children, dedicated lateral spine radiography may give better image quality.

front: For serial body composition DXA monitoring:
back:
Use the SAME scanner, SAME positioning, SAME scan mode; establish facility LSC for body composition; hydration status affects lean mass measurement

Serial body composition: same scanner, same positioning (NHANES standard), same scan mode; establish facility LSC for body composition; note that HYDRATION STATUS affects lean mass measurement (fluid is counted as lean). Standardize preparation (eat, drink, last void similar before each scan). Body composition LSC is often worse than BMD LSC.

front: A premenopausal woman with low BMD should:
back:
Undergo evaluation for secondary causes (menstrual/nutritional history, amenorrhea, anorexia, celiac, endocrine conditions); diagnose osteoporosis only with Z-score <= -2.0 plus fragility fractures or secondary causes; pharmacologic therapy is generally deferred

Premenopausal women with low BMD: evaluate for secondary causes (hypothalamic amenorrhea, anorexia, celiac, endocrine conditions, eating disorders). Z-scores (not T-scores) are used for interpretation. Osteoporosis diagnosis requires Z-score <= -2.0 PLUS fragility fractures or a clear secondary cause. Pharmacologic therapy often deferred unless severe; most benefit is from addressing the underlying cause, nutritional rehabilitation, and hormone correction if applicable.

front: For serial hip DXA monitoring:
back:
Scan the SAME hip as baseline each visit; reproduce positioning and ROI placement exactly

For serial monitoring, scan the same hip as baseline. Different hips may have slightly different BMDs; switching sides introduces variability. Reproduce all positioning details: femur rotation, foot positioner, scan mode, centering. Use copy/compare function. For bilateral hip, ISCD 2023 uses lowest T-score of any site for diagnosis but MEAN BILATERAL TOTAL HIP for monitoring.

front: A new vertebral fracture detected on follow-up VFA should:
back:
Prompt reconsideration of therapy: may indicate treatment failure or progression despite therapy; consider switching to a different class (e.g., antiresorptive to anabolic) in very high risk patients

A new VCF on therapy is a sentinel event. May indicate treatment failure, progression, or an adverse drug effect (e.g., AFF is a different concern). Reconsider management: verify adherence, check secondary causes (especially vitamin D), consider switching to anabolic therapy if on antiresorptive, consider more intensive monitoring. Communicate findings promptly to the referring provider.

front: FRAX can be calculated:
back:
With OR without BMD (BMD is optional); patients with femoral neck BMD entered have more accurate estimation

FRAX can be calculated WITH or WITHOUT BMD. Without BMD, FRAX uses clinical risk factors alone (age, sex, BMI, prior fracture, parental hip, smoking, glucocorticoid, RA, secondary OP, alcohol). With BMD (femoral neck only), estimation is more accurate. For screening in primary care without DXA access, clinical FRAX can identify those needing further workup.

front: An advantage of denosumab over bisphosphonates is:
back:
Can be used in CKD (including advanced CKD) without renal dose adjustment; bisphosphonates are contraindicated below CrCl 30-35 mL/min

Denosumab is not cleared by kidneys, so it does not require dose adjustment in renal impairment and can be used in advanced CKD where bisphosphonates are contraindicated. Important caveat: denosumab carries increased HYPOCALCEMIA risk in CKD (especially advanced CKD and dialysis patients; 2024 FDA boxed warning). Correct hypocalcemia and ensure adequate vitamin D before dosing.

front: On a follow-up hip DXA, if the lesser trochanter appears larger than on baseline:
back:
Indicates INSUFFICIENT internal rotation at the current visit; reposition with more internal rotation to match baseline before scanning

A MORE PROMINENT lesser trochanter at follow-up indicates insufficient rotation (external or neutral vs proper 15-25° internal). Reposition with more internal rotation to match baseline. Rotational inconsistency is a major source of precision error at the femoral neck ROI. Compare to the baseline image side by side before starting the scan.

front: Local reference data from a specific population (e.g., Korean, Japanese) per ISCD 2023:
back:
Should be used ONLY for Z-scores, not T-scores; preserves international WHO T-score standard

ISCD 2023: 'If local reference data are available they should be used to calculate only Z-scores but not T-scores.' Preserves the international WHO T-score standard (NHANES III white female 20-29 for hip) for consistent diagnostic classification. Z-scores should reflect age-matched local norms where adequate data exist.

front: Cross-calibration protocols (10 phantom scans, 30 patients, 20 days, etc.):
back:
Still testable on the 2022 spec (valid through Dec 31, 2026); REMOVED from the 2027 spec starting Jan 1, 2027

Cross-calibration IS testable on the 2022 ARRT BD spec valid through Dec 31, 2026. It was REMOVED from the 2027 spec effective Jan 1, 2027. So if testing in 2026, know cross-calibration protocols (10 phantoms for hardware change, 30 patients for different-manufacturer change, 20 days for same-model addition, and the corresponding thresholds: 1%, ISCD tool, 0.5%).

front: Why are the 30 × 2 and 15 × 3 precision study designs both acceptable?
back:
Both yield 30 degrees of freedom (df); 30 pairs in 30×2; 15 patients × 2 df each in 15×3 = 30 df

Degrees of freedom is the key statistical concept. Paired design (30 × 2): df = number of pairs = 30. Triplicate design (15 × 3): df = 2 × number of patients = 30 (each triplicate provides 2 df). Both give 30 df, ISCD minimum for valid precision estimate. More df = more reliable LSC estimate.

front: Idiopathic juvenile osteoporosis (IJO) is:
back:
A RARE primary pediatric osteoporosis; diagnosis of exclusion (rule out OI, secondary causes); often presents around puberty with fractures; may resolve with puberty

IJO is a RARE primary osteoporosis of unknown cause in prepubertal children. Diagnosis of exclusion: rule out OI (no family history of fractures, no blue sclerae, no dentinogenesis imperfecta), secondary causes (endocrine, malabsorption, medications), other genetic conditions. Often presents around puberty with vertebral and metaphyseal fractures. Many patients improve with puberty. Management: calcium, vitamin D, bisphosphonates in severe cases.

front: BHOF and Medicare (BMMA) default repeat DXA interval:
back:
Every 2 years (23 months for Medicare) in most patients; MORE FREQUENT (1 year or sooner) for rapid-change scenarios (new glucocorticoid use, recent anabolic start, ADT/AI therapy, significant risk factors)

Default repeat DXA interval: every 2 years (Medicare 23 months). More frequent testing (1 year or sooner) is appropriate for rapid-change scenarios: new or high-dose glucocorticoid use, recent initiation of anabolic therapy (expect large BMD increase), ADT/AI therapy, severe malabsorption, bariatric surgery, surgical menopause, SCI, prolonged immobilization. Individualized based on clinical context.

front: The FDA black box warning for osteosarcoma on teriparatide:
back:
Was REMOVED November 16, 2020 after real-world post-marketing data did not show increased osteosarcoma risk in humans; the strict 2-year cap was also removed (use beyond 2 years may be considered if high fracture risk persists)

Teriparatide's FDA boxed warning for osteosarcoma was REMOVED November 16, 2020 after real-world post-marketing data did not show increased osteosarcoma risk in humans (original concern was from rat toxicology studies). Importantly, the same FDA action revised the 2-year cumulative lifetime limitation: per the current Forteo label, use beyond 2 years may be considered 'if a patient remains at or has returned to having a high risk for fracture'. Tymlos (abaloparatide) had its BBW removed December 22, 2021, but Tymlos retains the 2-year cumulative cap in the current label.

front: Before starting any DXA scan, verify the physician order:
back:
Confirm valid, current order for the specific patient; note requested sites (e.g., spine, bilateral hip); verify clinical indications support the order

Order verification: valid, current physician order for the specific patient (2 identifiers); requested sites match what you'll scan (bilateral hip, L1-L4 spine, forearm if ordered); clinical indications support the order. Clarify with the ordering provider if anything is unclear (wrong patient, missing sites, no indication, expired order). Scanning without a valid order is a compliance violation.

front: Workup for suspected primary hyperparathyroidism (PHPT) includes:
back:
Serum calcium (ELEVATED), intact PTH (elevated or inappropriately normal for elevated Ca), 24h urine calcium (to exclude familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia), 25-OH vitamin D, creatinine, BMD with 33% radius

PHPT workup: serum calcium (elevated), intact PTH (elevated or inappropriately normal for the elevated calcium), 25-OH vitamin D, creatinine (renal function), 24-hour urine calcium (rules out FHH which has LOW urine calcium), BMD with 33% RADIUS (cortical-rich site most affected). Imaging (sestamibi scan, ultrasound, 4D CT) for preoperative parathyroid localization if surgery indicated. Surgical cure rate is very high when performed by experienced surgeons.

front: Match the drug class to the correct representative agents:
back:
ANTIRESORPTIVES: bisphosphonates (alendronate, risedronate, ibandronate, zoledronic acid), denosumab, SERMs (raloxifene), estrogen, calcitonin. ANABOLICS: teriparatide, abaloparatide, romosozumab (dual)

ANTIRESORPTIVES (reduce bone resorption): bisphosphonates, denosumab (anti-RANKL), SERMs (raloxifene), estrogen/HRT, calcitonin. ANABOLICS (stimulate bone formation): teriparatide (PTH 1-34), abaloparatide (PTHrP analog), romosozumab (anti-sclerostin, DUAL effect with reduced resorption too). Knowing the class drives treatment decisions: typically anabolic first for very high risk, antiresorptive for most others.

front: The BHOF FRAX MOF treatment threshold is:
back:
20% (for patients with T-score between -1.0 and -2.5)

BHOF 2022 FRAX MOF treatment threshold: 20% 10-year Major Osteoporotic Fracture probability. Paired threshold: hip fracture 10-year probability > 3%. Either threshold triggers treatment consideration in patients with low bone mass (T between -1.0 and -2.5). Patients with T <= -2.5 or hip/vertebral fragility fracture qualify for treatment regardless of FRAX.

front: For height loss DURING DXA follow-up observation (not peak height loss), the threshold suggesting new vertebral fracture is:
back:
> 2 cm height loss during observation (warrants VFA or lateral spine imaging)

Two height thresholds to remember: (1) HISTORICAL height loss > 4 cm (1.5 in) from peak height is a VFA indication (combined with T < -1.0). (2) Height loss > 2 cm DURING OBSERVATION suggests new or unrecognized vertebral compression fracture; VFA or lateral spine imaging indicated. Measure with stadiometer at each visit to catch this.

front: Before positioning a patient for follow-up DXA, pull up the baseline image to:
back:
Reproduce baseline positioning, vertebral labeling, and ROI placement; verify at scan time; use copy/compare after acquisition

Compare baseline image to current positioning BEFORE scanning. Match: cushion angle, patient centering, distance from iliac crest to scan field edge, scan mode. After acquisition, use copy/compare to import baseline ROI placement and vertebral labeling. This reduces precision error significantly compared to scanning without reference. One of the most impactful workflow steps a technologist can control.

front: A T-score of 0 means:
back:
The patient's BMD is equal to the MEAN of the young adult reference population (peak bone mass); higher (positive) T-scores indicate higher-than-peak BMD, lower (negative) T-scores indicate below-peak BMD

T-score = 0: patient's BMD equals the MEAN of the young adult reference (20-29 year old women for hip, NHANES III). T = +1: 1 SD above mean. T = -1: 1 SD below mean. WHO categories (age 50+ men, postmenopausal women): T >= -1.0 normal; -1.0 to -2.5 low bone mass; <= -2.5 osteoporosis. Z-scores compare to age/sex-matched peers, not peak mass.

front: Endogenous Cushing syndrome affects bone by:
back:
Excess cortisol: directly inhibits osteoblast function and bone formation; increases osteoclast activity; causes secondary hypogonadism; net rapid bone loss similar to exogenous glucocorticoid therapy

Endogenous Cushing syndrome (excess cortisol from adrenal, pituitary, or ectopic source) causes rapid bone loss via: direct inhibition of osteoblast differentiation/function, reduced bone formation, increased osteoclast activity, secondary hypogonadism (low sex steroids), altered calcium metabolism. Similar mechanism to exogenous glucocorticoid therapy but often more severe. Diagnosis: 24-hour urinary free cortisol, dexamethasone suppression test, ACTH level. Treatment of the underlying cause usually improves BMD.

front: Bilateral hip DXA is indicated:
back:
Per ISCD 2023, bilateral hip is acceptable for baseline diagnosis (use LOWEST T-score) and for monitoring (use MEAN BILATERAL TOTAL HIP); some facilities scan bilateral routinely, others only unilateral

Bilateral hip DXA is acceptable but not required. Per ISCD 2023: (1) For diagnostic classification, use LOWEST T-score of right or left femoral neck or total hip. (2) For MONITORING, use MEAN BILATERAL TOTAL HIP (better precision). Some facilities routinely do bilateral baseline for these advantages; others do unilateral and switch sides only when needed. Reproduce the same approach at follow-up.

front: A 74-year-old postmenopausal woman presents with new back pain, 5 cm height loss from peak, T-score -2.0 at femoral neck, T -2.3 at total hip, and she has been on 10 mg prednisone daily for 6 months for RA. Appropriate next steps include:
back:
VFA is INDICATED (T < -1.0 AND age >= 70 AND height loss > 4 cm AND glucocorticoid >= 5mg/3mo - meets multiple VFA criteria); if VCF found on VFA, she has clinical osteoporosis by BHOF regardless of T-score; start bone-protective therapy (bisphosphonate first-line for GIO, or teriparatide if high fracture risk)

This case integrates many concepts: Age 74 (WHO criteria applicable), postmenopausal. T-score -2.3 at total hip = low bone mass (between -1.0 and -2.5). Multiple VFA indications met: T < -1.0 plus age >= 70 plus height loss > 4 cm plus glucocorticoid >= 5 mg/day for >= 3 months (any ONE qualifies; she has all four). VFA should detect any silent VCFs; if present, she has clinical osteoporosis by BHOF regardless of T-score. She meets ACR criteria for GIO treatment (prednisone >= 5 mg >= 3 months with elevated fracture risk). Start bisphosphonate first-line for GIO; consider teriparatide if very high risk or failure. Full workup: calcium, phosphorus, 25-OH vitamin D, creatinine, TSH, consider SPEP if unexplained.
source: Integration of ISCD 2023, BHOF 2022, ACR 2022 GIO

front: Which demographic carries the highest risk of developing osteoporosis?
back:
Caucasian postmenopausal female

Caucasian and Asian postmenopausal women carry the highest osteoporosis risk, with Caucasian women showing the highest U.S. prevalence rates. Risk factors compound: female sex (estrogen loss after menopause), Caucasian or Asian ancestry (lower peak bone mass on average), and age above 50. Per BHOF 2022, screening DXA is recommended for all women aged 65 and older, and for postmenopausal women under 65 with additional risk factors.
source: BHOF 2022 Clinician's Guide; ISCD 2023 Adult Official Positions

front: What is the primary function of osteoblasts in bone remodeling?
back:
Synthesize and lay down new bone matrix

Osteoblasts are mononuclear cells derived from mesenchymal stem cells. They synthesize osteoid (unmineralized collagen matrix) and direct its mineralization with calcium and phosphate. After completing matrix deposition, osteoblasts either undergo apoptosis or become embedded in bone as osteocytes. Anabolic agents like teriparatide and abaloparatide stimulate osteoblast activity to build new bone.
source: ASRT Module 1 Bone Physiology; standard physiology references

front: Which of the following statements about osteoporosis risk is FALSE?
back:
Obesity increases the risk of developing osteoporosis

Higher body weight and obesity have historically been considered protective against osteoporosis: greater mechanical loading stimulates bone formation, and adipose tissue provides peripheral estrogen production. However, the relationship is more nuanced than once thought. Obesity does NOT increase osteoporosis risk in the conventional sense, but obese patients still suffer fragility fractures and may have elevated fracture risk despite normal or high BMD due to fall mechanics, sarcopenia, and bone quality issues.

front: Why does cigarette smoking contribute to decreased bone density in premenopausal women?
back:
Smoking reduces estrogen activity, removing its bone-protective effect

Cigarette smoking lowers circulating estrogen and accelerates its hepatic metabolism, reducing the protective effect of estrogen on bone. Smoking also impairs calcium absorption, reduces vitamin D activation, and is associated with earlier menopause. The result is reduced peak bone mass, accelerated bone loss, and approximately doubled fracture risk in heavy long-term smokers. Smoking cessation is part of every BHOF-recommended osteoporosis management plan.

front: Which of the following is NOT an established risk factor for osteoporosis?
back:
Obesity

Obesity is generally not considered a risk factor for osteoporosis and may be partially protective due to mechanical loading and peripheral estrogen production. The other three are major established risk factors per BHOF 2022 and FRAX: glucocorticoid use (oral prednisone 5 mg or more daily for 3+ months), parental history of hip fracture, and low body weight (low BMI is one of the FRAX inputs). Note: obese patients can still fracture and may have elevated fracture risk despite preserved BMD.
source: BHOF 2022 Clinician's Guide; FRAX risk factors

front: Per the 1997 Bone Mass Measurement Act, which of the following is NOT a covered indication for Medicare-reimbursed DXA?
back:
Person with hypothyroidism

Hypothyroidism is NOT a covered Medicare indication. The five BMMA-covered indications are: estrogen-deficient women at clinical risk; vertebral abnormality on imaging; long-term glucocorticoid therapy (prednisone 7.5 mg daily or equivalent for 3+ months); primary hyperparathyroidism; and monitoring response to FDA-approved osteoporosis drug therapy. HYPERthyroidism (excess thyroid hormone) accelerates bone loss and is a clinical risk factor, but isolated hypothyroidism is not a BMMA indication.
source: Bone Mass Measurement Act 1997; CMS coverage policy

front: Medicare covers DXA every 24 months under the Bone Mass Measurement Act. The exception that permits more frequent monitoring is:
back:
Patients on long-term glucocorticoid therapy

Patients on long-term glucocorticoid therapy (prednisone 5-7.5 mg daily or equivalent for 3+ months) may receive Medicare-covered DXA more often than every 24 months when medically necessary. Glucocorticoids cause rapid bone loss especially in the first year of treatment, so closer monitoring is justified. The default coverage rule is once every 24 months for stable patients.
source: Bone Mass Measurement Act 1997; CMS LCD policies

front: A patient has a visible vertebral fracture on lateral spine DXA and a total hip T-score of -2.6. The appropriate WHO classification is:
back:
Severe (established) osteoporosis

Severe (or established) osteoporosis per WHO 1994 criteria requires a T-score of -2.5 or lower PLUS one or more fragility fractures. This patient meets both criteria: T-score -2.6 (osteoporotic range) and a vertebral fracture (a fragility fracture). The presence of a fragility fracture markedly elevates future fracture risk and typically triggers immediate pharmacologic therapy. Note: a T-score of -2.6 alone without fracture would be classified as osteoporosis, not severe.

front: In vivo precision (quality control) testing is performed on:
back:
A live patient

In vivo precision testing measures scan-to-scan reproducibility on actual patients with repositioning between scans. ISCD requires each technologist to perform an in vivo precision study using either 30 patients scanned twice OR 15 patients scanned three times, then calculate Root Mean Square Standard Deviation (RMS-SD) and Coefficient of Variation (CV). Acceptable precision per ISCD: PA spine 1.9% (LSC 5.3%), total hip 1.8% (LSC 5.0%), femoral neck 2.5% (LSC 6.9%).
source: ISCD 2023 Adult Official Positions; ASRT Module 7

front: A new precision study and Least Significant Change (LSC) calculation is required when:
back:
Both 1 and 2

Per ISCD 2023, precision must be re-established and a new LSC calculated whenever there is a substantive change to the operator-scanner combination. Specifically: each technologist must perform their own precision study (operator-specific), and a new study is required after equipment replacement, scanner upgrade with software/hardware changes that could affect measurement, or cross-calibration after a manufacturer change. The LSC is the smallest change in BMD that exceeds measurement noise at 95% confidence (LSC = 2.77 x precision error).

front: When using DXA to evaluate the effect of long-term glucocorticoid (corticosteroid) therapy, the preferred measurement site is the:
back:
Lumbar spine

Glucocorticoids preferentially deplete trabecular bone, which is most concentrated in the lumbar spine. Spine BMD changes are typically detectable earlier and at greater magnitude than hip changes when monitoring glucocorticoid effects. Glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis is the most common cause of secondary osteoporosis and is one of the BMMA-covered indications for Medicare DXA reimbursement, with monitoring permitted more often than every 24 months.

front: On a PA lumbar spine DXA, the area below L3 shows uniformly increased density with no visible disc spaces. The most likely cause is:
back:
Spinal fusion (laminectomy with hardware or bone graft)

Spinal fusion (post-laminectomy with hardware, autograft, or allograft) produces a confluent dense region without visible disc spaces because the vertebrae have been surgically joined. ISCD 2023 requires that fused vertebrae be EXCLUDED from BMD analysis. Other reasons to exclude vertebrae per ISCD: vertebral fracture, more than 1 SD difference from adjacent vertebrae, focal structural abnormality. A valid spine analysis requires at least two evaluable vertebrae from L1-L4.
source: ISCD 2023 Adult Official Positions; ASRT Module 10

front: When using DXA to evaluate a patient with primary hyperparathyroidism, the preferred measurement site is the:
back:
Distal one-third (33%) radius

Primary hyperparathyroidism preferentially affects cortical bone, which is best measured at the distal one-third (33%) radius. ISCD 2023 specifies the 33% radius site for any condition where cortical bone loss is the primary concern: hyperparathyroidism, when hip and spine cannot be measured, or when patient body habitus exceeds the scanner table limit. Spine and hip are predominantly trabecular and cortical mixed and may underestimate cortical-specific disease.
source: ISCD 2023 Adult Official Positions; ASRT Module 12

front: Primary hyperparathyroidism affects bone density predominantly by:
back:
Decreasing cortical bone density more than trabecular bone

Excess parathyroid hormone (PTH) preferentially mobilizes calcium from cortical bone, leading to greater cortical loss than trabecular loss. This is why ISCD specifies the 33% (one-third) radius (predominantly cortical) as the preferred measurement site in hyperparathyroidism. Note that intermittent low-dose PTH (teriparatide therapy) has the opposite effect, stimulating osteoblast bone formation. The continuous high-PTH state of hyperparathyroidism is catabolic; pulsatile low-dose PTH is anabolic.

front: Per ISCD 2023, Vertebral Fracture Assessment (VFA) is indicated in postmenopausal women and men over 50 with which of the following findings?
back:
All of the above

Per ISCD 2023, VFA is indicated when knowledge of vertebral fracture would change management, including: documented height loss greater than 4 cm (1.5 inches), historical height loss greater than 6 cm (2 inches), self-reported prior fracture not documented, glucocorticoid therapy 5 mg or more daily for 3+ months, and any T-score -1.0 or below combined with one risk factor (age 70+, low BMD, height loss). Roughly two-thirds of vertebral fractures are clinically silent, so VFA finds fractures that would otherwise be missed.
source: ISCD 2023 Adult Official Positions; VFA guidelines

front: Which type of phantom is MOST commonly used for daily DXA quality control?
back:
Manufacturer-supplied spine phantom

Each DXA manufacturer supplies a proprietary spine phantom designed for that scanner platform: Hologic uses an aluminum spine phantom, GE Lunar uses an aluminum block phantom, and Norland uses a 77-step calibration block. Daily QC uses the manufacturer-supplied phantom. The European Spine Phantom (ESP) is used for cross-calibration between machines from different manufacturers, not for daily QC.
source: Manufacturer QC guidelines; ISCD 2023 Official Positions

front: A facility replaces its existing DXA scanner with a new scanner from the same manufacturer. To allow continued longitudinal monitoring of existing patients, the technologist should:
back:
Perform an in vivo cross-calibration study comparing 30 patients on both scanners

Per ISCD 2023, when changing to a different-manufacturer or different-technology DXA scanner, cross-calibration uses ~30 patients scanned in vivo on both systems within 60 days. Same-make/model hardware changes use phantom-only protocols (10 phantom scans before and after). A new precision study and LSC must be calculated for the new scanner. Without cross-calibration, longitudinal BMD comparisons are invalid. Note: this topic was REMOVED from the ARRT 2027 spec (effective Jan 1, 2027); it remains testable on the 2022 spec (in force through Dec 31, 2026).
source: ISCD 2023 Adult Official Positions; cross-calibration guidelines

front: When comparing BMD values from a Hologic scanner to a GE Lunar scanner on the same patient, the technologist should expect:
back:
GE Lunar BMD values to be systematically higher than Hologic, with a larger difference at the lumbar spine than at the hip

GE Lunar BMD values run systematically higher than Hologic , typically about 11-14% at the lumbar spine and a smaller margin (1-10%) at hip sites , because of different X-ray sources, edge detection algorithms, and reference databases. T-scores cannot be directly compared across manufacturers. BMD values from different manufacturers should never be directly compared without standardization (sBMD) or in vivo cross-calibration data.

front: Before performing the first patient scan of the day, the technologist should:
back:
Allow the X-ray tube to warm up and run the daily phantom QC

The X-ray tube and detector require a warm-up period (typically 15-30 minutes per manufacturer specifications) for stable output. After warm-up, the daily phantom QC must be performed and the result must fall within control limits before patient scanning begins. If the phantom QC fails, patient scanning is suspended until the issue is resolved. Some scanners auto-perform tube warm-up on startup.

front: Per ISCD 2023, an acceptable in vivo precision study requires:
back:
30 patients scanned twice OR 15 patients scanned three times, with repositioning between scans

ISCD 2023 specifies that an in vivo precision study requires either 30 patients scanned twice or 15 patients scanned three times, with full repositioning between every scan (patient gets up, scanner resets, patient repositioned). This produces 30 degrees of freedom for the statistical calculation. RMS-SD and CV are calculated, and the LSC = 2.77 x precision error at 95% confidence. Each technologist must perform their own precision study.
source: ISCD 2023 Adult Official Positions; Bonnick precision methodology

front: Per ISCD 2023 minimum acceptable precision for adult DXA, the maximum acceptable PA spine precision error (RMS-SD as %CV) is:
back:
1.9%

ISCD 2023 minimum acceptable precision values: PA spine 1.9% (LSC 5.3%), total hip 1.8% (LSC 5.0%), femoral neck 2.5% (LSC 6.9%). If a technologist's precision exceeds these limits, the cause must be investigated (positioning technique, ROI placement, equipment) and the precision study repeated after corrective action. Note that 5.3% is the LSC for spine (precision 1.9% x 2.77), not the precision itself.

front: An ISCD-compliant in vivo precision study must include which measurement site(s)?
back:
Each anatomical site that the technologist measures clinically

Per ISCD 2023, the precision study must be performed at every site that the technologist clinically reports for monitoring. If the technologist reports spine, total hip, and femoral neck for follow-up scans, all three sites must have established precision values and corresponding LSCs. The study uses the same population (representative of the clinic's patient mix) scanned at all relevant sites with repositioning between scans.
source: ISCD 2023 Adult Official Positions; precision study guidelines

front: When performing a follow-up DXA on a patient with a prior baseline, the most important consideration for valid serial comparison is:
back:
Matching the prior scan's positioning, ROI placement, and analysis parameters

Valid longitudinal BMD comparison requires reproducing the prior scan's geometry as closely as possible: same patient positioning, same ROI placement, same analysis parameters, and ideally the same scanner. The compare/copy ROI feature in DXA software places the ROI from the baseline onto the follow-up. Even small differences in ROI placement can introduce variability that exceeds the LSC, producing a false impression of bone change. Time of day does not affect BMD measurement.

front: Which practice BEST supports the ALARA principle during DXA imaging?
back:
Avoiding unnecessary repeat scans through correct positioning the first time

ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable) in DXA is best supported by minimizing repeat scans through proper initial patient positioning, room setup, and pre-scan checks (artifact removal, correct demographics entered, prior scans reviewed for ROI matching). Higher scan modes deliver more dose; repeat scans double the dose. While DXA dose is very low overall, ALARA still applies, especially for younger patients and those needing serial monitoring.
source: NCRP Report 168; ACR/AAPM DXA Technical Standard

front: Which of the following items in the patient's pre-scan history would MOST likely create an artifact on lumbar spine DXA?
back:
Barium oral contrast study within the past 7 days

Recent radiocontrast in the GI tract (especially overlying the spine) creates dense artifacts that falsely lower BMD because the algorithm subtracts the apparent soft tissue, removing real bone signal. ISCD recommends delaying DXA at least 7-10 days after iodinated IV contrast and at least 10-14 days after barium studies. Nuclear medicine studies typically require 3-7 days depending on isotope half-life (longer for I-131). Recent bowel movement actually helps by clearing intestinal contents. Mild scoliosis is identifiable but does not preclude analysis.
source: ISCD 2023 Adult Official Positions; manufacturer artifact guidelines

front: Which piece of information is MOST critical to obtain from the patient before a baseline DXA?
back:
Date of last menstrual period (premenopausal women) or date of menopause

Menopausal status is critical for BMD interpretation: postmenopausal women receive a T-score-based diagnosis per WHO criteria, while premenopausal women under 50 should be evaluated using Z-scores per ISCD. Date of last menstrual period helps confirm menopausal status when uncertain. Other critical pre-scan history: prior fractures, current medications (especially glucocorticoids and osteoporosis drugs), height and weight, prior DXA scans, recent contrast or nuclear medicine studies.

front: A patient weighs 350 lb (159 kg) and the DXA scanner table has a maximum weight limit of 300 lb (136 kg). The technologist should:
back:
Scan only the forearm using the 33% radius site

When patient weight exceeds the scanner's table limit, the forearm DXA can still be performed because the patient sits at the side of the table rather than lying on it, so table weight limits do not apply. The 33% (one-third distal) radius is the ISCD-preferred forearm site. Some newer scanners have higher weight limits (up to 450 lb / 204 kg) and wider tables. Exceeding the manufacturer weight limit risks scanner damage and inaccurate measurement.
source: ISCD 2023 Adult Official Positions; manufacturer specifications

front: Per ISCD 2023, vertebrae should be excluded from BMD analysis when:
back:
The vertebra differs from adjacent vertebrae by more than 1.0 T-score unit

Per ISCD 2023, a vertebra should be excluded from BMD analysis when it shows: visible fracture or compression deformity, focal structural abnormality (post-surgical hardware, marked degenerative change), or T-score that differs from adjacent vertebrae by more than 1.0 T-score unit (suggesting localized abnormality skewing the result). At least 2 evaluable vertebrae from L1-L4 must remain for valid spine analysis. Mild osteophytes that do not distort the vertebral body do not require exclusion.

front: During hip DXA positioning, the femur is internally rotated approximately 15-25 degrees. The purpose is to:
back:
Place the femoral neck parallel to the scan table for accurate measurement

Internal rotation of the femur 15-25 degrees brings the femoral neck axis parallel to the scan table. In neutral position, the femoral neck is angled (anteverted) relative to the table, which would foreshorten its appearance and cause overlap with the greater trochanter. Proper rotation aligns the lesser trochanter so it is barely visible (small) on the resulting scan; a prominent lesser trochanter indicates insufficient rotation. A foot positioner is used to maintain rotation throughout the scan.

front: On a properly positioned hip DXA scan, the lesser trochanter should appear:
back:
Small or barely visible

When the femur is correctly internally rotated 15-25 degrees, the lesser trochanter appears small or barely visible on the PA hip image because rotation brings the lesser trochanter posterior to (behind) the femoral shaft from the X-ray beam's perspective. A prominent lesser trochanter indicates insufficient internal rotation; a completely hidden lesser trochanter may indicate over-rotation. Proper rotation ensures the femoral neck is parallel to the table.

front: Per ISCD-recommended ROI placement, the femoral neck box should be:
back:
Placed at the narrowest point of the femoral neck, perpendicular to the neck axis

The femoral neck ROI box is placed perpendicular to the femoral neck long axis, at the narrowest point of the neck (the mid-neck). Manufacturer software auto-places the ROI but the technologist should verify and adjust if needed. Inconsistent ROI placement is a leading source of precision error. Wards triangle is a calculated subregion of the neck and is NOT recommended for diagnosis or monitoring per ISCD 2023.

front: For an initial baseline hip DXA, which side should be measured?
back:
Either hip; ISCD does not specify a side preference for baseline

Per ISCD 2023, either hip can be used at baseline. Many facilities default to the left hip for consistency. The same hip should be used for follow-up scans for valid serial monitoring. Bilateral hip acquisition is endorsed by ISCD 2023: for DIAGNOSIS, use the LOWEST of left/right femoral neck or total hip T-score; for MONITORING, use the MEAN bilateral total hip BMD. Total hip and femoral neck BMD are the diagnostic sites; trochanter and Ward's are not used.

front: A 72-year-old woman has bilateral total hip replacements. Which sites are appropriate for DXA-based osteoporosis diagnosis?
back:
Lumbar spine and 33% (one-third) radius

Hip replacement makes the hip non-evaluable for BMD. When both hips have hardware, the diagnosis defaults to other valid sites: PA lumbar spine (if at least 2 evaluable vertebrae remain) and the 33% (one-third) radius of the non-dominant forearm. Per ISCD, the lowest T-score from any of these valid sites is used for diagnosis. Whole body BMD is NOT used for adult osteoporosis diagnosis per WHO/ISCD criteria.

front: A postmenopausal woman has the following T-scores: PA spine -2.1, total hip -2.6, femoral neck -2.4, 33% radius -1.8. Per WHO/ISCD criteria, her diagnosis is:
back:
Osteoporosis

The diagnosis is based on the LOWEST T-score from among valid measurement sites. Total hip T-score of -2.6 falls in the osteoporosis range (-2.5 or below) per WHO criteria, so the patient is classified as osteoporosis. Severe osteoporosis would require both T-score -2.5 or below AND a fragility fracture. The other sites are osteopenic (-1.0 to -2.5) but do not change the diagnosis because the lowest qualifying value drives classification.

front: Per ISCD 2023, the appropriate score to report for a 35-year-old premenopausal woman is:
back:
Z-score only

Per ISCD 2023, in premenopausal women, men under 50, and children, Z-scores (comparison to age-matched and sex-matched peers) are the appropriate metric, NOT T-scores. The WHO osteoporosis diagnosis based on T-score does not apply to these populations. A Z-score of -2.0 or below is termed below the expected range for age. The diagnosis of osteoporosis in premenopausal women requires a Z-score below -2.0 plus secondary causes or fragility fracture.

front: Per WHO 1994 criteria, the T-score range defining osteopenia (low bone mass) is:
back:
-1.0 to -2.5 (between -1.0 and -2.5)

WHO 1994 T-score categories: Normal = T-score -1.0 or higher; Osteopenia (low bone mass) = T-score between -1.0 and -2.5 (i.e., -1.1 to -2.4); Osteoporosis = T-score -2.5 or lower; Severe osteoporosis = T-score -2.5 or lower PLUS fragility fracture. The term low bone mass is preferred over osteopenia in some current usage because osteopenia implies a disease process. The classification applies to postmenopausal women and men 50 and older.

front: FRAX calculates the 10-year probability of:
back:
Major osteoporotic fracture (clinical spine, hip, forearm, humerus) and hip fracture

FRAX (Fracture Risk Assessment Tool, WHO collaborative) calculates two 10-year probabilities: major osteoporotic fracture (combined risk of clinical spine, hip, forearm, or humerus fracture) and hip fracture alone. FRAX inputs include age, sex, weight, height, prior fracture, parental hip fracture, current smoking, glucocorticoid use, rheumatoid arthritis, secondary osteoporosis causes, alcohol intake (3+ units/day), and femoral neck BMD. FRAX is validated for ages 40-90 and untreated patients.
source: FRAX (Kanis et al, WHO Collaborating Centre)

front: When BMD is incorporated into FRAX, the only validated site to enter is:
back:
Femoral neck

FRAX is validated only with femoral neck BMD. Other sites (spine, total hip, forearm) are NOT validated as FRAX inputs and should not be substituted. If hip BMD is unavailable, FRAX can be calculated without BMD using clinical risk factors alone. The femoral neck T-score from the diagnostic side or the lower of bilateral hips can be used. This is one of the most testable FRAX details.

front: Per BHOF 2022, pharmacologic treatment is recommended for postmenopausal women with osteopenia (T-score -1.0 to -2.5) when the FRAX 10-year hip fracture probability exceeds:
back:
3%

Per BHOF 2022 (formerly NOF), treatment is recommended for postmenopausal women and men 50+ with osteopenia who have FRAX 10-year hip fracture probability of 3% or greater, OR major osteoporotic fracture probability of 20% or greater. Patients with T-score -2.5 or below or with prior hip/vertebral fracture qualify for treatment regardless of FRAX. The 3%/20% thresholds are U.S.-specific; other countries use different cutoffs based on local cost-effectiveness analyses.

front: Denosumab (Prolia) treats osteoporosis by:
back:
Inhibiting RANK ligand, blocking osteoclast formation and activity

Denosumab (Prolia) is a fully human monoclonal antibody to RANK ligand (RANKL). RANKL normally binds to RANK receptors on osteoclast precursors, driving their differentiation and activity. By neutralizing RANKL, denosumab blocks osteoclast formation and bone resorption. Dosing: 60 mg subcutaneous every 6 months with concurrent calcium and vitamin D. As of January 19, 2024, the FDA added a BOXED WARNING for severe hypocalcemia in patients with advanced chronic kidney disease (especially dialysis patients), and Prolia is now under a REMS program. The label also carries a prominent Warnings and Precautions statement: discontinuation can trigger rebound multiple vertebral fractures within approximately 7 months; transition to a bisphosphonate (commonly zoledronic acid) is recommended at end of therapy.
source: BHOF 2022; FDA prescribing information for Prolia

front: Teriparatide (Forteo) and abaloparatide (Tymlos) are categorized as:
back:
Anabolic (bone-forming) agents

Teriparatide (recombinant PTH 1-34) and abaloparatide (PTHrP analog) are anabolic agents that stimulate osteoblast bone formation when given as intermittent daily subcutaneous injections. They differ from bisphosphonates and denosumab (antiresorptives) which slow bone breakdown. Use is limited to 2 years of cumulative lifetime treatment. After completing anabolic therapy, patients should transition to an antiresorptive to preserve gains. Romosozumab (Evenity) is a third anabolic option.

front: Romosozumab (Evenity) is a unique anabolic agent because it:
back:
Both stimulates bone formation AND inhibits bone resorption (dual action)

Romosozumab is a humanized monoclonal antibody against sclerostin, a protein that inhibits bone formation. By blocking sclerostin, romosozumab simultaneously stimulates osteoblast bone formation and reduces osteoclast bone resorption (uncouples remodeling). Dosing: 210 mg subcutaneous monthly (given as two 105 mg injections at one visit) for 12 months total. After the 12-month course, patients must transition to an antiresorptive to maintain gains. BOXED WARNING per FDA: potential risk of myocardial infarction, stroke, and cardiovascular death. Do NOT initiate romosozumab in patients who have had MI or stroke within the preceding year; discontinue if either occurs during therapy.
source: BHOF 2022; FDA prescribing information for Evenity

front: After 5 years of oral alendronate therapy in a low-fracture-risk patient, the BHOF recommendation is to consider:
back:
A drug holiday (treatment pause) with continued monitoring

Per BHOF 2022 and the ASBMR Task Force (Adler 2016), a drug holiday from oral bisphosphonates after 5 years (or 3 years for IV zoledronic acid) is reasonable for low-risk patients. 'Low-risk' is defined as femoral neck or total hip T-score above -2.5 after treatment, no new fractures during therapy, and no prior hip or vertebral fracture. High-risk patients (T-score -2.5 or below, age over 70, prior major osteoporotic fracture, or new fracture on therapy) should continue therapy up to 10 years oral or 6 years IV. Bisphosphonates persist in bone after stopping, providing residual effect for 1-3 years. Drug holidays do NOT apply to denosumab (rebound bone loss), romosozumab, teriparatide, or abaloparatide; all of these require a sequential antiresorptive.

front: Per BHOF 2022, the typical interval for follow-up DXA in a treated osteoporosis patient is:
back:
Every 1-2 years

Per BHOF 2022, follow-up DXA every 1-2 years is typical for treated osteoporosis patients to assess treatment response. Medicare covers DXA every 24 months under the BMMA (more frequently for glucocorticoid patients). Some protocols use every 3-5 years for stable patients on therapy. Repeating before LSC has had time to be exceeded is wasteful: if LSC is 5% and BMD typically changes 1-2% per year on therapy, scanning more often than 18-24 months risks meaningless fluctuations.

front: Per ISCD 2023, the minimum number of evaluable vertebrae required for a valid PA spine BMD analysis is:
back:
2

Per ISCD 2023, at least 2 evaluable vertebrae from L1-L4 must be included for valid spine BMD analysis, and ISCD 2023 explicitly states a single vertebral body should NOT be used for diagnosis or monitoring. The technologist must exclude vertebrae per ISCD criteria (fracture, hardware, focal abnormality, T-score deviation greater than 1.0 from neighbors). When fewer than 2 vertebrae are evaluable, base the diagnosis on a different valid skeletal site (hip or 33% radius).

front: When labeling vertebrae on a PA spine DXA, the most reliable initial landmark for identifying the L4 region is:
back:
The level of the iliac crests (top of the iliac crests typically corresponds to the L4 body or L4-L5 region)

The top of the iliac crests (Jacoby's line / supracristal plane) typically corresponds to the L4 body or the L4-L5 disc space. The exact L4-L5 disc level is hit in only about 30% of patients; the broader L4 body or L4-L5 region accounts for approximately 70-86% combined (Walsh 2006; Chakraverty 2007). From there, count upward to identify L1-L3. Variant anatomy (transitional vertebrae, lumbarized S1, sacralized L5, 6 lumbar vertebrae) requires careful comparison with prior imaging. The technologist should always verify spinal level using multiple anatomical landmarks rather than rely on a fixed percentage.

front: A patient's baseline lumbar spine BMD was 0.850 g/cm2. The follow-up scan shows 0.870 g/cm2. The percent change is:
back:
+2.4%

Percent change = ((follow-up - baseline) / baseline) x 100 = ((0.870 - 0.850) / 0.850) x 100 = (0.020 / 0.850) x 100 = 2.35%, rounded to 2.4%. To determine if this represents a real change, compare to the facility's LSC. If the spine LSC is 5.3% (per ISCD acceptable precision), this 2.4% change is below LSC and would be reported as no statistically significant change.

front: The facility's total hip LSC is 5.0%. Average annual BMD loss in untreated postmenopausal women is approximately 1% per year. The minimum interval between baseline and follow-up DXA at which a real change can typically be detected is approximately:
back:
5 years

Time to detect change = LSC / annual rate of change. With LSC of 5.0% and 1% annual loss, it takes approximately 5 years for the cumulative change to exceed LSC. This is why repeat DXA more frequently than every 1-2 years is rarely useful in untreated patients. Treated patients on antiresorptives may show 2-4% spine BMD gain per year, allowing change detection within 1-2 years. Glucocorticoid users lose bone fastest in the first 6-12 months of therapy (often 5-15% in the first year with high-dose therapy), justifying more frequent monitoring.

front: Per ISCD 2023, the required precision study output and follow-up DXA report must include:
back:
RMS-SD in g/cm2 and LSC at 95% confidence; the follow-up report must state the facility LSC

Per ISCD 2023, the precision study calculates RMS-SD (in g/cm2) and the corresponding LSC at 95% confidence (LSC = 2.77 x RMS-SD). The follow-up DXA report MUST state the facility LSC and indicate whether any change reaches statistical significance. RMS percent CV is a supplemental output from the ISCD advanced precision calculator and is useful for comparing precision across sites with different absolute BMD, but it is not a required report element.

front: After an X-ray tube is replaced on a DXA scanner, what action must the technologist take BEFORE resuming patient scanning?
back:
Establish a new phantom baseline mean and SD, then verify performance

X-ray tube replacement is a significant hardware change that affects beam characteristics. After replacement and manufacturer service, a new phantom baseline must be established (typically 10-25 phantom scans). The new mean and SD become the basis for ongoing QC limits. Phantom values before and after replacement should be compared to verify continuity; if a systematic shift is present, in vivo cross-calibration may be needed. A new precision study and LSC are also required for follow-up reporting accuracy.

front: A 38-year-old premenopausal woman has a lumbar spine Z-score of -2.4. Per ISCD 2023, this should be reported as:
back:
Below the expected range for age

Per ISCD 2023, Z-scores -2.0 or below in premenopausal women, men under 50, or children should be reported as 'below the expected range for age,' NOT as osteopenia or osteoporosis. The WHO T-score-based osteoporosis diagnosis applies only to postmenopausal women and men 50 and older. A premenopausal woman with Z below -2.0 plus a fragility fracture or secondary cause may receive an osteoporosis diagnosis based on clinical context, but the DXA report itself should use the 'below expected range' terminology.

front: Per ISCD 2023, the BMD report MUST include all of the following EXCEPT:
back:
The patient's predicted lifetime fracture probability

Per ISCD 2023, required report elements include: demographic information, indication for the test, measurement site(s), BMD in g/cm2 (3 decimal places), T-score for postmenopausal women and men 50+, Z-score for premenopausal women and men under 50, reference database used, technologist and interpreter, diagnostic classification (per WHO if applicable), and recommendation for follow-up. Lifetime fracture probability is not standardly reported; FRAX 10-year probability MAY be included when calculated. ISCD recommends NOT reporting BMD comparison percentage of young adult or age-matched mean (these are derived from T/Z scores).

front: Per ISCD 2023, Vertebral Fracture Assessment (VFA) is indicated based on historical height loss greater than:
back:
4 cm (1.5 inches)

Per ISCD 2023, historical height loss greater than 4 cm (1.5 inches) is one of the criteria triggering consideration of VFA. The historical-height-loss threshold of >4 cm has been in force since the 2007 PDC; the 2013 simplified ISCD indications eliminated a separate PROSPECTIVE measured height-loss criterion (>2 cm), not a 6 cm threshold. VFA is also indicated for: glucocorticoid therapy 5 mg or more daily for 3+ months, self-reported but undocumented prior vertebral fracture, T-score -1.0 or below combined with one of: age 70+, low BMD, or height loss. About two-thirds of vertebral fractures are clinically silent.

front: When VFA cannot adequately visualize a suspected vertebral fracture, the recommended next step is:
back:
Order a conventional lateral spine radiograph for clarification

Per ISCD 2023, when VFA is inconclusive or vertebrae cannot be adequately visualized (often the upper thoracic vertebrae T1-T4 are difficult to image), conventional lateral spine radiographs should be obtained for diagnostic clarification. VFA is a screening tool with moderate sensitivity for fracture detection; conventional radiographs remain the diagnostic standard. CT and MRI are reserved for specific clinical questions (acute trauma, suspected metastasis, infection) and are not first-line for VFA follow-up.

front: A rare but well-documented adverse effect associated with long-term bisphosphonate therapy is:
back:
Atypical femoral fracture (subtrochanteric or femoral shaft)

Atypical femoral fracture (AFF) is a rare adverse effect of long-term (typically 5+ years) bisphosphonate therapy. AFFs occur in the subtrochanteric or femoral diaphyseal region with characteristic features: transverse or short oblique fracture line, lateral cortical thickening, minimal trauma, often bilateral. Patients may experience prodromal thigh or groin pain. Risk increases with treatment duration. This finding contributes to the rationale for drug holidays after 5 years of oral bisphosphonate therapy in low-risk patients. Osteonecrosis of the jaw (ONJ) is another rare adverse effect.
source: BHOF 2022; Shane et al. JBMR 2014 ASBMR Task Force on AFF

front: After completing approximately 24 months of teriparatide for osteoporosis, the recommended next step is to:
back:
Transition to an antiresorptive (bisphosphonate or denosumab) to maintain BMD gains

Teriparatide and abaloparatide are typically used for up to 24 months. Per the post-November 2020 FDA label change, the hard 24-month lifetime cap and osteosarcoma boxed warning were removed; continued use beyond 24 months may be considered if the patient remains at high fracture risk. After anabolic therapy, BMD gains are rapidly lost without sequential antiresorptive treatment. Standard practice: transition to a bisphosphonate (oral or IV) or denosumab to consolidate gains. The same applies after romosozumab (still limited to 12 monthly doses): always follow with an antiresorptive. Sequential anabolic-then-antiresorptive therapy produces greater BMD gains than antiresorptive alone in high-risk patients.

front: A 65-year-old woman has PA spine T-score of -1.2 and total hip T-score of -2.7. Her appropriate diagnosis is:
back:
Osteoporosis (use the lower T-score)

Per ISCD 2023, the diagnosis is based on the LOWEST T-score from valid measurement sites (PA spine, total hip, femoral neck, and 33% radius in special cases). The total hip T-score of -2.7 falls in osteoporosis range and drives the diagnosis. Spine-hip discordance is common: major discordance (e.g., normal at one site, osteoporosis at the other) occurs in about 3-5% of patients; minor discordance (1+ unit T-score difference, but same or adjacent diagnostic category) occurs in approximately 40% (Faulkner 1999, El Maghraoui 2009). T-score is never averaged. Large discordance does not require rescanning if positioning and analysis were correct.
source: ISCD 2023; Leslie et al. on T-score discordance

front: Severe aortic calcification overlying the lumbar spine on PA DXA will most likely:
back:
Falsely elevate spine BMD

Aortic calcification overlying the lumbar spine on PA projection adds dense material that the algorithm interprets as bone, falsely elevating apparent BMD. This is most prominent at L3 and L4 in older patients with vascular disease. The lateral projection avoids this artifact since the aorta is anterior to the spine. Other artifacts that falsely elevate spine BMD: degenerative osteophytes, vertebral fractures (compressed vertebra has higher density), surgical hardware, and scoliosis. ISCD recommends excluding affected vertebrae per the standard criteria (T-score deviation > 1.0 from neighbors).

front: FRAX is validated for use in patients aged:
back:
40 to 90 years

FRAX is validated for ages 40 to 90 years and for patients NOT currently on osteoporosis pharmacotherapy. Below age 40, fracture risk is too low to model accurately; above 90 the population data are sparse. For patients under 40 entering FRAX, the tool defaults to age 40 calculations. FRAX is also designed for treatment-naive patients; patients on therapy may have falsely reassuring FRAX scores due to the BMD improvement from treatment. FRAX inputs: age, sex, weight, height, prior fracture, parental hip fracture, smoking, glucocorticoids, RA, secondary causes, alcohol, femoral neck BMD.
source: FRAX (Kanis et al, WHO Collaborating Centre)

front: All of the following are FRAX risk factor inputs EXCEPT:
back:
Number of falls in the past year

FRAX inputs include: age, sex, weight, height, prior major osteoporotic fracture, parental hip fracture, current smoking, glucocorticoid use (5 mg or more prednisolone-equivalent per day for more than 3 months), rheumatoid arthritis, secondary osteoporosis cause, alcohol intake (3 or more units/day), and optionally femoral neck BMD. Falls history is NOT a FRAX input despite being a major fracture risk factor. This is one of the recognized FRAX limitations: the tool underestimates risk in fall-prone patients. Some clinicians manually adjust FRAX upward for high fall risk.
source: FRAX (Kanis et al); FRAX inputs documentation

front: The total hip ROI on a hip DXA includes:
back:
Femoral neck, trochanter, and intertrochanteric region combined

The total hip ROI combines the femoral neck, trochanter, and intertrochanteric (inter-troch) regions, excluding Wards triangle. Total hip BMD is the average density across these three subregions and is the most precise hip measurement. Total hip and femoral neck are the diagnostic sites per ISCD; trochanter alone is not used. Wards triangle is calculated but not recommended for diagnosis. The intertrochanteric region carries the highest absolute BMD due to thicker cortical bone and weight-bearing load.

front: A patient has been on denosumab (Prolia) for 3 years and asks about taking a 'drug holiday' similar to bisphosphonates. The correct counsel is:
back:
No, denosumab does NOT have a drug holiday option; stopping causes rapid rebound bone loss and increased vertebral fracture risk

Denosumab does NOT have a drug holiday option. Unlike bisphosphonates which persist in bone for years after stopping, denosumab effects rapidly reverse when dosing stops. Bone turnover markers and BMD return to baseline within 12-18 months, and patients experience rebound multiple vertebral fractures (clustering 4-18 months after a missed dose, median ~7-9 months). Per BHOF 2022 and the FDA label, patients stopping denosumab should transition to a bisphosphonate (most commonly zoledronic acid) within 6 months of the last denosumab dose. Note also: as of January 19, 2024, the FDA added a Boxed Warning to Prolia for severe hypocalcemia in patients with advanced CKD (especially those on dialysis with CKD-MBD), and Prolia is now under a REMS program. This is one of the most clinically important pharmacology concepts.
source: BHOF 2022; FDA Prolia label; ASBMR position

front: Lead shielding for the operator during DXA scanning is:
back:
Generally not necessary because scatter at typical operator distances is negligible

Lead shielding for the DXA operator is generally not necessary because the operator stands at the control console (typically 6+ feet / 2 meters from the scan table) where scatter radiation is negligible. The very low primary beam dose, combined with the inverse-square law fall-off of scatter, results in operator doses far below regulatory limits even at high patient volumes. Per IAEA RPOP guidance: minimum operator distance is 1 meter for pencil-beam and 2 meters for fan-beam systems. Lead aprons offer minimal additional benefit at this distance and add ergonomic burden. Pregnant operators should still maintain distance and may use shielding as institutional policy dictates, but DXA dose poses no special pregnancy concern at proper operating distance.
source: ACR/AAPM DXA Technical Standard; NCRP Report 147 (medical X-ray shielding); IAEA RPOP DXA staff guidance

front: Per ISCD 2019 Pediatric Official Positions, in children and adolescents (under age 19), the term 'osteoporosis' should NOT be applied based on DXA findings alone. Diagnosis requires:
back:
Z-score below -2.0 PLUS clinically significant fracture history

Per ISCD Pediatric Official Positions, osteoporosis in children and adolescents requires BOTH a low BMD/BMC for age (Z-score -2.0 or below using race/sex/age-matched references) AND a clinically significant fracture history. A clinically significant fracture history is defined as: two or more long bone fractures by age 10 years, OR three or more long bone fractures at any age up to 19 years. ALTERNATIVELY, one or more vertebral compression (crush) fractures without high-energy trauma or local disease is diagnostic of osteoporosis independent of BMD. The terms 'low bone mineral content for chronologic age' or 'low bone mineral density for chronologic age' are used when low Z-score exists without fracture. Pediatric DXA also requires height-for-age adjustment because children with short stature have falsely low BMD/BMC.

front: Per ISCD 2019 Pediatric Official Positions, the preferred DXA sites for ambulatory children and adolescents are:
back:
PA lumbar spine and total body less head (TBLH)

Per ISCD 2019 Pediatric Official Positions, PA lumbar spine and total body less head (TBLH) are preferred for most ambulatory children and adolescents. Hip BMD CAN be used when adequate pediatric reference data are available, particularly for children with reduced lower-extremity weight-bearing or transitioning into adult monitoring; it is not preferred due to higher precision error in skeletally immature patients. The 33% (one-third) radius is a valid alternate when preferred sites are not feasible. Lateral distal femur (LDF) is used for non-ambulatory children. TBLH is preferred over whole body because head BMD is high and disproportionately influences total body BMD in children. Z-scores must use pediatric-specific reference databases matched for age, sex, and race/ethnicity.

front: A 14-year-old with chronic disease has short stature (height-for-age Z-score -2.5) and a lumbar spine BMD Z-score of -2.4. The Z-score should be:
back:
Adjusted upward for height-for-age, recognizing that short children have lower BMD/BMC for chronological age

Per ISCD 2019 Pediatric Official Positions, BMD Z-scores in children should be adjusted (or interpreted in context) for height-for-age, pubertal stage, and bone age when growth is delayed or significant. Chronologically aged Z-scores will be falsely low in children with short stature, growth delay, or pubertal delay. Methods include using a height-for-age adjusted Z-score, calculating BMC from bone area (avoids the artifact of small bones being labeled low BMD), or comparing to bone-age-matched reference values. Without adjustment, these patients risk being falsely diagnosed with low bone mass.

front: Per ISCD 2019 Pediatric Official Positions, which term is PROHIBITED in pediatric DXA reports?
back:
T-score

Per ISCD 2019 Pediatric Official Positions, T-scores must NOT be used in children and adolescents (under 19 years) because the WHO T-score-based osteoporosis diagnosis applies only to postmenopausal women and men 50+. Using a T-score in children would compare their BMD to peak bone mass of a young adult, which is not yet achieved in growing patients. Z-scores (matched for age, sex, race) are the correct metric for pediatrics. Diagnostic terminology in pediatrics: 'low bone mineral density for age' (Z below -2.0) or 'osteoporosis' (Z below -2.0 PLUS significant fracture history).

front: After a software upgrade on the DXA scanner that affects edge detection algorithms, the technologist should:
back:
Re-establish phantom QC baseline; consider whether a new precision study is needed if the upgrade affects measurement

Software upgrades that affect measurement (edge detection, ROI placement algorithms, BMD calculation) can introduce systematic shifts. After such an upgrade, re-establish phantom QC baseline (typical practice: phantom scans for 10-25 days post-upgrade) and verify that values remain within acceptable limits. If a measurement-affecting upgrade is significant enough to change reported values, a new precision study and LSC may be needed. Cosmetic or workflow software upgrades that do not affect measurement do not require QC changes.

front: Per ISCD 2023, in men aged 50 and older, osteoporosis is diagnosed using:
back:
T-score of -2.5 or below using a male reference database

Per ISCD 2023, men age 50 and older are diagnosed using T-scores (not Z-scores), with the same -2.5 threshold as in women. The 2023 Adult Official Positions changed the men's reference database: T-scores in men of all ethnic groups should be calculated using a uniform White (non-race/ethnicity adjusted) FEMALE normative reference. Specifically, NHANES III White female ages 20-29 is used for the femoral neck and total hip; the manufacturer's database is used for the lumbar spine. The diagnostic threshold for osteoporosis in men remains T-score -2.5 or below. Men under 50 use Z-scores like premenopausal women.

front: During hip DXA, a foot positioning device (typically a foam or molded brace) is used to:
back:
Maintain the femur in 15-25 degrees of internal rotation throughout the scan

The foot positioning device (foot brace or angled foot block) holds the patient's foot in a fixed position so the femur remains internally rotated 15-25 degrees throughout the scan. Without the device, patients tend to externally rotate during the scan, which would foreshorten the femoral neck and produce inaccurate measurement. The patient places their foot against the device with the hip slightly abducted to align the femoral shaft parallel to the scan axis. Some manufacturers integrate the device into the scan table.

front: A patient has 6 lumbar vertebrae instead of 5. When labeling vertebrae for PA spine DXA, the technologist should:
back:
Compare to prior imaging and use the same labeling scheme; if no prior, count down from T12 (last rib-bearing vertebra)

Transitional vertebrae (lumbarized S1, sacralized L5, 6 lumbar bodies) require careful labeling. Best practice: identify T12 as the last rib-bearing vertebra and count downward, or compare to prior imaging if available so the labeling is consistent across exams. Inconsistent labeling between baseline and follow-up scans is a major source of false BMD change. Document the variant anatomy in the scan notes. ISCD permits using L1-L4 (skipping the 6th if present) as long as the same vertebrae are used across serial scans.

topic: uniform White female reference for men
front: Per ISCD 2023, T-scores in men age 50 and older should be calculated using:
back:
A uniform White (non-race/ethnicity adjusted) female reference (NHANES III White female ages 20-29 for hip; manufacturer database for spine)

Per ISCD 2023, T-scores in men of all ethnic groups should be calculated using a uniform White (non-race/ethnicity adjusted) FEMALE normative reference. Specifically: NHANES III White female ages 20-29 is used for the femoral neck and total hip; the manufacturer's database is used for the lumbar spine. The diagnostic threshold for osteoporosis in men remains T-score -2.5 or below. This was a change from prior practice (separate male reference). Men under 50 use Z-scores like premenopausal women.

front: Per the current (post-November 2020) FDA label for teriparatide, lifetime cumulative use:
back:
Is no longer hard-capped; continued use beyond 24 months may be considered if the patient remains at high fracture risk

In November 2020, the FDA removed the 24-month lifetime cumulative use restriction and the osteosarcoma Boxed Warning from the teriparatide (Forteo) label. Per the current label, treatment beyond 2 years 'should only be considered if a patient remains at, or has returned to having, a high risk for fracture.' The 24-month duration remains the typical treatment course in clinical practice. Abaloparatide (Tymlos) similarly has no hard lifetime cap. Romosozumab is still limited to 12 monthly doses. All anabolics should be followed by an antiresorptive to maintain BMD gains.
source: FDA Forteo prescribing information (post-2020 update); BHOF 2022

front: As of January 19, 2024, the FDA added a Boxed Warning to denosumab (Prolia) for:
back:
Severe hypocalcemia in patients with advanced chronic kidney disease, especially those on dialysis with CKD-MBD

On January 19, 2024, the FDA added a Boxed Warning to Prolia (denosumab) for severe hypocalcemia in patients with advanced chronic kidney disease, particularly those on dialysis with CKD-MBD (mineral and bone disorder). Prolia is now also under a REMS program. The rebound multiple vertebral fracture risk after discontinuation remains in Warnings and Precautions (a prominent label warning) but is NOT the boxed warning. ONJ and AFF are class warnings shared with bisphosphonates.
source: FDA Drug Safety Communication January 19, 2024; Prolia label

front: Per IAEA RPOP guidance, the minimum recommended operator distance from a DXA scanner during acquisition is:
back:
1 meter for pencil-beam systems and 2 meters for fan-beam systems

Per IAEA Radiation Protection of Patients (RPOP) guidance for DXA staff, the minimum recommended operator distance during scanning is 1 meter for pencil-beam systems and 2 meters for fan-beam systems. Fan-beam scanners produce higher scatter at table level, so greater operator distance is needed. At these distances, scatter is negligible and lead aprons are not required. The inverse square law makes distance the most effective protection: doubling distance reduces dose four-fold.
source: IAEA RPOP DXA staff guidance; ACR/AAPM DXA Technical Standard

front: T-score discordance between PA spine and total hip is:
back:
Common: major discordance (e.g., normal at one site and osteoporosis at the other) approximately 3-5%; minor discordance approximately 40%

Spine-hip T-score discordance is common in DXA practice. Per Faulkner 1999, El Maghraoui 2009, and Mounach 2009: major discordance (e.g., normal at one site, osteoporosis at the other) occurs in approximately 3-5% of patients; minor discordance (1+ unit T-score difference but same or adjacent diagnostic category) occurs in approximately 40%. Per ISCD 2023, the diagnosis is based on the LOWEST T-score from valid sites; T-scores are NEVER averaged. Discordance reflects different bone compartments (trabecular at spine, mixed at hip) responding differently to age, disease, and treatment.

front: Per ISCD 2023, the follow-up DXA report for a serial scan MUST include:
back:
A statement of the facility LSC and whether any change reaches statistical significance

Per ISCD 2023, follow-up DXA reports MUST state the facility's Least Significant Change (LSC) at 95% confidence and indicate whether any observed change reaches statistical significance (i.e., exceeds LSC). Without this context, BMD changes cannot be properly interpreted. LSC is calculated from the facility's own precision study (RMS-SD x 2.77). Reporting BMD change without LSC reference is incomplete per ISCD requirements.

front: The Hologic NHANES protocol specifies femoral internal rotation of:
back:
25 degrees

The Hologic NHANES protocol specifies 25 degrees of femoral internal rotation, which positions the femoral neck parallel to the scan plane and exposes its full length for accurate measurement. The 15-25 degree range cited in general DXA practice covers both Hologic and GE Lunar protocols (Lunar typically uses 15-20 degrees). Internal rotation prevents foreshortening of the femoral neck and reduces lesser trochanter visibility, both indicators of correct positioning.
source: Hologic operator manual; NHANES III Bone Densitometry Manual

front: The FRAX glucocorticoid risk factor input is met when a patient is:
back:
Currently or previously exposed to oral glucocorticoids 5 mg or more prednisolone-equivalent per day for more than 3 months

Per FRAX risk factor definitions, the glucocorticoid input is met when a patient is currently or has previously been exposed to ORAL glucocorticoids 5 mg or more prednisolone-equivalent per day for MORE than 3 months. Inhaled and topical glucocorticoids do not count. The criterion includes both current and past use; once exposed, the risk factor remains. Note: this differs from the CMS BMMA glucocorticoid coverage criterion (5 mg/day prednisone for more than 3 months), which has similar but not identical wording and is for Medicare coverage rather than fracture risk modeling.
source: FRAX risk factor documentation (Sheffield); WHO Collaborating Centre

front: Lateral distal femur (LDF) DXA is the recommended site for:
back:
Non-ambulatory children, especially those with cerebral palsy or muscular dystrophy

Per ISCD 2019 Pediatric Official Positions, lateral distal femur (LDF) DXA is recommended for non-ambulatory children, especially those with cerebral palsy, muscular dystrophy, or other conditions that prevent positioning at the standard PA spine and TBLH sites. LDF correlates well with fragility fracture risk in non-ambulatory children. Reference data are available for the Hologic platform. Hardware and contractures often make hip and spine measurements unreliable in this population.

front: Ibandronate (Boniva) has documented fracture risk reduction at the:
back:
Spine only (not hip)

Ibandronate (Boniva) has demonstrated vertebral fracture risk reduction in clinical trials but has NOT shown statistically significant non-vertebral or hip fracture risk reduction. This contrasts with alendronate, risedronate, and zoledronic acid, all of which reduce vertebral, non-vertebral, and hip fractures. For patients at high hip fracture risk, ibandronate is generally not first-line. Dosing: 150 mg PO monthly or 3 mg IV every 3 months.

front: Abaloparatide (Tymlos) differs from teriparatide (Forteo) in that abaloparatide is:
back:
A synthetic analog of PTH-related protein (PTHrP 1-34) rather than PTH 1-34

Abaloparatide (Tymlos) is a synthetic analog of PTH-related protein (PTHrP) amino acids 1-34, while teriparatide (Forteo) is a synthetic analog of native PTH amino acids 1-34. Both are anabolic, both administered SC daily (abaloparatide 80 mcg; teriparatide 20 mcg), both indicated for postmenopausal women at high fracture risk. Important regulatory difference: TERIPARATIDE had both the 24-month lifetime cap AND the osteosarcoma boxed warning removed by FDA on November 16, 2020. ABALOPARATIDE had only the osteosarcoma boxed warning removed (December 22, 2021); the 2-year lifetime use limitation remains in the current Tymlos label, and cumulative use of abaloparatide plus other PTH analogs (including teriparatide) beyond 2 years lifetime is not recommended.

front: Per BHOF 2022, the recommended interval for follow-up DXA in a patient newly started on oral bisphosphonate therapy is:
back:
1 to 2 years after starting therapy, then less frequent intervals once stable

BHOF 2022 recommends follow-up DXA approximately 1-2 years after initiating or changing pharmacotherapy, with subsequent intervals based on clinical circumstances. Per ISCD and clinical practice, less frequent intervals (every 2-3 years) suffice in stable patients on long-term therapy, while expected rapid bone loss (high-dose glucocorticoids, hyperparathyroidism, transplant recipients) warrants every 6-12 months. Repeat DXA more often than every year is rarely useful because BMD change typically does not exceed LSC.

front: Per BHOF 2022, pharmacologic treatment is recommended in postmenopausal women and men 50+ with osteopenia (T-score between -1.0 and -2.5) when FRAX 10-year probability is:
back:
3% or higher for hip fracture, OR 20% or higher for major osteoporotic fracture

Per BHOF 2022 (LeBoff et al.), pharmacologic treatment is recommended in postmenopausal women and men 50+ with: (1) T-score -2.5 or below at the femoral neck, total hip, lumbar spine, or 33% radius; (2) prior hip or vertebral fracture regardless of BMD; (3) low bone mass (T-score -1.0 to -2.5 at the femoral neck or total hip) with US-adapted FRAX 10-year hip fracture probability 3% or higher OR major osteoporotic fracture probability 20% or higher; (4) fracture of proximal humerus, pelvis, or distal forearm in a person with low bone mass (individualized). Note FRAX-based threshold applies to femoral neck or total hip T-scores specifically, not to spine osteopenia alone.

topic: in vivo vs in vitro precision
front: ISCD-required precision study to calculate facility LSC must be performed:
back:
On 30 patients with 2 scans each, with repositioning between scans (in vivo, with degrees of freedom equal to 30)

Per ISCD 2023, the precision study to calculate facility LSC must be performed in vivo (on patients) because patient-related factors (positioning, soft tissue, motion) contribute to real-world precision and are not captured by phantom-only QC. Acceptable designs: 30 patients with 2 scans each (yielding 30 degrees of freedom), OR 15 patients with 3 scans each. Patients are repositioned between scans (gets up off the table). Phantom QC monitors scanner stability over time but does NOT replace the in vivo precision study. A new precision study is needed when a new DXA system is installed, a technologist's skill level changes, or after major equipment service per facility QC policy.

front: Severe scoliosis with significant vertebral rotation on PA lumbar spine DXA most likely:
back:
Falsely elevates BMD due to vertebral rotation and superimposed structures

Severe scoliosis falsely elevates PA spine BMD due to vertebral rotation and superimposed structures (Pappou 2006; phantom and cadaveric studies).
