ISCD 2023 does NOT specify a numeric Cobb-angle cutoff but instructs that vertebrae 'clearly abnormal and non-assessable' or differing by more than 1.0 T-score from neighbors be excluded; if fewer than 2 evaluable vertebrae remain, use a different valid site (hip or 33% radius). Phantom data suggest BMD is minimally affected at axial rotation 15 degrees or less; severe rotation creates unreliable measurements. Document scoliosis presence and severity.

front: A patient has a unilateral total hip arthroplasty. The DXA technologist should:
back:
Measure the contralateral (non-operated) hip plus PA lumbar spine

When one hip has a prosthesis, the contralateral (non-operated) hip can be measured normally if anatomically intact, plus PA lumbar spine. The operated hip CANNOT be scanned for diagnostic BMD because the metal prosthesis prevents valid measurement (peri-prosthetic Gruen-zone DXA is a separate analysis). For patients with bilateral hip replacement, ISCD recommends substituting the 33% (one-third) radius of the non-dominant forearm. Document any orthopedic hardware in the scan field.

front: Per ISCD 2023, the 33% (one-third) radius DXA is indicated when:
back:
Hip and/or spine cannot be measured or interpreted, OR primary hyperparathyroidism is present, OR the patient exceeds the DXA table weight limit

Per ISCD 2023, the 33% (one-third) radius DXA is indicated for: hip and/or spine cannot be measured or interpreted (severe arthritis, scoliosis, hardware, bilateral hip replacement); primary hyperparathyroidism (cortical bone is preferentially affected and the 33% radius is cortical-rich); patients exceeding the DXA table weight limit; very obese patients in whom hip and spine measurements are unreliable. The 33% (also called 1/3 radius) is preferred over the ultradistal or mid-distal forearm. The non-dominant forearm should be measured.

front: Per WHO 1994 criteria, 'severe osteoporosis' (also called 'established osteoporosis') is defined as:
back:
T-score -2.5 or below PLUS one or more fragility fractures

Per WHO 1994 diagnostic criteria, the categories are: Normal = T-score -1.0 or above; Osteopenia (low bone mass) = T-score between -1.0 and -2.5; Osteoporosis = T-score -2.5 or below; Severe (or established) osteoporosis = T-score -2.5 or below PLUS one or more fragility (low-trauma) fractures. The 'severe' designation has historically guided more aggressive treatment, though current practice often uses fracture probability rather than the WHO severe category for treatment decisions.

front: Per ISCD 2023, VFA is indicated in patients on long-term glucocorticoid therapy when the dose is:
back:
5 mg or more prednisone-equivalent per day for 3 months or more

Per ISCD 2023, VFA is indicated in patients on glucocorticoid therapy of 5 mg or more prednisone-equivalent per day for 3 months or more. This same threshold is used for the FRAX glucocorticoid risk factor and approximates the CMS BMMA glucocorticoid coverage criterion. Glucocorticoids cause rapid bone loss (3-15% in the first year, depending on dose) and increase vertebral fracture risk independent of BMD. VFA detects the often-asymptomatic vertebral fractures that confirm osteoporosis diagnosis and influence treatment decisions.

front: The bone remodeling cycle (Basic Multicellular Unit, BMU) consists of phases in this order:
back:
Activation, resorption, reversal, formation, mineralization

The bone remodeling cycle proceeds: (1) Activation (osteoclast precursor recruitment to the remodeling site, ~3 days), (2) Resorption (osteoclast removal of bone matrix, ~14 days), (3) Reversal (transition phase, ~7 days), (4) Formation (osteoblast deposition of new osteoid, ~50-80 days), (5) Mineralization (osteoid mineralization, ~100+ days). Total cycle ~3-6 months. Antiresorptives (bisphosphonates, denosumab) inhibit the resorption phase. Anabolics (teriparatide, abaloparatide) stimulate formation. Romosozumab is dual: stimulates formation AND inhibits resorption.

front: Osteoprotegerin (OPG), produced by osteoblasts, regulates osteoclast activity by:
back:
Acting as a decoy receptor that binds RANKL, preventing RANKL from activating RANK on osteoclast precursors

OPG is a decoy receptor produced by osteoblasts that binds RANKL (Receptor Activator of NF-kB Ligand), preventing RANKL from binding to RANK receptors on osteoclast precursors. The RANKL/RANK/OPG axis controls osteoclast formation and activity. When estrogen decreases (menopause), OPG production drops while RANKL increases, shifting balance toward bone resorption. Denosumab (Prolia) is a monoclonal antibody that mimics OPG action by binding and neutralizing RANKL. Understanding this pathway explains why denosumab effects rapidly reverse on stopping (no RANKL inhibition continuing).

front: Primary hyperparathyroidism causes which characteristic pattern of BMD changes?
back:
Most pronounced loss at cortical-rich sites (especially 33% radius), with relative sparing of trabecular spine

Primary hyperparathyroidism (chronic PTH excess) preferentially resorbs cortical bone; the distal 1/3 radius shows the most pronounced BMD loss, while the lumbar spine is relatively preserved (trabecular sparing reflects retained PTH anabolism on cancellous bone despite chronic elevation - distinct from the strong anabolic effect of intermittent PTH dosing such as teriparatide). This is why the 33% radius is a required site for evaluation of suspected hyperparathyroidism. After successful parathyroidectomy, BMD recovers LARGEST AND EARLIEST at the lumbar spine and femoral neck; the distal 1/3 radius recovers slowly and often incompletely (Silverberg 1995; Bilezikian 2022 Fifth International Workshop).

front: Critical pre-DXA history elements include all EXCEPT:
back:
Patient's hair color

Critical pre-DXA history: current osteoporosis medications (and duration), prior fragility fractures (and which sites), recent contrast or nuclear medicine studies (artifact risk), recent height loss, parental hip fracture history, smoking, alcohol, glucocorticoid use, secondary osteoporosis causes (RA, hyperparathyroidism, GI malabsorption), prior DXA scans (for serial comparison), pregnancy status, and any orthopedic hardware. This information determines risk factor inputs for FRAX, validity of measurements, and choice of measurement sites. Patient hair color is not relevant.

front: If 3 of 4 lumbar vertebrae must be excluded due to artifacts, the technologist should:
back:
Decline to use the spine for diagnosis (ISCD 2023 says do NOT use a single vertebra for diagnosis or monitoring); base diagnosis on hip and 33% radius if needed

Per ISCD 2023, if only one valid vertebra remains after exclusions, do NOT use a single vertebra for diagnosis or monitoring. The minimum requirement is 2 evaluable vertebrae from L1-L4. When fewer than 2 are valid, base the diagnosis on a different valid skeletal site (hip or 33% radius). Vertebrae are excluded for: visible fracture, focal abnormality (lytic/blastic lesion), surgical hardware, or T-score deviation greater than 1.0 from adjacent vertebrae. Document which vertebrae were excluded and why.

front: Per ISCD 2023, Ward's triangle BMD:
back:
Is calculated by the software but should NOT be used for diagnosis or monitoring

Ward's triangle is an anatomical region in the femoral neck where the principal compressive and tensile trabeculae intersect. DXA software calculates a small ROI in this region, but per ISCD 2023, Ward's triangle BMD should NOT be used for diagnosis or monitoring. It is highly variable, has poor precision, and produces falsely low T-scores. The diagnostic hip sites are total hip and femoral neck. The trochanter alone is also not used for diagnosis. Ward's may be reported by the software but should be ignored or disabled in clinical reports.

front: The standard QC phantom supplied with a Hologic DXA system is:
back:
An anthropomorphic (block-shaped) spine phantom

Hologic uses an anthropomorphic spine phantom containing four anatomically shaped cast-aluminum vertebrae embedded in a tissue-equivalent epoxy/plastic surround. Lunar/GE uses a rectangular aluminum-bar (block) phantom of varying thickness simulating T12-L5. Norland uses a different phantom design. Each manufacturer's phantom has its own established BMD value with allowed tolerance. The phantom should be scanned daily before patient scanning to verify scanner stability. Phantom values are tracked on a Shewhart chart against the established baseline mean.
source: Manufacturer documentation; ACR/AAPM DXA Technical Standard

front: A vertebra showing definite compression fracture on DXA spine analysis should be:
back:
Excluded from BMD analysis (compressed vertebra has falsely high BMD due to volume reduction)

Compression fractures cause vertebrae to appear DENSER on DXA because the same bone mineral content is compressed into a smaller projected area. Including a fractured vertebra falsely elevates spine BMD and masks osteoporosis. Per ISCD 2023, vertebrae with definite compression fracture, focal abnormality, or T-score deviation greater than 1.0 from neighbors should be excluded as a local structural change. A vertebral fragility fracture alone is sufficient for a clinical diagnosis of osteoporosis (NBHA 2014); 'severe (established) osteoporosis' per WHO 1994 additionally requires T-score -2.5 or below.

front: Bazedoxifene combined with conjugated estrogens (Duavee) is FDA-approved for postmenopausal women WITH A UTERUS for which two indications?
back:
Treatment of moderate-to-severe vasomotor symptoms associated with menopause, AND prevention of postmenopausal osteoporosis

Duavee (conjugated estrogens 0.45 mg + bazedoxifene 20 mg) is FDA-approved for postmenopausal women WITH A UTERUS for two distinct indications: (1) treatment of moderate-to-severe vasomotor symptoms associated with menopause, and (2) prevention of postmenopausal osteoporosis (with the limitation that non-estrogen options should be considered first). Bazedoxifene is a SERM that protects the uterus from estrogen-stimulated endometrial hyperplasia, eliminating the need for added progestin. Like other estrogen-containing therapies, it carries Boxed Warnings for cardiovascular events, stroke, VTE, and (with prolonged use) breast cancer. Not first-line for osteoporosis treatment in patients without vasomotor symptoms.

front: A premenopausal woman with Z-score of -2.4 at the spine is correctly characterized as:
back:
Below the expected range for age, requiring evaluation for secondary causes before any pharmacotherapy

Per ISCD 2023, in premenopausal women Z-scores are used (not T-scores). Z-score -2.0 or below is classified as 'below the expected range for age.' A diagnosis of osteoporosis in a premenopausal woman generally requires Z below -2.0 PLUS either a fragility fracture or a known secondary cause; otherwise the appropriate term is 'low BMD for chronological age.' Evaluation for secondary causes is mandatory before pharmacotherapy: serum calcium, phosphorus, magnesium, creatinine, albumin, LFTs, CBC, 25(OH)D, PTH, TSH, tissue-transglutaminase IgA (with total IgA), SPEP/serum free light chains, 24-hour urine calcium and creatinine, plus testosterone/gonadotropins (men) or estradiol/FSH, 24-hour urinary free cortisol, and others guided by clinical context. Treatment is typically directed at the underlying disease.

front: Per the current FDA Tymlos (abaloparatide) label, lifetime cumulative use of abaloparatide:
back:
Is not recommended beyond 2 years total lifetime, and cumulative use of abaloparatide plus other PTH analogs (including teriparatide) beyond 2 years lifetime is not recommended

Per the current FDA Tymlos label (NDA 208743 supplement s015, rev. 12/2023), use of abaloparatide for more than 2 years during a patient's lifetime is not recommended (Section 2.3). The osteosarcoma Boxed Warning was removed effective December 22, 2021. Note: the explicit cumulative-with-teriparatide restriction that appeared in the 2018 label was deleted when the BBW was removed; current Section 5.1 states that data on osteosarcoma risk beyond 2 years of TYMLOS and/or any PTH-analog use are limited. This contrasts with teriparatide (Forteo), which had BOTH the 24-month cap and the osteosarcoma boxed warning removed November 16, 2020. Romosozumab is limited to 12 monthly doses.

front: Why does teriparatide (daily SC injection) produce anabolic bone effects while primary hyperparathyroidism causes net bone loss, even though both involve elevated PTH?
back:
Intermittent (daily SC injection) PTH exposure favors osteoblast activation; chronic continuous PTH elevation favors osteoclast activation and net resorption

The skeletal effect of PTH depends on exposure pattern. INTERMITTENT high-peak PTH (e.g., once-daily SC injection, mimicking the pulse pattern of teriparatide) preferentially activates osteoblasts and produces net bone formation - the basis of anabolic therapy. CHRONIC SUSTAINED PTH elevation (as in primary hyperparathyroidism) preferentially activates osteoclasts and causes net resorption, particularly at cortical sites like the 33% radius. This is one of the most elegant pharmacology concepts in bone medicine: same hormone, opposite skeletal outcomes based on exposure pattern.

front: The Hologic anthropomorphic spine phantom contains:
back:
Four simulated vertebrae cast from calcium hydroxyapatite, embedded in a tissue-equivalent epoxy resin block

The Hologic anthropomorphic spine phantom contains 4 simulated vertebrae (L1-L4) cast from calcium hydroxyapatite, embedded in a tissue-equivalent epoxy resin block. The GE Lunar Aluminum Spine Phantom (ASP) is a rectangular block-style phantom containing aluminum bars that simulate vertebral bone. Norland uses a different phantom design. Each manufacturer's phantom has its own established BMD value with allowed tolerance. The phantom should be scanned daily and tracked on a Shewhart chart against the established baseline mean.
source: Bonnick Bone Densitometry in Clinical Practice; Sutter et al. Bone 2021

front: After successful parathyroidectomy for primary hyperparathyroidism, BMD recovery is largest and earliest at:
back:
The lumbar spine and femoral neck

After successful parathyroidectomy, BMD recovery is largest and earliest at the lumbar spine and femoral neck (Silverberg 1995; Bilezikian 2022 Fifth International Workshop). The distal 1/3 radius, which experienced the most pronounced bone loss before surgery (cortical bone is preferentially affected by chronic PTH excess), recovers slowly and often incompletely. Pre-operatively, the radius shows the worst T-score; post-operatively, the spine shows the best recovery. This pattern reflects the differential response of trabecular vs cortical bone to PTH.

front: Per NBHA 2014 expanded criteria for clinical diagnosis of osteoporosis, which of the following fragility fractures is diagnostic of osteoporosis ALONE (without requiring BMD)?
back:
Hip fracture alone

Per NBHA 2014 (Siris, Adler, Bilezikian et al.), a low-trauma HIP fracture alone is diagnostic of osteoporosis with or without BMD. A low-trauma clinical vertebral, proximal humerus, or pelvis fracture is diagnostic of osteoporosis ONLY in a person with osteopenia (T-score between -1.0 and -2.5 at spine or hip). A distal forearm fracture with osteopenia may be diagnostic in selected cases. FRAX 3% or higher (hip) or 20% or higher (major osteoporotic fracture) 10-year probability also confers diagnosis. By contrast, WHO 1994 'severe (established) osteoporosis' (Technical Report Series 843) requires BOTH a T-score -2.5 or below AND one or more fragility fractures.
source: NBHA 2014 (Siris, Adler, Bilezikian et al. Osteoporos Int 2014;25:1439); WHO 1994

front: A 35-year-old premenopausal woman has Z-score -2.4 at the lumbar spine, no fragility fractures, and no identified secondary cause. The correct DXA report terminology is:
back:
Below the expected range for age (also termed 'low BMD for chronological age')

Per ISCD 2019, in premenopausal women Z-scores are used (not T-scores). Z-score -2.0 or below is reported as 'below the expected range for age.' ('Low BMD for chronological age' is older legacy terminology that has been superseded; current ISCD wording uses only 'below the expected range for age.') A diagnosis of osteoporosis in a premenopausal woman generally requires Z-score below -2.0 PLUS either a fragility fracture or a known secondary cause; otherwise the appropriate term is 'below the expected range for age.' This patient has neither, so the correct terminology applies. Evaluation for secondary causes is mandatory before pharmacotherapy.

front: Duavee (conjugated estrogens 0.45 mg + bazedoxifene 20 mg) is FDA-approved for postmenopausal women with a uterus for:
back:
Treatment of moderate-to-severe vasomotor symptoms associated with menopause, AND prevention of postmenopausal osteoporosis (two distinct indications)

Duavee has two distinct FDA indications, both restricted to postmenopausal women with a uterus: (1) treatment of moderate-to-severe vasomotor symptoms (hot flashes) associated with menopause, and (2) prevention of postmenopausal osteoporosis (with the limitation that non-estrogen options should be considered first). Bazedoxifene is a SERM that protects the uterus from estrogen-stimulated endometrial hyperplasia, eliminating the need for added progestin. Like other estrogen-containing therapies, it carries Boxed Warnings for cardiovascular events, stroke, VTE, and (with prolonged use) breast cancer.

front: When a patient exceeds the manufacturer's DXA table weight limit (typically 300-450 lb depending on system), the recommended alternative measurement site is:
back:
33% (one-third) radius of the non-dominant forearm

When a patient exceeds the DXA table weight limit, the 33% (one-third) radius of the non-dominant forearm is the recommended alternative site. The forearm scanner is a separate accessory or position that does not require placing the patient on the main scan table. Per ISCD 2023, the 33% radius is also indicated for hyperparathyroidism and other situations where hip and spine cannot be measured or interpreted. Weight limits per current manufacturer specifications: Hologic Horizon series 450 lb / 204 kg standard (current marketing literature cites up to 500 lb / 227 kg); GE Lunar iDXA 450 lb / 204 kg; GE Lunar Prodigy 350 lb / 159 kg. Confirm exact limit for your specific scanner before declining a scan based on weight.
source: Hologic Horizon Site Preparation Guide; GE Lunar specifications; ISCD 2023

front: FRAX can be calculated without femoral neck BMD input when:
back:
DXA is unavailable, contraindicated, or not yet performed; FRAX without BMD provides risk stratification for screening decisions

FRAX can be calculated without femoral neck BMD input when DXA is unavailable, contraindicated, or not yet performed. FRAX-without-BMD provides useful risk stratification, particularly for screening decisions about who needs DXA. When BMD becomes available, FRAX should be recalculated. The clinical risk factors alone (age, sex, weight, height, prior fracture, parental hip fracture, smoking, glucocorticoids, RA, secondary causes, alcohol) provide meaningful fracture probability estimates. Adding femoral neck BMD improves prediction. FRAX without BMD is a recognized validated approach (Kanis 2007).

front: If a patient has a previously placed femoral neck screw or fracture fixation hardware in the proximal femur, the correct DXA approach is:
back:
Cannot perform diagnostic hip BMD on that side; use the contralateral hip if anatomically intact, or substitute with PA spine and 33% radius

Hardware in the proximal femur (screws, plates, fixation devices) makes diagnostic hip BMD impossible on that side because metal absorbs X-rays heavily and falsely elevates apparent BMD or distorts ROI placement. The correct approach: use the contralateral non-operated hip if anatomically intact, plus PA lumbar spine; if both hips have hardware, substitute the 33% radius of the non-dominant forearm. Document hardware presence in scan notes. Even partial hardware (e.g., a small screw) within the ROI invalidates the measurement.

front: A patient has had lumbar spinal fusion at L4-L5. The correct PA spine DXA approach is:
back:
Exclude the fused vertebrae from analysis; report only the unfused vertebrae if at least 2 valid (typically L1-L3 if L4-L5 fused); if only one evaluable vertebra remains, base diagnosis on a different valid skeletal site (e.g., hip or 33% radius)

Spinal fusion hardware and the fused vertebrae themselves invalidate BMD measurement at those levels. Exclude affected vertebrae and report only the unfused vertebrae (typically L1-L3 in this case). Per ISCD 2023, a minimum of 2 evaluable vertebrae from L1-L4 is required; do NOT report a single vertebra. If only one evaluable vertebra remains, base diagnosis on a different valid skeletal site (e.g., hip or 33% radius). Document fusion levels in scan notes. The vertebrae below the fusion may have altered biomechanical loading and may show accelerated bone loss.

front: When establishing a new DXA scanner's phantom QC baseline, the technologist should:
back:
Use the manufacturer-supplied phantom dedicated to that specific scanner, with its serial number documented in QC records

Each DXA scanner should be paired with its own manufacturer-supplied phantom (Hologic anthropomorphic, Lunar aluminum bar, etc.). The phantom identity (including serial/lot number where applicable) should be documented in the QC log. If the phantom is damaged, lost, or replaced, a new baseline must be established because each individual phantom has slightly different absolute values. Per ISCD, when replacing a phantom with the same make/model, perform a 20-day cross-calibration with mean BMD tolerance of 0.5% or less. Sharing phantoms between scanners is not recommended unless specifically validated.
source: Manufacturer guidelines; ACR/AAPM DXA Technical Standard

front: On a hip DXA, the femoral shaft should appear:
back:
Straight and parallel to the long axis of the scan table

Proper hip positioning produces a femoral shaft that appears straight and parallel to the long axis of the scan table. Achieved by: patient supine, hip slightly abducted, foot internally rotated 15-25 degrees against a foot positioning device, knee extended. A curved or angled shaft indicates positioning error and produces unreliable BMD. Lower extremity rotation (uncorrected external rotation tendency) foreshortens the femoral neck and falsely lowers BMD; over-rotation also distorts measurement. Visual landmarks of correct positioning: small or barely visible lesser trochanter, straight femoral shaft, neck length appearing maximal.

front: Per ISCD 2023, the 33% (one-third) radius can be used as a diagnostic site for osteoporosis in which situations?
back:
When hip and/or spine cannot be measured or interpreted; in hyperparathyroidism; or in very obese patients exceeding the DXA table weight limit

Per ISCD 2023, the 33% (one-third) radius is a valid diagnostic site (T-score -2.5 or below = osteoporosis) in three situations: (1) when hip and/or spine cannot be measured or interpreted (severe spine artifacts, bilateral hip replacement, severe arthritis); (2) in hyperparathyroidism (cortical bone preferentially affected); (3) in very obese patients exceeding the DXA table weight limit. The non-dominant forearm should be measured. The 33% (also called 1/3 radius) is preferred over the ultradistal or mid-distal forearm because of higher cortical content and better precision.

front: Modern DXA scanners maintain calibration during patient scanning, but the methods differ by manufacturer. Which is correctly described?
back:
Hologic uses a rotating calibration wheel/drum; GE Lunar uses a static cerium K-edge filter plus internal reference standard; Norland uses a samarium filter plus internal reference standard

Modern DXA scanners maintain calibration during patient scanning, but the methods differ by manufacturer. Hologic systems use a rotating calibration wheel/drum containing bone-, soft-tissue-, and air-equivalent reference materials that pass continuously through the X-ray beam, enabling real-time pixel-by-pixel calibration. GE Lunar systems use a static cerium K-edge filter combined with an internal reference standard rather than a rotating wheel. Norland systems use a samarium filter with an internal calibration standard within the scan field. Daily phantom QC still verifies long-term scanner stability and detects drift, regardless of manufacturer.
source: Manufacturer documentation; AAPM 2025; Bonnick Bone Densitometry

front: Some recent literature suggests L1 may be the most informative single vertebra for DXA because:
back:
It is least affected by degenerative changes (osteophytes, facet arthritis) that typically progress at lower lumbar levels

L1 is least affected by degenerative changes (osteophytes, facet arthritis, disc degeneration, aortic calcification overlying lower lumbar levels) that progressively contaminate L2, L3, and L4 in older patients. Lower lumbar BMD is therefore often falsely elevated in age-related degeneration. Some recent DXA literature suggests L1 alone may predict fracture risk as well as or better than L1-L4 average in older patients. ISCD 2023 still requires at least 2 evaluable vertebrae for diagnosis (single vertebra not used), but L1 is increasingly recognized as the most informative individual level. TBS analysis per ISCD 2023 uses L1-L4 with no exclusions.

front: Per ISCD 2023, the BEST sites for MONITORING serial BMD changes during therapy are:
back:
PA lumbar spine and total hip (mean BMD if bilateral hip acquired)

Per ISCD 2023, PA lumbar spine and total hip are the best sites for monitoring because they have the largest absolute BMD changes during therapy and the smallest precision error. Femoral neck precision is poorer than total hip. For bilateral hip acquisition, use the MEAN total hip for monitoring (vs the LOWEST for diagnosis). The 33% radius shows minimal change with antiresorptive therapy and is a poor monitoring site. Wards triangle has high precision error and is not used for monitoring or diagnosis. Always use the same site, ROI, and ideally same scanner for serial comparison.

front: A 32-year-old woman of childbearing potential presents for DXA. She is uncertain about pregnancy status. The technologist should:
back:
Confirm pregnancy status (verbal screening at minimum, urine HCG if uncertain per facility policy) before scanning; if pregnant, postpone non-urgent DXA until after delivery

Although DXA dose is very low, ALARA principles require confirming pregnancy status in women of childbearing potential before any X-ray procedure. Verbal screening at minimum; some facilities require urine HCG testing if uncertainty exists or per policy. If pregnant, postpone non-urgent DXA until after delivery (and ideally after lactation, as lactation transiently affects BMD). Document pregnancy status in scan notes. If DXA is medically necessary during pregnancy (rare), use the lowest reasonable dose, lead shielding over the abdomen, and document the medical justification.
source: ALARA principles; ACR/AAPM DXA Technical Standard

front: Per ISCD 2023 bilateral hip reporting position, when both hips are scanned:
back:
Use the LOWEST total hip or femoral neck T-score for diagnosis; use the MEAN bilateral total hip BMD for monitoring

Per ISCD 2023 Bilateral Hip DXA Reporting position (Tanner 2023): when both hips are scanned, use the LOWEST total hip or femoral neck T-score for diagnosis, and use the MEAN bilateral total hip BMD for monitoring. The lowest-T-score rule is conservative and reflects fracture risk; the mean rule for monitoring reduces measurement variability and improves serial comparison sensitivity. Bilateral hip acquisition is endorsed by ISCD as it provides additional diagnostic information without significant additional dose or time burden.
source: ISCD 2023 Bilateral Hip DXA Reporting position (Tanner et al.)

front: Per ISCD 2023 'Reporting Fewer Than Four Vertebrae' position (Rosen et al. 2023), when the standard 4-vertebrae L1-L4 cannot be reported:
back:
Report 3 or 2 valid vertebrae if available; do NOT report a single vertebra for diagnosis or monitoring; if fewer than 2 valid, base diagnosis on a different valid skeletal site

Per ISCD 2023 Reporting Fewer Than Four Vertebrae position (Rosen et al. 2023), when standard L1-L4 cannot be fully reported due to artifacts, fractures, or hardware: report 3 or 2 valid vertebrae if available; do NOT report a single vertebra for diagnosis or monitoring (variability and unreliability); if fewer than 2 valid vertebrae remain, base the diagnosis on a different valid skeletal site (hip and/or 33% radius). Document which vertebrae were excluded and why. The Reporting Fewer Than Four Vertebrae position formalized prior recommendations on minimum number of vertebrae and the prohibition on single-vertebra reporting.
source: ISCD 2023 Reporting Fewer Than Four Vertebrae position (Rosen et al.)

front: Per ISCD 2023 (Lau A et al.) Reporting of Full-Femur Length Images to Detect Incomplete Atypical Femoral Fracture position, full-femur DXA imaging is recommended:
back:
In patients receiving bisphosphonate or denosumab therapy (or who discontinued within the past year) with cumulative exposure of 3 or more years; clinical assessment of prodromal symptoms is NOT required

Per ISCD 2023 Reporting of Full-Femur Length Images to Detect Incomplete Atypical Femoral Fracture position (Lau A et al., JCD 2024;27(1):101439): consider bilateral full-length femur imaging (FFI) to detect abnormalities in the spectrum of AFF in patients receiving bisphosphonate or denosumab therapy (or who discontinued within the past year) with CUMULATIVE EXPOSURE of 3 or more years, especially those also on glucocorticoid therapy. Clinical assessment of prodromal symptoms (pain) is explicitly NOT required to obtain FFI. Findings: focal lateral cortical thickening (beaking) plus a transverse lucent line indicates high likelihood of incomplete AFF. Early detection allows intervention before complete fracture.

topic: very high fracture risk anabolic first
front: Per BHOF 2022 and the Endocrine Society 2020 guideline update, patients at VERY HIGH fracture risk (multiple fragility fractures, T-score below -3.0, or recent fracture) should be considered for:
back:
Initial therapy with an anabolic agent (teriparatide, abaloparatide, or romosozumab) followed by an antiresorptive, rather than starting with a bisphosphonate

Per AACE 2020 (Camacho et al.) and the ASBMR/BHOF 2024 Goal-Directed Treatment Task Force (Cosman et al.), patients at very high fracture risk (multiple fragility fractures, T-score -3.0 or below, recent fracture especially within 12 months, fracture while on therapy, or FRAX major osteoporotic fracture probability above 30%) should be considered for initial anabolic therapy (teriparatide, abaloparatide, or romosozumab) followed by an antiresorptive, rather than starting with a bisphosphonate. The Endocrine Society 2020 update (Shoback et al.) defines very high risk more narrowly as 'severe osteoporosis (T-score below -2.5 with fracture) or multiple vertebral fractures' and recommends romosozumab for up to 1 year (excluding patients with prior MI or stroke) or teriparatide/abaloparatide for up to 2 years, without specifying a preference between agents. After completing the anabolic course, transition to a bisphosphonate or denosumab to maintain gains.
source: AACE 2020 (Camacho et al.); ASBMR/BHOF 2024 (Cosman et al.); Endocrine Society 2020 update

front: After DXA acquisition, the BMD calculation requires:
back:
Software analysis of bone area, bone mineral content (BMC), and BMD = BMC/area

After acquisition, software analyzes the scan to detect bone edges, calculate bone area (cm2), measure bone mineral content (BMC, in grams), and compute BMD = BMC/area (g/cm2). Modern software auto-places ROIs and edge detection but the technologist verifies and adjusts. The 'B' in 'BMD' is bone mineral; BMD is areal density (g/cm2), not volumetric (which would be g/cm3 from QCT). Lab values, T-scores, and Z-scores are then derived from the BMD measurement and the relevant reference database.
source: Bonnick Bone Densitometry; standard DXA workflow

front: Per ISCD 2023, the trochanter T-score:
back:
Is calculated by software but should NOT be used for diagnosis or monitoring

Per ISCD 2023, the trochanter T-score is calculated by software but should NOT be used for diagnosis (similar to Wards triangle). The diagnostic hip sites are TOTAL HIP and FEMORAL NECK. ISCD 2023 prohibits trochanter and Wards area for diagnosis but does not formally address monitoring; trochanter is calculated as a subregion of total hip but has higher variability and is not endorsed as a stand-alone monitoring site. The total hip ROI includes the trochanter as a subregion (along with femoral neck and intertrochanteric).

front: All of the following osteoporosis drugs have specific renal function restrictions EXCEPT:
back:
Teriparatide (Forteo) - contraindicated below CrCl 60 mL/min

Teriparatide does NOT have a specific renal contraindication; it is generally well-tolerated in renal impairment, though dosage adjustment is not specified. Renal function restrictions: Zoledronic acid contraindicated CrCl below 35 mL/min (per FDA label); oral bisphosphonates (alendronate, risedronate, ibandronate) generally avoided CrCl below 30-35 mL/min; denosumab now carries a Boxed Warning for severe hypocalcemia in advanced CKD (especially dialysis with CKD-MBD, January 2024); romosozumab has no specific renal contraindication but caution warranted; raloxifene no renal limit. Teriparatide and abaloparatide are options for patients with CKD where bisphosphonates are contraindicated.
source: FDA prescribing information for each drug; BHOF 2022

front: Critical pre-DXA history elements that map to FRAX inputs include all EXCEPT:
back:
Number of falls in the past year

Falls history is NOT a FRAX input despite being a major fracture risk factor. FRAX inputs map to specific history elements: age, sex, weight, height, prior fragility fracture, parental hip fracture, current smoking, alcohol 3+ units/day, glucocorticoid use 5+ mg/day for more than 3 months (oral, current or past), rheumatoid arthritis (physician-diagnosed), secondary osteoporosis cause, and femoral neck BMD. Falls is a recognized FRAX limitation. The technologist should still note fall history because it informs clinical decision-making even though it's not in the FRAX algorithm. Some clinicians manually adjust FRAX upward for fall-prone patients.

front: The standard adult human spine has how many lumbar vertebrae?
back:
5

The standard adult human spine has 5 lumbar vertebrae (L1-L5), with L4-L5 being the most caudal lumbar level before the sacrum. PA spine DXA analyzes L1-L4. Lumbosacral transitional vertebrae (LSTV) anomalies occur with reported prevalence of approximately 4-30% of patients (Konin & Walz 2010; Apazidis 2011), with most large series reporting a mean of about 10-12%: lumbarization of S1 (6 lumbar bodies), sacralization of L5 (4 lumbar bodies), or other transitional anomalies. Variant anatomy must be recognized and consistently labeled across serial scans. T12 (last rib-bearing vertebra) and the iliac crest are key landmarks for vertebral identification.
source: Konin & Walz AJNR 2010; Apazidis Spine J 2011

front: Ibandronate (Boniva) IV dosing for osteoporosis is:
back:
3 mg IV every 3 months

Ibandronate IV dosing: 3 mg IV (over 15-30 seconds) every 3 months. Oral dosing: 150 mg PO once monthly. Boniva is the only quarterly IV bisphosphonate option for patients who cannot tolerate oral bisphosphonates but do not need annual zoledronic acid. Note: Boniva has only documented vertebral fracture risk reduction; no statistically significant non-vertebral or hip fracture protection. For high hip fracture risk, alendronate, risedronate, or zoledronic acid are preferred.

front: The WHO diagnostic criteria for osteoporosis (T-score -2.5 or below) were established by:
back:
A WHO Study Group in 1994 that defined T-score thresholds based on the relationship between BMD and fracture risk in postmenopausal Caucasian women

The WHO 1994 Study Group on Assessment of Fracture Risk and Its Application to Screening for Postmenopausal Osteoporosis established the T-score-based diagnostic criteria. Categories: Normal (T -1.0 or above), Osteopenia/low bone mass (-1.0 to -2.5), Osteoporosis (T -2.5 or below), Severe/established osteoporosis (T -2.5 or below PLUS fragility fracture). The criteria were originally developed for postmenopausal Caucasian women and were later extended to men 50+ (using NHANES III with the uniform White female reference per ISCD 2023). Z-scores are used for premenopausal women, men under 50, and children.

front: A new DXA technologist completing training at a facility should:
back:
Perform their own in vivo precision study (30 patients x 2 scans, or 15 patients x 3 scans) to calculate their personal LSC, because precision is technologist-dependent

Per ISCD 2023, precision is technologist-dependent. A new technologist must perform their own in vivo precision study to establish a personal LSC. Acceptable design: 30 patients x 2 scans each (yielding 30 degrees of freedom), OR 15 patients x 3 scans. Patients are repositioned between scans (gets up off the table). The new technologist's LSC may differ from the previous technologist's LSC; serial monitoring requires using the technologist's actual LSC. Using manufacturer-published or another technologist's values is not acceptable for clinical reporting.

front: Per ISCD 2023, when an individual vertebra's T-score differs by more than 1.0 from the average of adjacent vertebrae:
back:
Exclude that vertebra from BMD analysis (likely artifact: fracture, osteophyte, focal abnormality, or hardware)

Per ISCD 2023, when an individual vertebra's T-score differs by more than 1.0 from the average of adjacent vertebrae, exclude that vertebra from BMD analysis. The deviation indicates likely artifact: vertebral compression fracture (falsely high BMD), osteophyte or degenerative change (falsely high), focal lytic or sclerotic lesion, or surgical hardware. The technologist verifies by visual inspection of the scan image and clinical correlation. Document which vertebrae were excluded and why. Minimum 2 evaluable vertebrae required; if fewer than 2 valid, base diagnosis on hip and/or 33% radius.

front: Metal hardware in or near the femoral neck on a hip DXA most likely:
back:
Falsely elevates apparent BMD because metal absorbs X-rays heavily and the algorithm interprets it as bone

Metal hardware (screws, plates, rods, prosthetic components) absorbs X-rays much more strongly than bone, and the DXA algorithm interprets the high attenuation as bone, falsely elevating apparent BMD. Even partial hardware in the ROI invalidates the measurement. The correct response: exclude that hip from analysis; use the contralateral non-operated hip if intact, or substitute the 33% radius and PA spine. Document hardware presence in scan notes. This is why hip prostheses, fracture fixation hardware, and intramedullary rods preclude diagnostic hip BMD on the affected side.

front: The USPSTF recommends initial bone density screening for:
back:
Women age 65 and older, and younger postmenopausal women at increased risk based on a formal risk assessment

Per the USPSTF 2025 Final Recommendation Statement (January 14, 2025; JAMA 2025;333(6):498-508), which REPLACED the 2018 statement: screen women 65 and older (Grade B); screen postmenopausal women under 65 at increased risk based on formal clinical risk assessment, e.g., FRAX with or without BMD, OST, ORAI, SCORE, or OSIRIS (Grade B); insufficient evidence for or against screening men (Grade I, unchanged from 2018). BHOF 2022 has broader recommendations: BMD testing in men 70 and older regardless of risk factors and in men 50-69 with clinical risk factors for fracture, plus any man with fragility fracture at age 50 or older.

front: Per ISCD 2019 Pediatric Official Positions, a 'clinically significant fracture history' in a child or adolescent is defined as:
back:
Two or more long-bone fractures by age 10 years; OR three or more long-bone fractures at any age up to 19 years; OR one or more vertebral compression fractures in the absence of local disease or high-energy trauma

Per ISCD 2019 Pediatric Official Positions, a 'clinically significant fracture history' in a child or adolescent is: (a) two or more long-bone fractures by age 10 years; OR (b) three or more long-bone fractures at any age up to 19 years. Separately, one or more vertebral compression fractures in the absence of local disease or high-energy trauma is itself indicative of pediatric osteoporosis (independent of the long-bone count). Pediatric osteoporosis is NEVER diagnosed by densitometric criteria alone; clinical history of fracture or known bone-fragility disease is required.

front: Per ISCD 2019 Pediatric Official Positions, the preferred DXA measurement sites in children are:
back:
PA spine and total body less head (TBLH)

Per ISCD 2019 Pediatric Official Positions, the preferred DXA sites in children are PA spine (L1-L4) and total body less head (TBLH). ISCD endorses proximal femur measurement WHEN ADEQUATE REFERENCE DATA EXIST, especially for non-weight-bearing patients and continuity-of-care into adulthood. Lateral distal femur (LDF) is recommended for non-ambulatory children (cerebral palsy, muscular dystrophy). Z-scores (not T-scores) are used. For short stature or growth delay, ISCD-endorsed adjustments are bone mineral apparent density (BMAD, spine only) and height-for-age Z-score (HAZ) for both spine and TBLH.

front: Practical implementation of ALARA principles in DXA scanning includes all of the following EXCEPT:
back:
Performing additional scan repeats to improve image quality

Performing additional scan repeats VIOLATES ALARA because each repeat doubles the dose for that anatomic region. Repeats are justified only when the original is non-diagnostic due to motion, positioning error, or artifact. ALARA principles in DXA: keep dose As Low As Reasonably Achievable while obtaining diagnostic-quality results. Practical steps: confirm pregnancy status before exposure; use appropriate scan mode (fast for routine, precision for research); maintain minimum operator distance (IAEA RPOP: 1 m for pencil-beam, 2 m for fan-beam scanners); minimize scan field; do not unnecessarily repeat acquisitions; train and credential operators per ISCD/ARRT standards.
source: IAEA RPOP DXA staff guidance; ALARA principles

front: The Evenity (romosozumab) Boxed Warning specifically warns against:
back:
Cardiovascular events (myocardial infarction, stroke, cardiovascular death); do not initiate within 1 year of MI or stroke

The Evenity (romosozumab) Boxed Warning warns against cardiovascular events: myocardial infarction, stroke, and cardiovascular death. Per the Boxed Warning and Section 5.1, romosozumab should NOT be initiated in patients with MI or stroke within the previous year (this is in the Boxed Warning and Warnings & Precautions, NOT a formal Section 4 Contraindication; Section 4 contraindications are hypocalcemia and hypersensitivity). Risks/benefits should be weighed in patients with other CV risk factors. Dosing: 210 mg SC monthly for 12 doses (limited to 12-month course). After completion, transition to antiresorptive (typically alendronate or denosumab) to maintain BMD gains. ARCH trial showed CV signal vs alendronate (HR 1.87).

front: When Prolia (denosumab) therapy is permanently discontinued, the appropriate management is:
back:
Transition to a bisphosphonate (oral or IV) within 6 months of the last Prolia dose to mitigate rebound vertebral fracture risk

Per Tsourdi et al. ECTS Position Paper (J Clin Endocrinol Metab 2021;106:264-281) and Cummings et al. FREEDOM analysis (JBMR 2018;33:190-198), denosumab discontinuation is associated with rapid bone turnover rebound and a substantial increase in multiple vertebral fractures, with peak risk 8-16 months after the last dose. Patients permanently discontinuing denosumab should be transitioned to a bisphosphonate (oral or IV) at the time the next dose would be due (approximately 6 months after the last Prolia) to mitigate rebound vertebral fracture risk. Most evidence supports IV zoledronic acid 5 mg given approximately 6 months after the last denosumab dose, sometimes with additional doses based on bone turnover markers. Rebound risk is in the Warnings and Precautions section, NOT the Boxed Warning (which addresses severe hypocalcemia in advanced CKD per the January 19 2024 update).
source: Tsourdi 2021 ECTS Position Paper; Cummings 2018 JBMR; FDA Prolia label

front: The Least Significant Change (LSC) at 95% confidence is calculated as:
back:
2.77 x RMS-SD precision error

LSC at 95% confidence = 2.77 x RMS-SD (root mean square standard deviation) precision error from an in vivo precision study. The factor 2.77 = 2 x 1.96 / square root of 2 (because comparing two measurements requires accounting for variance from both). For example, a precision error of 0.012 g/cm^2 yields LSC = 0.033 g/cm^2. Serial BMD changes smaller than the LSC are NOT statistically significant and should not drive clinical decisions. ISCD 2023 requires the follow-up DXA report to state the facility LSC and indicate whether change reaches statistical significance.

front: Per ISCD 2023, VFA is indicated based on historical height loss greater than:
back:
4 cm (1.5 in)

Per ISCD 2023, VFA is indicated based on historical height loss greater than 4 cm (greater than 1.5 inches) compared to peak adult height. The 6 cm threshold from older guidance was removed in 2013. Other VFA indications: prospective height loss greater than 2 cm during adult monitoring; T-score -1.0 or below in postmenopausal women or men 50+ with risk factors (prior fracture, glucocorticoid use 5+ mg/d for more than 3 months, or T-score below -1.5); known vertebral fracture; chronic systemic glucocorticoid use 5+ mg/day prednisolone-equivalent for more than 3 months.

front: Per the Genant semi-quantitative classification of vertebral fractures, the height-loss thresholds are:
back:
Grade 1 = 20-25%, Grade 2 = 25-40%, Grade 3 = above 40%

Per Genant 1993 semi-quantitative vertebral fracture classification: Grade 0 = normal (no height loss); Grade 1 (mild) = 20-25% height loss; Grade 2 (moderate) = 25-40% height loss; Grade 3 (severe) = approximately 40% or greater height loss. Anterior, middle, or posterior wedge configurations are documented separately. Genant criteria assess both height reduction and projected vertebral area reduction. The Genant scale is the most widely used VFA reading system and is endorsed by ISCD 2023 for VFA reporting. Vertebral fractures are determined by visualization plus quantitative height ratio assessment.

front: VFA imaging typically does NOT reliably visualize:
back:
T1-T4 (upper thoracic vertebrae, due to overlying anatomy)

Upper thoracic vertebrae (T1-T3, often T4) are typically not reliably visualized due to overlying ribs, scapulae, and mediastinum. Standard VFA covers approximately T4-L4. ISCD 2023 specifies that vertebrae between T7-L4 must be identifiable on VFA; unidentifiable vertebrae in this range warrant follow-up imaging. Most common osteoporotic vertebral fracture location is the thoracolumbar junction (T11-L2, especially T12 and L1), well-visualized on VFA. Document any vertebrae that cannot be evaluated with reasons.

front: FRAX 10-year fracture probability is validated for which age range?
back:
Ages 40-90

FRAX is validated for ages 40-90. Outside this range the algorithm should not be used. Other key FRAX limitations: validated only with femoral neck BMD (not spine); validated only in untreated patients (current bisphosphonate or other osteoporosis therapy alters fracture risk); does NOT include falls history (recognized limitation); does NOT include lumbar spine BMD; does NOT account for dose-response of glucocorticoids or smoking. US-adapted FRAX is recalibrated to US fracture and mortality rates and is the version used in BHOF treatment thresholds.
source: FRAX (Kanis et al. WHO Collaborating Centre)

front: When a DXA scanner is replaced with a same-make/model unit, the ISCD cross-calibration procedure is:
back:
Per ISCD: scan one phantom on 20 different days on each device; investigate or service if mean BMD differs by more than 0.5%; for different manufacturer or out-of-tolerance same-model, perform in-vivo cross-calibration with 30 patients scanned twice on each system

Per ISCD 2019/2023, when adding a scanner of the same make and model: scan one phantom on 20 different days on each device; investigate or service if mean BMD differs by more than 0.5%. When changing to a different manufacturer or technology (or when a same-model device is out of tolerance), perform in-vivo cross-calibration using 30 patients representative of the population, scanned twice on the index and twice on the new system within 60 days (no more than 30 days apart). A new in-vivo precision study and LSC must be established for the new device. Cross-calibration was a knowledge area in the 2022 ARRT BD content specifications but was REMOVED from the 2027 specifications (effective Jan 1, 2027). Note: 1%/2% tolerances apply to body-composition cross-calibration, not BMD.
source: ISCD 2019/2023 Cross-calibration Position; 2022 vs 2027 ARRT specs

front: On hip DXA, the femoral neck ROI is oriented:
back:
Perpendicular to the long axis of the femoral neck (not the shaft); GE Lunar places the box at the narrowest portion of the neck, while Hologic places a fixed-width 1.5 cm box at a fixed mid-neck location anchored to the lateral edge of the greater trochanter

The femoral neck ROI is oriented perpendicular to the long axis of the femoral neck (not the shaft) and excludes the femoral head and greater/lesser trochanters. GE Lunar software places the neck box at the narrowest portion of the neck. Hologic places a fixed-width (approximately 1.5 cm / 15-pixel) box at a fixed mid-neck location anchored to the lateral edge of the greater trochanter. ROI placement is automated but must be verified by the technologist. For follow-up scans, use the manufacturer's compare-prior tool so identical ROI position is applied.

front: Per BHOF 2022, a bisphosphonate drug holiday after 3-5 years (oral) or 3 years (IV) of therapy may be considered in patients at LOW fracture risk who meet all of the following:
back:
T-score above -2.5 at all measured sites; no new fragility fractures during therapy; no prior hip or vertebral fracture

Per BHOF 2022, a bisphosphonate drug holiday may be considered in LOW-risk patients after 3-5 years of oral or 3 years of IV bisphosphonate therapy who meet ALL of the following: T-score above -2.5 at all measured sites; no new fragility fractures during therapy; no prior hip or vertebral fragility fracture. HIGH-risk patients (T-score below -2.5, prior hip/vertebral fracture, or fracture during therapy) should continue therapy or transition to a different agent. Drug holiday duration is typically 1-3 years for oral bisphosphonates (or until BMD declines significantly or fracture occurs); the long bone-residence time of bisphosphonates supports this strategy. This concept does NOT apply to denosumab (rebound risk) or anabolics (not used long-term).

front: Per ISCD 2019 Pediatric, a vertebral compression fracture occurring without high-impact trauma or local disease in a child:
back:
Is diagnostic of pediatric osteoporosis even without low BMD Z-score

Per ISCD 2019 Pediatric Official Positions, a vertebral compression fracture occurring in the absence of high-impact trauma or local disease is itself diagnostic of pediatric osteoporosis, regardless of BMD Z-score. This contrasts with adults, where vertebral fragility fracture alone is diagnostic only with osteopenia (per NBHA 2014). Pediatric clinical context: children with chronic illness (cerebral palsy, leukemia, glucocorticoid therapy, anorexia nervosa) are at risk; vertebral fractures may be asymptomatic and require imaging recognition. Vertebral fragility fracture in pediatric is high-priority for treatment consideration.

front: Per AAOMS 2022 Position Paper, MRONJ incidence in cancer patients on high-dose IV bisphosphonates (Zometa) or high-dose denosumab (Xgeva 120 mg monthly) is approximately:
back:
Less than 5% (with most studies reporting 1-2%)

Per AAOMS 2022 Position Paper on Medication-Related Osteonecrosis of the Jaw (Ruggiero et al. JOMS 2022), MRONJ incidence varies by drug exposure and indication. In cancer patients receiving high-dose IV bisphosphonates (Zometa) or high-dose denosumab (Xgeva 120 mg monthly): less than 5% incidence (most studies report 1-2%). In osteoporosis-dose use (oral bisphosphonates or Prolia 60 mg every 6 months): less than 0.05% incidence (literature range approximately 0.001-0.1%). Major risk factors: invasive dental procedures (extractions especially), poor oral hygiene, glucocorticoid use, smoking, diabetes, longer treatment duration. Pre-treatment dental evaluation recommended.
source: AAOMS 2022 Position Paper (Ruggiero et al. JOMS 2022)

front: Per the Shane 2014 ASBMR Task Force revised case definition, atypical femoral fracture (AFF) diagnosis requires at least 4 of 5 MAJOR features. Which of the following is one of the 5 MAJOR features?
back:
Localized periosteal or endosteal thickening of the lateral cortex at the fracture site (beaking or flaring)

Per Shane 2014 ASBMR Task Force revised case definition (JBMR 29:1), AFFs occur along the femoral diaphysis from just distal to the lesser trochanter to just proximal to the supracondylar flare (excluding femoral neck, intertrochanteric, peri-prosthetic, and pathologic fractures). Diagnosis requires 4 or more of 5 MAJOR features: (1) minimal or no trauma; (2) fracture line originating at the lateral cortex, substantially transverse (may become oblique medially); (3) complete fractures extend through both cortices and may have a medial spike, incomplete fractures involve only the lateral cortex; (4) non-comminuted or minimally comminuted; (5) localized periosteal or endosteal thickening of the lateral cortex at the fracture site (beaking or flaring). MINOR features (NOT required for diagnosis): generalized cortical thickening; prodromal thigh/groin pain; bilaterality; delayed healing; comorbidities (vit D deficiency, RA, hypophosphatasia); concurrent pharmaceuticals. Risk factors: long-term bisphosphonate or denosumab therapy 3+ years, Asian ancestry, glucocorticoid co-therapy, femoral bowing.
source: Shane et al. JBMR 2014;29:1 ASBMR Task Force

front: Standard DXA consent process typically includes:
back:
Verbal brief consent because the radiation dose is very low; institutional policy may require written acknowledgment

Standard DXA consent: verbal brief consent because the radiation dose is very low (less than 1 day natural background); institutional policy may require written acknowledgment forms. The technologist explains: the procedure, dose context, expected duration (15-30 minutes typical), need to remove metal, what to expect during scan. Research DXA follows IRB-mandated written informed consent. Patient questions about diagnosis or treatment should be redirected to the ordering physician (technologist scope of practice does not include diagnosis or treatment counseling).
source: Standard institutional protocols; ARRT/ASRT scope of practice

front: For a standard hip DXA, the foot should be internally rotated approximately:
back:
15-25 degrees against a foot positioning device

For standard hip DXA, the foot is internally rotated approximately 15-25 degrees against a foot positioning device (Hologic NHANES protocol specifies 25 degrees; general DXA practice 15-25 degrees covers Hologic and Lunar; Lunar typically 15-20 degrees). Internal rotation maximizes femoral neck length on the projected image and minimizes BMD variability. Without proper rotation, the femoral neck appears foreshortened (lesser trochanter prominent) and BMD is artifactually lowered. The foot positioning device provides reproducibility for serial scans. Document rotation deviations.
source: Hologic NHANES protocol; manufacturer documentation; Slart 2024

front: Menopausal hormone therapy (MHT) for osteoporosis prevention is FDA-approved indication:
back:
For prevention of postmenopausal osteoporosis when non-estrogen therapies are unsuitable; with consideration of CV/breast cancer risks per Boxed Warnings

MHT (estrogen alone if hysterectomized; estrogen plus progestin if uterus intact, or Duavee/conjugated estrogens-bazedoxifene) is FDA-approved for prevention of postmenopausal osteoporosis WHEN NON-ESTROGEN therapies are unsuitable, with consideration of CV/breast cancer risks per Boxed Warnings. Lowest effective dose for shortest duration. WHI trial (2002) showed risks (CHD, stroke, VTE, breast cancer with combined therapy) outweighed benefits in older women; MHT is now reserved for younger postmenopausal women (under 60 or within 10 years of menopause) with vasomotor symptoms. NOT first-line for osteoporosis in patients without vasomotor symptoms.

front: Severe scoliosis on PA spine DXA most commonly causes BMD to be:
back:
Falsely elevated due to vertebral rotation and superimposed structures

Severe scoliosis falsely elevates PA spine BMD due to vertebral rotation and superimposed structures (Pappou 2006; phantom and cadaveric studies). The rotated vertebrae project additional bone mass into the BMD calculation. ISCD 2023 does NOT specify a numeric Cobb-angle cutoff but instructs that vertebrae 'clearly abnormal and non-assessable' or differing by more than 1.0 T-score from neighbors be excluded. If fewer than 2 evaluable vertebrae remain, use a different valid site (hip or 33% radius). Phantom data suggest BMD is minimally affected at axial rotation 15 degrees or less.

front: Per WHO 1994, a postmenopausal woman with T-score -1.7 at the femoral neck is classified as:
back:
Low bone mass (osteopenia)

Per WHO 1994 diagnostic categories: Normal = T-score -1.0 or above; Low bone mass (osteopenia) = T-score between -1.0 and -2.5; Osteoporosis = T-score -2.5 or below; Severe (established) osteoporosis = T-score -2.5 or below PLUS one or more fragility fractures. T-score -1.7 falls in the osteopenia range. Note: WHO 1994 categories were originally developed for postmenopausal Caucasian women; ISCD 2023 extended applicability to men 50+ using the uniform White female reference database. The term 'low bone mass' is preferred over 'osteopenia' to avoid implying disease in what may be normal age-related variation.

front: As of 2025, which osteoporosis drug currently carries a Boxed Warning?
back:
Denosumab (Prolia) for severe hypocalcemia in advanced CKD; Romosozumab (Evenity) for cardiovascular events; Raloxifene (Evista) for VTE/stroke; estrogen products for cardiovascular events/breast cancer

Current Boxed Warnings on osteoporosis drugs as of 2025: PROLIA (denosumab) - severe hypocalcemia in advanced CKD especially dialysis CKD-MBD (added January 19, 2024; REMS program); EVENITY (romosozumab) - cardiovascular events (MI, stroke, CV death; do not initiate within 1 year of MI/stroke); EVISTA (raloxifene) - VTE/PE plus death from stroke in PMW with documented CHD or at risk for major coronary events; ESTROGEN products including DUAVEE - cardiovascular events, stroke, VTE, and breast cancer with prolonged use. NO Boxed Warnings on: teriparatide (Forteo) (removed November 16 2020), abaloparatide (Tymlos) (removed December 22 2021), oral bisphosphonates, IV zoledronic acid, calcitonin (warning language but not Boxed).
source: Current FDA prescribing information for each drug

front: Postmenopausal estrogen deficiency causes accelerated bone loss primarily through:
back:
Increased RANKL expression and decreased OPG production by osteoblasts, shifting the RANKL/OPG ratio toward osteoclast activation

Postmenopausal estrogen deficiency causes accelerated bone loss primarily by shifting the RANKL/OPG (osteoprotegerin) ratio toward osteoclast activation. Estrogen normally suppresses RANKL expression and increases OPG production by osteoblasts; loss of estrogen does the opposite. RANKL binds RANK on osteoclast precursors, activating osteoclastogenesis and bone resorption. OPG is a soluble decoy receptor that binds RANKL and prevents RANK activation. Denosumab is a monoclonal antibody against RANKL that mimics OPG action. Other contributors to postmenopausal bone loss: reduced calcium absorption, increased PTH, increased osteocyte apoptosis. The RANKL/OPG axis is a central concept in bone biology.

front: In patients expected to lose bone rapidly (high-dose glucocorticoids, transplant, hyperthyroidism), follow-up DXA is typically performed every:
back:
6-12 months

In patients expected to lose bone rapidly (high-dose glucocorticoids 7.5+ mg/day prednisone, organ transplant recipients in first 1-2 years, untreated hyperthyroidism, primary hyperparathyroidism, advanced anorexia nervosa), follow-up DXA every 6-12 months is typical. Standard pharmacotherapy follow-up is 1-2 years per BHOF 2022. Stable long-term patients on therapy can extend to every 2-3 years per ISCD/clinical practice. More frequent than 1 year provides little useful information because BMD change must exceed LSC to be meaningful, and LSC corresponds to roughly 1 year of expected change in most clinical settings.

front: Pharmacotherapy options FDA-approved for men with osteoporosis include all EXCEPT:
back:
Raloxifene (Evista)

Raloxifene (Evista) is FDA-approved for postmenopausal women only; not approved for men. FDA-approved for men with osteoporosis: alendronate, risedronate, zoledronic acid (all bisphosphonates with men's-osteoporosis indications); denosumab (Prolia) for men at high fracture risk; teriparatide (Forteo) for primary or hypogonadal men's osteoporosis. Abaloparatide (Tymlos) was originally approved only for postmenopausal women; FDA expanded the indication to include men with osteoporosis at high risk of fracture on December 20, 2022 (based on the ATOM trial). Romosozumab (Evenity) currently approved only for postmenopausal women.
source: FDA prescribing information for each drug; Radius Health press release Dec 20 2022

front: Trabecular Bone Score (TBS) is calculated from PA spine DXA images and:
back:
Provides indirect assessment of trabecular microarchitecture from gray-level texture analysis; used as an adjunct to BMD for fracture risk assessment in adults 40+; integrates with FRAX

Trabecular Bone Score (TBS) is calculated from PA spine DXA images via gray-level texture analysis of the L1-L4 region. It provides indirect assessment of trabecular microarchitecture (homogeneity of pixel intensity variation reflects underlying bone structure quality). Per ISCD 2023, TBS is endorsed as an adjunct to BMD for fracture risk assessment in adults 40+. TBS integrates with the TBS-adjusted FRAX algorithm to refine 10-year fracture probability. L1-L4 is used WITHOUT EXCLUSIONS for moderate degenerative changes or chronic compression fractures, BUT TBS should NOT be reported when there is severe structural or pathological artifact (vertebra plana, laminectomy, hardware, metastases). Numeric thresholds (1.310 normal, below 1.230 degraded) come from Medimaps software, NOT ISCD; ISCD does not publish numeric cutoffs and cautions that monitoring TBS change with current algorithms is not helpful.

front: Per ISCD 2019 Pediatric, ideal reference data for pediatric DXA Z-score calculation:
back:
Should be sex-specific, age-specific, and from the same DXA scanner manufacturer; race/ethnicity-specific data preferred when available; adjustment for height-for-age (HAZ) or Tanner stage may be needed for short or delayed-puberty children

Per ISCD 2019 Pediatric Official Positions, pediatric reference data must be sex-specific, age-specific, and ideally from the same DXA scanner manufacturer because manufacturer-specific values differ substantially. Race/ethnicity-specific reference data should be used when available. For short stature or delayed puberty, additional adjustments for height-for-age Z-score (HAZ) or Tanner/sexual maturity stage are recommended to avoid false-positive 'low BMD for chronological age' findings. The Bone Mineral Density in Childhood Study (BMDCS) provides reference data for Hologic Discovery scanners aged 5-20. For adolescents 18-20, use either pediatric or adult references depending on growth completion.

front: When a patient asks the technologist 'What did my scan show?', the appropriate response is:
back:
Explain that the technologist's role is to acquire the scan; results will be interpreted by the radiologist or ordering physician who will communicate them to the patient

The technologist's scope of practice does NOT include result interpretation, diagnosis, or treatment counseling. Appropriate response: acknowledge the patient's question, explain that results will be interpreted by the radiologist/interpreting physician and communicated by the ordering provider, and offer to assist with workflow questions (when results expected, how to follow up). The technologist may share that the scan was acquired successfully and any technical findings (e.g., 'I noted hardware on the right hip and we used the left for analysis'). Discussing T-scores or providing interpretive opinions exceeds scope and may be considered the unauthorized practice of medicine.
source: ARRT Standards of Ethics; ASRT Scope of Practice

front: When DXA scanner software is updated to a new version, the appropriate QC response is:
back:
Verify scanner stability with phantom QC; rerun a sample of recent patients with both old and new versions to assess BMD shifts; document software version on all reports; consider whether a new precision study is required if analysis algorithms changed substantially

Software updates may affect BMD calculation, ROI placement, edge detection, or reference databases. Appropriate QC response: verify scanner stability with phantom QC after the update; if phantom values shift, contact the manufacturer; rerun a representative sample of recent patients with both old and new software versions to assess clinical impact (BMD shifts greater than 1-2% may require cross-calibration or recalibration); document software version on all reports going forward; consider a new in vivo precision study if analysis algorithms changed substantially. Some software updates change reference databases (e.g., race/ethnicity adjustments removed); this affects T-scores and Z-scores and must be communicated to interpreting physicians.
source: Manufacturer software upgrade documentation; ISCD recommendations

front: A 38-year-old premenopausal woman has Z-score -2.6 at the spine and a recent low-trauma vertebral fracture. Initial evaluation should focus on:
back:
Identifying secondary causes (thyroid disease, celiac disease, hyperparathyroidism, hypercortisolism, multiple myeloma, hypogonadism, eating disorder, medications including glucocorticoids and chemotherapy); treating the underlying cause is often more effective than empiric bisphosphonate

A premenopausal woman with low BMD plus fragility fracture meets criteria for premenopausal osteoporosis (per ISCD 2019). Approximately 50% of premenopausal osteoporosis cases have an identifiable secondary cause. Initial evaluation focuses on identifying secondary causes: thyroid disease (TSH); celiac disease (tTG IgA with total IgA); hyperparathyroidism (PTH, calcium); hypercortisolism (24-hr urinary free cortisol); multiple myeloma (SPEP/serum free light chains); hypogonadism (estradiol, FSH/LH, prolactin); eating disorder (clinical history, weight, menses); medications (glucocorticoids, chemotherapy, anticonvulsants, PPIs); other (CKD, liver disease). Treating the underlying cause is often more effective than empiric bisphosphonate; bisphosphonates may be teratogenic (long bone residence) and require contraceptive counseling.

front: Per ISCD 2023, the recommended VFA reading approach is:
back:
Visual semi-quantitative approach (Genant method) is most widely used; quantitative morphometry may supplement; readers should be trained in vertebral fracture identification

Per ISCD 2023 VFA position, the recommended approach is the visual semi-quantitative method (Genant 1993): visual assessment of vertebral shape (wedge, biconcave, crush) plus height ratio measurement, classified as Grade 0 (normal), Grade 1 (mild, 20-25% height loss), Grade 2 (moderate, 25-40%), Grade 3 (severe, more than 40%). Quantitative six-point morphometry may supplement when shape is ambiguous. Readers should be trained in vertebral fracture identification (radiologist or ISCD-certified clinical densitometrist). Differentiate true fractures from physiologic shape variations (Schmorl nodes, Cupid's bow, congenital wedging). Document each vertebral level reviewed.

front: On a PA lumbar DXA scan, T12 is reliably identified by:
back:
The presence of the lowest pair of ribs articulating with it

T12 is the lowest rib-bearing vertebra and is the primary landmark for lumbar spine DXA labeling. Once T12 is identified by its rib articulations, L1 through L4 are counted downward. Anatomic variants (lumbarization, sacralization, 11 or 13 ribs) require careful re-counting. Document any variants in the report so the same labeling convention is applied at follow-up.
source: ARRT 2022 BD spec; DXA practice references

front: The standard PA spine DXA ROI for diagnosis includes:
back:
L1-L4

The standard PA lumbar spine DXA ROI is L1 through L4. T12 and L5 are not included because of overlying ribs (T12) and pelvic/sacral structures (L5). When evaluable vertebrae must be excluded for structural artifact, the remaining 2 or 3 vertebrae are reported with appropriate notation. ISCD 2023 specifies that diagnosis should never be based on a single vertebra.

front: Per ISCD 2023 (Rosen position), the minimum number of evaluable vertebrae required for spine BMD reporting is:
back:
2 evaluable vertebrae (single vertebra is never used)

Per ISCD 2023 Reporting Fewer Than 4 Vertebrae position (Rosen et al.), a single vertebra is never used for diagnosis or monitoring. A minimum of 2 evaluable vertebrae are required. When fewer than 2 evaluable vertebrae remain after exclusion of structural artifacts, the spine is non-diagnostic and another valid skeletal site (hip or 33% radius) should be used.

front: If structural artifacts require exclusion of all 4 lumbar vertebrae from analysis, the technologist should:
back:
Report the spine as non-diagnostic and rely on another valid site (hip or 33% radius)

When structural artifact requires exclusion of all 4 lumbar vertebrae, the spine site is non-diagnostic and the technologist or interpreter should rely on another valid measurement site (hip or 33% radius). A single vertebra is never used. The DXA report should clearly state which vertebrae were excluded and the reason.

front: Severe scoliosis typically affects PA lumbar BMD in which way?
back:
Falsely elevated BMD due to vertebral rotation and projection of additional bone into the ROI

Severe scoliosis falsely elevates PA lumbar BMD because vertebral rotation and lateral curvature project additional bone mass into the projected area, increasing measured BMC without a corresponding increase in true bone density. Mild scoliosis has minimal effect; significant lateral curvature warrants consideration of an alternative site (hip or 33% radius). Document the scoliosis on the report with the affected vertebrae.

front: Severe osteophytes and end-plate sclerosis on a PA lumbar scan typically:
back:
Falsely elevate the apparent BMD because the additional dense bone is included in the ROI

Severe osteophytes, end-plate sclerosis, and facet joint osteoarthritis falsely elevate PA lumbar BMD because the dense degenerative bone is included in the ROI but does not reflect true vertebral bone density. Affected vertebrae meet criteria for exclusion when the focal increase is structural and obvious. Hip or 33% radius is preferred when most lumbar vertebrae are affected by degenerative change.

front: Aortic calcification overlying the lumbar spine on a PA DXA scan:
back:
Falsely elevates the apparent BMD if not excluded from the ROI

Aortic calcification overlying the lumbar spine on PA DXA falsely elevates the apparent BMD because the dense calcific deposits are projected into the vertebral ROI and cannot be separated from bone by the dual-energy algorithm. Lateral spine DXA can avoid this artifact but lateral BMD is not used for diagnosis per ISCD 2023. When extensive aortic calcification is present, consider hip or 33% radius for diagnosis.

front: A laminectomy at L4 has what effect on the PA spine DXA measurement at that level?
back:
Falsely lowers the apparent BMD because posterior elements have been removed and contribute less to the projected bone mass

A laminectomy at L4 removes the posterior elements (lamina, spinous process, possibly portions of the pedicles) that normally contribute to the projected bone mass on PA DXA. The resulting BMD at the affected level is falsely lowered. Exclude the affected vertebra from analysis. Document the surgical history. If multiple posterior surgeries make the spine non-diagnostic, default to hip or 33% radius.

front: Posterior spinal fusion with rods and pedicle screws affects PA lumbar DXA by:
back:
Severely distorting BMD; affected vertebrae must be excluded from analysis

Spinal fusion hardware (rods, pedicle screws, interbody cages) severely distorts PA DXA BMD measurement. The metal hardware has very high attenuation that cannot be separated from bone by the dual-energy algorithm. Affected vertebrae must be excluded. If most or all of L1-L4 are involved (multilevel fusion), the spine is non-diagnostic; use hip or 33% radius.

front: On a PA lumbar DXA, the intervertebral disc spaces are obscured. The technologist should:
back:
Manually adjust intervertebral lines using anatomic landmarks (T12 ribs, iliac crest, vertebral shape) and verify against the patient's history

When intervertebral spaces are obscured (severe degenerative disc disease, fusion, scoliosis), the technologist manually adjusts the intervertebral lines using anatomic landmarks: T12 identified by lowest pair of ribs, iliac crest crossing at L4 body or L4-L5 disc space, vertebral body shape. Verify labeling against any prior imaging or surgical history. Document any anatomic variants for consistent labeling at follow-up scans.

front: A patient has an L2 vertebral compression fracture visible on the DXA scan image. Per analysis convention, L2:
back:
Is excluded from BMD analysis because the compressed vertebra has falsely elevated BMD due to compaction of bone into a smaller projected area

Vertebral fractures inflate the apparent BMD because bone is compressed into a smaller projected area, producing higher BMC per unit area. Fractured vertebrae must be excluded from BMD analysis. The DXA scan image (or VFA) should be reviewed for vertebral fractures; suspected fractures warrant lateral spine imaging for confirmation. Document the exclusion clearly with the reason.

front: On a PA lumbar DXA report, the standard reported values include:
back:
BMC in g, area in cm^2, BMD in g/cm^2 (areal)

DXA reports areal BMD (g/cm^2) calculated as BMC (g) divided by projected bone area (cm^2). BMC is in grams; area is in square centimeters; BMD is grams per square centimeter. T-score and Z-score are unitless standardized values. Per ISCD 2023, BMD is reported to 3 decimal places, BMC to 2 decimal places, area to 2 decimal places, T-scores and Z-scores to 1 decimal place.
source: ARRT 2022 BD spec; ISCD 2023 Decimal Digits

front: The T-score on a DXA report compares the patient's BMD to:
back:
The young-adult reference population (peak bone mass), expressed in standard deviations

The T-score expresses the patient's BMD as the number of standard deviations from the mean of a young-adult reference population at peak bone mass (typically aged 20-29). Per WHO 1994, T-score thresholds (-1.0 normal, -1.0 to -2.5 osteopenia, -2.5 or below osteoporosis) apply to postmenopausal women and men 50 and older. The Z-score (age-matched) is used for premenopausal women, men under 50, and children.

front: The Z-score on a DXA report compares the patient's BMD to:
back:
An age-, sex-, and ethnicity-matched reference population

The Z-score expresses the patient's BMD as the number of standard deviations from the mean of an age-, sex-, and (when available) ethnicity-matched reference population. Z-scores are the appropriate score for premenopausal women, men under age 50, and children/adolescents. ISCD specifies Z-score below -2.0 in premenopausal women is termed 'below the expected range for age' rather than 'osteoporosis.'

front: For follow-up PA lumbar DXA, the technologist should:
back:
Reproduce baseline patient positioning, scan parameters (mode), ROI placement, and use the compare-prior tool to maintain identical analysis

Per ISCD 2023, follow-up scans must reproduce baseline acquisition and analysis as closely as possible. The same scanner, same scan mode, same patient positioning, and same ROI placement (using compare-prior tools) are required. Changes in any of these parameters introduce artifactual BMD differences that may exceed the LSC. Document any deviations from baseline.
source: ARRT 2022 BD spec; ISCD 2023 Follow-up BMD

front: The PA lumbar spine is classified as:
back:
A central DXA site (along with hip)

Central DXA sites are the lumbar spine and proximal femur (hip). The 33% radius is also a central-equivalent diagnostic site per ISCD 2023 when one of the central sites cannot be measured. Peripheral DXA refers to measurements at the heel, finger, or wrist using portable peripheral devices, which are screening tools and not used for diagnosis or monitoring.

front: A 62-year-old postmenopausal woman has a PA lumbar T-score of -2.7 with no prior fragility fractures. Per WHO 1994 criteria, the diagnosis is:
back:
Osteoporosis

Per WHO 1994 criteria, T-score of -2.5 or below = osteoporosis. T-score -1.0 or above = normal; T-score between -1.0 and -2.5 = low bone mass (osteopenia); T-score -2.5 or below plus fragility fracture = severe (established) osteoporosis. T-score -2.7 falls in the osteoporosis range without fragility fracture.

front: A patient has a PA lumbar T-score of -1.4. Per WHO 1994, this is classified as:
back:
Low bone mass (osteopenia)

Per WHO 1994, T-score between -1.0 and -2.5 = low bone mass (also called osteopenia). T-score -1.4 falls in this range. The clinical implication depends on additional risk factors; FRAX should be calculated using femoral neck BMD (not spine) to estimate 10-year fracture probability and guide treatment decisions.

front: Per ISCD 2023, when multiple skeletal sites are measured (lumbar spine, total hip, femoral neck), the diagnostic T-score for WHO classification is:
back:
The lowest of the lumbar spine, femoral neck, or total hip T-scores

Per ISCD 2023, the diagnostic T-score is the LOWEST of the lumbar spine, femoral neck, or total hip. The 33% radius can be used when one of these sites cannot be measured. The trochanter and Ward's area are NOT used for diagnosis. WHO classification (-2.5 or below = osteoporosis) is then applied to that lowest T-score.
