front: A 65-year-old woman has a PA lumbar T-score of -1.2 and a femoral neck T-score of -2.7. The diagnostic conclusion (per ISCD/WHO):
back:
Osteoporosis (use the lowest T-score; diagnosis driven by femoral neck)

Per ISCD 2023, the diagnostic T-score is the lowest among lumbar spine, femoral neck, and total hip. Femoral neck T-score of -2.7 falls in the osteoporosis range. The patient is diagnosed with osteoporosis. The discordance between spine and hip is common in older patients (hip declines faster after age 65; spine may be falsely elevated by degenerative changes or aortic calcification).

front: A 38-year-old premenopausal woman has a PA lumbar Z-score of -2.4 with no fractures or known secondary cause. The interpretation per ISCD is:
back:
Below the expected range for age (Z-score of -2.0 or lower); osteoporosis is NOT diagnosed by Z-score alone in premenopausal women

Per ISCD 2023, a Z-score of -2.0 or lower in a premenopausal woman is termed 'below the expected range for age.' Osteoporosis is NOT diagnosed by Z-score alone in this population. The diagnosis of premenopausal osteoporosis requires additional findings: clinically significant fragility fracture history, OR identifiable secondary cause (glucocorticoid use, eating disorder, hypogonadism, celiac disease).

front: Standard follow-up DXA interval after starting bisphosphonate therapy is typically:
back:
1 to 2 years (Medicare BMMA covers every 23 months)

Standard follow-up DXA interval after starting or changing osteoporosis therapy is 1 to 2 years. Medicare BMMA covers central DXA every 23 months baseline; shorter intervals require documented medical necessity (rapid bone loss conditions: high-dose glucocorticoids, transplant first 1 to 2 years, untreated hyperthyroidism, primary hyperparathyroidism, advanced eating disorder). Stable long-term patients can extend to every 2 to 3 years.

front: On the DXA patient history form, glucocorticoid use is clinically significant for bone health when:
back:
Prednisone-equivalent 5 mg/day or more for 3 months or more (cumulative chronic use)

Per BHOF 2022, ACR Glucocorticoid-Induced Osteoporosis guidelines, and FRAX inputs, glucocorticoid use is clinically significant when the dose is prednisone-equivalent 5 mg/day or more for 3 months or more (cumulative chronic use). Higher doses (7.5 mg/day or more) confer higher fracture risk and warrant more frequent DXA monitoring. Inhaled steroids at standard doses generally do not confer significant bone risk; chronic high-dose forms do.

front: A patient has T-score -2.7 and a known vertebral compression fracture from a low-trauma mechanism. The diagnostic category per WHO 1994 is:
back:
Severe (established) osteoporosis

Per WHO 1994, severe (established) osteoporosis is defined by T-score -2.5 or below PLUS one or more fragility fractures. The patient meets both criteria. Severe osteoporosis indicates higher fracture risk and may justify anabolic therapy first per AACE/ASBMR-BHOF very-high-risk classifications.

front: Two postmenopausal women have lumbar T-scores of -2.5 and -2.6 respectively. Which is more meaningfully different from a clinical perspective?
back:
Neither is clinically meaningfully different from the other; the LSC at most facilities is approximately 0.3 to 0.5 T-score units

T-score precision is limited by the LSC. At most facilities the LSC at the spine is approximately 5 percent BMD, corresponding to roughly 0.3 to 0.5 T-score units. A 0.1 T-score difference between two patients (or between baseline and follow-up) is well below LSC and not clinically meaningful. WHO 1994 classifies T-score -2.5 vs -2.6 differently in classification, but the underlying measurement uncertainty means small differences should not drive treatment decisions.

front: L5 is not part of the standard PA lumbar DXA ROI primarily because:
back:
L5 is overlapped by the pelvis and sacrum on PA projection, making accurate isolation difficult, and standard reference data are based on L1-L4

L5 is excluded from standard PA lumbar DXA primarily because the pelvis and sacrum overlap it on the PA projection, making accurate vertebral isolation difficult. The standard reference databases (Hologic, GE Lunar) are based on L1-L4. Including L5 with overlying pelvic structures would systematically skew BMD values. T12 is also typically excluded due to overlying ribs.
source: ARRT 2022 BD spec; manufacturer reference databases

front: A new technologist's in vivo precision study at the lumbar spine consists of:
back:
Scanning 30 patients twice each (or 15 patients three times each), with patient repositioning between scans, then calculating RMS-SD and LSC

Per ISCD, an in vivo precision study uses 30 patients scanned twice each (or 15 patients three times each) with full patient repositioning between scans. Patients should be representative of the technologist's population (not all young, healthy volunteers). RMS-SD precision error is calculated, then LSC = 2.77 x RMS-SD. Each technologist establishes their own LSC; precision is technologist-dependent. Spine and hip have separate precision studies and LSCs.

front: Before performing a PA lumbar DXA on a 32-year-old woman of childbearing potential:
back:
Verify pregnancy status (last menstrual period, possibility of pregnancy); defer scan if pregnant or possibly pregnant

Per ALARA, pregnancy status must be assessed in women of childbearing potential before any X-ray exposure including DXA. Verify last menstrual period and any possibility of pregnancy. Defer DXA if pregnant or possibly pregnant; the procedure is not urgent and can be rescheduled. Although DXA dose is low (a few microSv), there is no clinical justification for fetal exposure that can be avoided.

front: WHO 1994 T-score osteoporosis criteria are validated for:
back:
Postmenopausal women and men 50 and older (per ISCD 2023, using young-adult reference)

WHO 1994 T-score osteoporosis criteria are validated for postmenopausal women and men age 50 and older. ISCD 2023 specifies the T-score uses the young-adult White female NHANES III reference for hip and femoral neck regardless of patient sex AND ethnicity (manufacturer's reference for spine). ISCD 2023 explicitly updated terminology from 'Caucasian' to 'White'. T-scores are NOT used for premenopausal women, men under 50, or children/adolescents; Z-scores are used for those populations.

front: The diagnostic standard for osteoporosis is:
back:
Central DXA at the lumbar spine and proximal femur (with 33% radius as alternative)

Central DXA at the lumbar spine and proximal femur is the diagnostic standard for osteoporosis. ISCD 2023 endorses the 33% radius as a diagnostic site when one of the central sites cannot be measured (or in primary hyperparathyroidism). Peripheral DXA (heel, finger, wrist) is a screening tool only and not used for diagnosis. QUS is also screening only. Plain radiography is insensitive for early bone loss.

front: Typical lumbar spine BMD precision (CV) at a well-controlled DXA facility is approximately:
back:
1.9 percent (corresponding to LSC ~5.3 percent)

Per ISCD acceptable precision standards, typical lumbar spine CV is approximately 1.9 percent at a well-controlled facility. The corresponding LSC at 95 percent confidence is approximately 5.3 percent (calculated as 2.77 x precision error). LSC depends on the specific facility's precision study and may differ. Total hip CV is typically ~1.8 percent (LSC ~5.0 percent); femoral neck CV is poorer (~2.5 percent, LSC ~6.9 percent).

front: After the DXA software completes automated analysis of the PA lumbar scan:
back:
Visually verify edge detection, ROI placement, and intervertebral line placement; manually adjust if needed before reporting

Automated DXA analysis is a starting point, not the final result. The technologist must visually verify: bone edge detection (particularly at lateral spine borders), ROI placement (centered on each vertebra), intervertebral line placement (at correct disc spaces), and labeling (T12 confirmed by ribs, iliac crest landmark). Manual adjustments are made when needed.

front: Standard Hologic Horizon DXA scan-table weight limit is approximately:
back:
450 lb / 204 kg

Standard Hologic Horizon DXA scan-table weight limit is approximately 450 lb / 204 kg (technical specification). GE Lunar Prodigy is approximately 350 lb / 159 kg; iDXA approximately 450 lb / 204 kg; older DPX approximately 300 lb / 136 kg. Patients exceeding the weight limit may require forearm-only DXA (the 33% radius is well off the scan table).
source: Hologic Site Prep Guide; GE Lunar enCORE manual

front: Severe scoliosis (Cobb angle > 15-20 degrees) on PA lumbar DXA:
back:
May significantly affect L1-L4 BMD due to vertebral rotation; consider hip or 33% radius for primary diagnosis

Mild scoliosis (Cobb angle < 15 degrees) typically has minimal BMD effect; moderate to severe scoliosis (> 15-20 degrees) significantly distorts PA BMD measurement due to vertebral rotation and projection geometry. ISCD 2023 does not specify a numeric Cobb-angle cutoff but advises that vertebrae 'clearly abnormal and non-assessable' or differing by > 1.0 T-score from neighbors be excluded. When most vertebrae are affected, default to hip or 33% radius.
source: ARRT 2022 BD spec; published scoliosis-DXA literature

front: The PA lumbar scan field is positioned to include:
back:
From upper L5 (or iliac crest) to lower T12 with at least one rib visible to confirm T12

The PA lumbar scan field extends from the upper portion of L5 (or includes the iliac crest as a landmark) to the lower portion of T12, with at least one rib visible to confirm T12 identification. Adequate margins above and below ensure all of L1-L4 are captured even with slight positioning variation. Verify field placement visually before starting.

front: A patient's baseline DXA was on Hologic; at follow-up, only a GE Lunar scanner is available. The technologist:
back:
Recognizes that direct comparison across manufacturers is not valid without cross-calibration; the new scan establishes a new baseline

BMD values are NOT directly comparable across different manufacturers (Hologic and GE Lunar use different reference databases and slightly different measurement approaches). Per ISCD, cross-calibration requires 30 patients scanned twice on each system within 60 days. Without cross-calibration, the new scan establishes a new baseline. Document this clearly so the interpreting physician understands the limitation.

front: On the patient history form, a 'fragility fracture' refers to:
back:
A fracture from low-trauma mechanism such as fall from standing height or less, or impact that would not normally cause a fracture in a healthy adult

A fragility fracture (also called low-trauma or osteoporotic fracture) results from a mechanism that would not normally cause a fracture in a healthy adult: fall from standing height or less, minor trauma, or no recognized trauma. Fragility fractures are clinically significant: per NBHA 2014, a low-trauma hip fracture alone is diagnostic of osteoporosis regardless of BMD; vertebral, proximal humerus, pelvis, wrist fragility fractures are diagnostic when BMD is in the osteopenia range.

front: Per ISCD 2023, BMD on a DXA report is conventionally reported to:
back:
3 decimal places (e.g., 0.987 g/cm^2)

Per ISCD 2023 Decimal Digits position, BMD is reported to 3 decimal places (e.g., 0.987 g/cm^2). BMC is reported to 2 decimal places. Area is reported to 2 decimal places. T-scores and Z-scores are reported to 1 decimal place (e.g., -2.5). Percent of reference is reported as a whole integer.

front: On PA lumbar DXA, the iliac crest may project over the lower portion of L4, causing:
back:
Possible falsely elevated BMD at L4 if the iliac crest projects into the L4 ROI; verify iliac crest position relative to vertebral labeling

On PA lumbar DXA, the iliac crest may project over the lower portion of L4, particularly when the iliac crest is high (close to L4 body rather than L4-L5 disc). This can cause falsely elevated BMD at L4 if pelvic bone projects into the L4 ROI. Verify the iliac crest position relative to vertebral labeling; if L4 is significantly affected, consider exclusion. Documenting the anatomic relationship supports consistent labeling at follow-up.

front: A patient reports sudden 2 cm height loss in the past year accompanied by acute back pain. The technologist should:
back:
Document the prospective height loss and back pain as VFA indications; per BHOF/clinical practice, prospective height loss > 2 cm during observation triggers VFA consideration

Sudden height loss (more than 2 cm during observation) accompanied by acute back pain strongly suggests new vertebral compression fracture. Per BHOF/clinical practice, prospective height loss > 2 cm during observation triggers VFA consideration. (Note: this 2 cm threshold is from BHOF/clinical practice, not verbatim ISCD 2023, which focuses on the 4 cm historical threshold.) Document the height loss and back pain. Order VFA per facility protocol; lateral spine X-ray for confirmation if needed.

front: A 38-year-old premenopausal woman has a PA lumbar Z-score of -2.4 with no fractures or known secondary cause. Per ISCD:
back:
Below the expected range for age (Z-score below -2.0); osteoporosis is NOT diagnosed by Z-score alone in premenopausal women

Per ISCD, Z-score below -2.0 in a premenopausal woman is termed 'below the expected range for age.' Osteoporosis is NOT diagnosed by Z-score alone in this population. The diagnosis of premenopausal osteoporosis requires additional findings: clinically significant fragility fracture history, OR identifiable secondary cause (glucocorticoid use, eating disorder, hypogonadism, celiac disease, etc.).

front: A patient has T-score -2.7 and a known vertebral compression fracture from a low-trauma mechanism. Per WHO 1994:
back:
Severe (established) osteoporosis

Per WHO 1994, severe (established) osteoporosis is defined by T-score -2.5 or below PLUS one or more fragility fractures. The patient meets both criteria. Severe osteoporosis indicates higher fracture risk and may justify anabolic therapy first per AACE/ASBMR-BHOF very-high-risk classifications.

front: Lateral spine DXA BMD may be useful in:
back:
Selective monitoring contexts where PA spine is unreliable due to extensive aortic calcification or facet osteoarthritis (per ISCD 2023, NOT for diagnosis)

Lateral spine DXA BMD may be useful in selective monitoring contexts where PA spine is unreliable due to extensive aortic calcification or facet osteoarthritis. The lateral projection isolates the vertebral body trabecular bone better than PA. However, per ISCD 2023, lateral spine BMD should NOT be used for diagnosis due to poorer precision and lack of robust reference data. PA spine remains the standard.

front: FRAX 10-year fracture probability uses BMD from:
back:
Femoral neck only (NOT spine, NOT total hip, NOT 33% radius)

FRAX is validated only with femoral neck BMD as the BMD input. Spine BMD, total hip BMD, and 33% radius BMD are NOT used in FRAX calculation. This means a patient with osteoporosis defined by spine BMD alone (uncommon, given hip is also typically measured) cannot have FRAX directly calculated unless femoral neck BMD is also available. The interpreting physician integrates spine results with clinical context.

front: The diagnostic standard for osteoporosis is:
back:
Central DXA at lumbar spine and proximal femur (with 33% radius as alternative)

Central DXA at the lumbar spine and proximal femur is the diagnostic standard for osteoporosis. ISCD 2023 endorses the 33% radius as a diagnostic site when one of the central sites cannot be measured (or in primary hyperparathyroidism). Peripheral DXA (heel, finger, wrist) is a screening tool only and not used for diagnosis. QUS is also screening only. Plain radiography is insensitive for early bone loss.

front: The femoral neck on a hip DXA scan is the region:
back:
Between the femoral head and the trochanteric region

The femoral neck is the narrow region of the proximal femur between the femoral head (which articulates with the acetabulum) and the trochanteric region (greater and lesser trochanters). It is one of the standard hip DXA ROIs along with the total hip, trochanter, intertrochanteric, and Ward's region. Per ISCD 2023, the femoral neck and total hip are the diagnostic ROIs; trochanter and Ward's are not used for diagnosis.

front: Per ISCD 2023, the diagnostic ROIs for hip DXA are:
back:
Total hip and femoral neck only

Per ISCD 2023, the diagnostic hip ROIs are total hip and femoral neck. The trochanter, intertrochanteric region, and Ward's area are reported on the scan but are NOT used for diagnosis. The lowest of total hip, femoral neck, and lumbar spine T-scores is used for the WHO classification (with 33% radius as alternative when one of the central sites cannot be measured).

front: For initial baseline hip DXA, the side typically scanned is:
back:
Either hip per facility protocol; some facilities scan the left by convention; bilateral hip is acceptable per ISCD 2023

For initial baseline hip DXA, either hip can be scanned per facility protocol; many facilities scan the left hip by convention. Per ISCD 2023 (Tanner position), bilateral hip DXA is also acceptable; the diagnostic T-score uses the lowest of the 4 ROIs (left femoral neck, left total hip, right femoral neck, right total hip) when bilateral is performed. Mean total hip BMD across both sides is used for monitoring response to therapy.

front: If the planned hip cannot be scanned (prior fracture, hardware, severe pain), the technologist should:
back:
Scan the contralateral hip and document the reason

If the planned hip cannot be scanned (prior fracture, surgical hardware including hip replacement, severe pain preventing positioning, severe contracture), scan the contralateral hip and document the reason. If both hips are not scannable, the spine and 33% radius become the diagnostic sites. Document any deviations from protocol clearly so follow-up scans can match.

front: When the foot is not adequately internally rotated for hip DXA, the femoral neck appears:
back:
Foreshortened, with the lesser trochanter prominent and falsely lowered apparent BMD

Inadequate internal rotation makes the femoral neck appear foreshortened on the projected image, with the lesser trochanter becoming prominent (it should be barely visible or hidden behind the femoral shaft when properly positioned). The foreshortened neck has a smaller projected area and appears to have falsely lowered BMD (lower BMC/area ratio). Reproducible positioning is essential for serial monitoring.

front: For hip DXA, the femoral shaft should be:
back:
Straight and parallel to the long axis of the scan table (or per manufacturer recommendation)

For hip DXA, the femoral shaft should be straight and parallel to the long axis of the scan table per manufacturer recommendation. The hip should be slightly abducted (15 to 25 degrees from midline per Hologic protocol). The foot is internally rotated 15 to 25 degrees against a positioning device. Lateral pelvic tilt should be avoided; the patient is supine and centered.

front: A patient has a left total hip replacement. For hip DXA:
back:
Scan the right (contralateral) hip and document the left-side replacement

A hip replacement (total or hemiarthroplasty) on one side makes that hip unscannable for BMD; the metal implant has very high attenuation that distorts the dual-energy algorithm. Scan the contralateral (unaffected) hip and document the side with the replacement. If both hips have implants, the spine and 33% radius are the diagnostic sites. Document the deviation clearly.

front: A standard hip DXA report includes BMD values for:
back:
Femoral neck, total hip, trochanter, intertrochanteric, and Ward's regions (with diagnostic interpretation based on femoral neck and total hip per ISCD 2023)

A standard hip DXA report includes BMD for femoral neck, total hip, trochanter, intertrochanteric, and Ward's regions, plus T-scores and Z-scores. Per ISCD 2023, only femoral neck and total hip are used for WHO diagnostic classification; trochanter and Ward's are reported but not used for diagnosis. Total hip is preferred for monitoring response to therapy because it has the best precision (lowest CV).

front: For monitoring response to antiresorptive therapy at the hip, the preferred ROI is:
back:
Total hip (best precision among hip ROIs, lowest LSC)

For monitoring response to therapy, total hip is preferred among hip ROIs because it has the best precision (typical CV ~1.8 percent vs ~2.5 percent for femoral neck). Better precision means smaller LSC (~5.0 percent vs ~6.9 percent), so smaller real BMD changes can be detected as statistically significant. The lumbar spine is typically more sensitive to therapy effects (larger absolute change) than the total hip. Per ISCD 2023, both spine and total hip are valid monitoring sites.

front: For diagnosis from a hip DXA, the relevant T-score is:
back:
The lowest of the femoral neck T-score or total hip T-score

Per ISCD 2023, the diagnostic hip T-score is the lowest of the femoral neck and total hip. The trochanter, intertrochanteric, and Ward's region are NOT used for diagnosis. WHO classification (-2.5 or below = osteoporosis) is then applied. The overall diagnostic T-score combines the lowest of spine, total hip, and femoral neck (with 33% radius as alternative).

front: Per ISCD 2023 (Tanner position), when bilateral hip DXA is performed, diagnosis uses:
back:
The lowest of the 4 ROIs (left femoral neck, left total hip, right femoral neck, right total hip)

Per ISCD 2023 (Tanner et al. position), when bilateral hip DXA is performed, the diagnostic T-score is the lowest of the 4 ROIs: left femoral neck, left total hip, right femoral neck, right total hip. This minimizes false-negative diagnoses when one side is preserved despite osteoporosis at the other. For monitoring response to therapy, the mean total hip BMD across both sides is used (better precision than single-side measurement).

front: Per ISCD 2023, when bilateral hip DXA is used for monitoring response to therapy, the relevant value is:
back:
The mean total hip BMD across both sides (better precision)

Per ISCD 2023, bilateral hip DXA for monitoring uses the mean total hip BMD across both sides because averaging improves precision (lower combined CV). For diagnosis, the lowest of 4 ROIs is used. This is a deliberate distinction: diagnosis seeks to capture worst-affected site; monitoring seeks best precision for change detection. Document baseline approach so follow-up scans use the same method.

front: For follow-up hip DXA, the technologist should:
back:
Reproduce the same side, same internal rotation, same ROI placement (using compare-prior tool); document any deviation

Follow-up hip DXA must reproduce baseline as closely as possible: same side (left or right or bilateral), same internal rotation, same ROI placement (using compare-prior tool to copy baseline ROI to the follow-up scan). Deviation from baseline introduces artifactual BMD differences. If positioning cannot be reproduced (new contracture, fracture), document the deviation; the interpreting physician can adjust interpretation accordingly.
source: ISCD 2023 Follow-up BMD; ARRT 2022 BD spec

front: Per ISCD 2023, the T-score reference database for hip DXA is:
back:
The young-adult Caucasian female NHANES III reference for femoral neck and total hip, regardless of patient sex

Per ISCD 2023, the T-score for hip (femoral neck and total hip) uses the uniform young-adult Caucasian female NHANES III reference database, regardless of the patient's sex. This standardizes T-scores across manufacturers and populations. The spine T-score uses the manufacturer's reference. Z-scores use age-, sex-, and (when available) ethnicity-matched populations. The reason: NHANES III provides robust reference data; using a uniform reference avoids systematic bias.

front: A 70-year-old woman has total hip T-score of -2.6 and femoral neck T-score of -2.8. Per WHO 1994:
back:
Osteoporosis

Per WHO 1994, T-score -2.5 or below = osteoporosis. The patient has T-scores of -2.6 (total hip) and -2.8 (femoral neck), both in the osteoporosis range. The diagnostic T-score is the lowest of the relevant ROIs, so -2.8 (femoral neck) is used. No fragility fracture mentioned, so 'osteoporosis' rather than 'severe osteoporosis.' Diagnosis is osteoporosis.

front: Per NBHA 2014, a low-trauma hip fracture in a postmenopausal woman:
back:
Is diagnostic of osteoporosis with or without BMD measurement

Per NBHA 2014 (Siris et al.), a low-trauma hip fracture in a postmenopausal woman is diagnostic of osteoporosis with or without BMD measurement. This is a clinical-diagnostic criterion that supplements the WHO BMD-based criteria. Vertebral, proximal humerus, and pelvis fragility fractures are diagnostic when BMD is in the osteopenia range. The hip fracture criterion reflects the high mortality and morbidity associated with hip fragility fracture.

front: FRAX 10-year fracture probability incorporates BMD from:
back:
Femoral neck only (NOT spine, NOT total hip)

FRAX is validated only with femoral neck BMD as the BMD input. Spine BMD, total hip BMD, and other site BMDs are not used in FRAX calculation. This limits FRAX use when femoral neck BMD is unavailable (hardware, replacement). Other limitations: validated ages 40-90 only; only in untreated patients; does not account for falls history; does not include lumbar spine BMD; does not adjust for dose-response of glucocorticoids.

front: Typical effective dose from a hip DXA scan is approximately:
back:
Approximately 1 to 10 microSv (less than 1 day of natural background)

Typical effective dose from a hip DXA scan is approximately 1 to 10 microSv depending on scan mode and patient size. This is far below 1 mSv (the dose from a chest CT) and less than 1 day of natural background radiation (~3.1 mSv per year per NCRP 160 = ~8.5 microSv per day). DXA's very low dose makes it well-suited for serial monitoring. Always apply ALARA: pregnancy verification, appropriate scan mode, no unnecessary repeats.

front: A patient weighs 460 lb (208 kg) and exceeds the Hologic Horizon scan-table weight limit. The technologist should:
back:
Defer central DXA and consider forearm DXA (33% radius), which can be performed with the patient seated and the arm extended off the scan table

When a patient exceeds the scanner table weight limit (Hologic Horizon ~450 lb / 204 kg), central DXA cannot be performed safely. The 33% radius can be measured by extending the patient's arm onto the scan table while the patient is seated; the rest of the body is off the table. Per ISCD 2023, the 33% radius is a valid diagnostic site when central sites are unavailable, including for body habitus exceeding scanner capacity.

front: Per WHO 1994 applied to hip DXA, the diagnostic T-score thresholds are:
back:
-1.0 or above normal, -1.0 to -2.5 osteopenia, -2.5 or below osteoporosis

WHO 1994 thresholds applied to hip DXA: T-score -1.0 or above = normal; -1.0 to -2.5 = low bone mass (osteopenia); -2.5 or below = osteoporosis; -2.5 or below + fragility fracture = severe (established) osteoporosis. Same thresholds as for the spine. Use the lowest of femoral neck and total hip per ISCD 2023.

front: For monitoring serial hip BMD, the technologist must:
back:
Use the same side scanned at baseline (left vs right vs bilateral); document the side clearly

Serial hip BMD monitoring requires the same side scanned at baseline (left, right, or bilateral). Switching sides between baseline and follow-up introduces artifactual BMD differences that may be larger than the LSC. Document the side clearly on the report. If the originally scanned side becomes unscannable (new fracture, hardware), document the change; the new measurement establishes a new baseline.

front: Per ISCD acceptable precision standards, typical total hip BMD CV is approximately:
back:
Approximately 1.8 percent (corresponding to LSC ~5.0 percent)

Per ISCD acceptable precision standards, typical total hip CV is approximately 1.8 percent (LSC ~5.0 percent at 95 percent confidence). Lumbar spine CV is typically ~1.9 percent (LSC ~5.3 percent). Femoral neck CV is poorer (~2.5 percent, LSC ~6.9 percent). Each technologist must establish their own LSC by performing an in vivo precision study at each site. Total hip is preferred for monitoring because of best precision.

front: For follow-up hip DXA, the compare-prior (or copy-prior) tool:
back:
Copies the baseline ROI placement to the follow-up scan, ensuring identical analysis and reducing artifactual BMD differences

The compare-prior (or copy-prior) tool in DXA software copies the baseline ROI placement to the follow-up scan. This ensures identical ROI placement, which is essential for valid serial monitoring. Without this tool, slight differences in auto-placement can introduce artifactual BMD differences. The technologist verifies the copied ROI fits the follow-up image; manual adjustment may be needed if patient positioning differs significantly.

front: A patient has hip DXA T-scores: total hip -1.7, femoral neck -2.2. The diagnosis per WHO/ISCD 2023:
back:
Low bone mass (osteopenia) - the lower of the two T-scores (-2.2) is in the osteopenia range (-1.0 to -2.5)

Per ISCD 2023, the diagnostic hip T-score is the lower of total hip and femoral neck. T-score -2.2 (femoral neck) is in the osteopenia range (-1.0 to -2.5 per WHO 1994). Diagnosis: low bone mass (osteopenia). FRAX should be calculated using the femoral neck BMD to estimate 10-year fracture probability and guide treatment. The total hip T-score of -1.7 alone is also osteopenia.

front: On the patient history form, chronic glucocorticoid use is a particular hip-fracture risk factor because:
back:
Glucocorticoids preferentially affect trabecular bone first and accelerate cortical bone loss, increasing hip fracture risk; FRAX includes glucocorticoid use as an input

Chronic glucocorticoid use (prednisone-equivalent 5 mg/day or more for 3 months or more) preferentially affects trabecular bone first and accelerates cortical bone loss, increasing fracture risk at all sites including the hip. FRAX includes glucocorticoid use as a binary risk factor input. Per ACR Glucocorticoid-Induced Osteoporosis guidelines, patients on chronic glucocorticoids should be considered for prophylactic bone-protective therapy (oral bisphosphonate first-line in most cases).
source: ARRT 2022 BD spec; FRAX inputs; ACR GIO

front: On a DXA report, percent of reference (or percent of young adult) is:
back:
An expression of the patient's BMD as a percentage of the reference mean BMD; e.g., 80% means BMD is 80% of the reference mean

Percent of reference (or percent of young adult, percent of mean) expresses the patient's BMD as a percentage of the reference mean BMD. For example, 80% means the patient's BMD is 80% of the reference mean. T-score gives the standardized difference (in standard deviations) which is more clinically standard. Both can be reported. Per ISCD 2023, percent of reference is reported as a whole integer.

front: The trochanter ROI on hip DXA:
back:
Is reported but is NOT used for WHO diagnostic classification per ISCD 2023; only femoral neck and total hip are used for diagnosis

The trochanter ROI is reported on hip DXA but is NOT used for WHO diagnostic classification per ISCD 2023. Only femoral neck and total hip are diagnostic ROIs. The trochanter, intertrochanteric, and Ward's region are reported but not used for diagnosis. The trochanter contains a mix of cortical and trabecular bone.

front: The Ward's region (Ward's triangle) on hip DXA:
back:
Is a small region of low BMD between the femoral neck and trochanteric region; reported but NOT used for WHO diagnostic classification per ISCD 2023

The Ward's region (also called Ward's triangle) is a small region of relatively low BMD between the femoral neck and trochanteric region. It is reported on hip DXA but is NOT used for WHO diagnostic classification per ISCD 2023. Ward's region has high precision error (because it is small and ROI placement is variable) and is unreliable for monitoring. Only femoral neck and total hip are diagnostic; total hip is preferred for monitoring.

front: Per NBHA 2014, a low-trauma hip fracture in any postmenopausal woman:
back:
Is independently diagnostic of osteoporosis (with or without BMD measurement)

Per NBHA 2014 (Siris et al. Osteoporos Int 2014;25:1439), a low-trauma hip fracture in any postmenopausal woman is independently diagnostic of osteoporosis with or without BMD measurement. A DXA T-score below -2.5 also diagnoses osteoporosis (WHO 1994), but the hip fracture criterion stands alone. Low-trauma vertebral, proximal humerus, and pelvis fragility fractures are diagnostic only when BMD is in the osteopenia range (T-score -1.0 to -2.5).

front: A patient has bilateral total hip replacements. The technologist:
back:
Cannot perform diagnostic hip DXA; uses lumbar spine and 33% radius for diagnosis

Bilateral hip replacements make hip DXA unscannable. Per ISCD 2023, when both hips have implants, the lumbar spine and 33% radius are used for diagnosis (the 33% radius is the alternative when one of the central sites is unavailable). The interpreting physician applies WHO classification to the lowest available T-score among spine and 33% radius. Document the bilateral implants on the report.

front: Per ISCD 2023, the 33% radius can be used as a diagnostic site:
back:
When central sites (spine and hip) cannot be measured or interpreted, OR in specific clinical contexts (primary hyperparathyroidism, exceeding scan-table weight limit)

Per ISCD 2023, the 33% radius is a valid diagnostic site when the central sites (spine and hip) cannot be measured or interpreted, OR in specific clinical contexts: primary hyperparathyroidism (cortical bone loss preferentially affects 33% radius), patients exceeding scan-table weight limit, bilateral hip implants, severe spine artifacts. The 33% radius is the non-dominant arm by default. WHO classification can be applied at the 33% radius.

front: On the DXA patient history, a prior low-trauma hip fracture is:
back:
Highly significant; per NBHA 2014 it is independently diagnostic of osteoporosis and predicts future fracture risk

A prior low-trauma hip fracture is highly significant. Per NBHA 2014, it is independently diagnostic of osteoporosis with or without BMD measurement. It also predicts substantially elevated future fracture risk (recurrent hip fracture, vertebral fracture, mortality). FRAX includes prior fracture as a binary risk factor input. Document the fracture date, mechanism (low-trauma vs trauma), and any treatment history. The patient is automatically a high-risk osteoporosis patient.

front: Compared to a chest X-ray (~20-50 microSv), a hip DXA scan typically delivers:
back:
Substantially less dose (1 to 10 microSv)

Hip DXA delivers approximately 1 to 10 microSv, substantially less than a standard chest X-ray (approximately 20-50 microSv per current NCRP 184 / IAEA references). DXA's very low dose allows it to be used safely for serial monitoring. Apply ALARA: confirm pregnancy, use appropriate scan mode, no unnecessary repeats. Communicate the very low dose to patients in plain terms (e.g., 'less than one day of natural background radiation').

front: On the analyzed hip DXA image, the technologist sees a prominent lesser trochanter. This indicates:
back:
Inadequate internal rotation of the foot/hip; the femoral neck appears foreshortened with falsely lowered BMD

A prominent lesser trochanter on the projected hip DXA image indicates inadequate internal rotation. With proper internal rotation (15 to 25 degrees), the lesser trochanter should be barely visible or hidden behind the femoral shaft. A prominent lesser trochanter means the foot was not rotated enough, the femoral neck is foreshortened, and BMD appears falsely lowered. Reposition and rescan if practical; otherwise document the deviation and reproduce at follow-up.

front: After auto-analysis of a hip DXA, the technologist should:
back:
Visually verify ROI placement (femoral neck, total hip), edge detection, ischium-femur gap, and trochanter alignment; manually adjust if needed

Automated hip DXA analysis is a starting point. The technologist visually verifies: ROI placement (femoral neck and total hip well-positioned), bone edge detection (clear borders), ischium-to-femur spacing adequate, lesser trochanter not prominent (good rotation). Manual ROI adjustment is made when needed. The technologist's quality verification is essential for accurate BMD reporting; software auto-analysis can place ROIs poorly in patients with anatomic variants.

front: Per ISCD 2023, hip BMD on a DXA report is reported to:
back:
3 decimal places (e.g., 0.832 g/cm^2)

Per ISCD 2023 Decimal Digits position, hip BMD is reported to 3 decimal places (e.g., 0.832 g/cm^2). BMC is reported to 2 decimal places. Area is reported to 2 decimal places. T-scores and Z-scores are reported to 1 decimal place (e.g., -2.5). Percent of reference is reported as a whole integer. Standardizing decimal places improves report consistency.

front: For valid serial monitoring of hip BMD:
back:
ROI placement on follow-up scans must reproduce baseline; use the compare-prior tool to copy baseline ROI to the follow-up scan

For valid serial monitoring of hip BMD, ROI placement on follow-up scans must reproduce baseline. Use the compare-prior tool (or copy-prior tool) in the manufacturer software to copy the baseline ROI to the follow-up scan. The technologist verifies the copied ROI fits the new image; manual adjustment may be needed if patient positioning differs significantly from baseline. Inconsistent ROI placement creates artifactual BMD differences.

front: After auto-analysis of a hip DXA, the technologist should:
back:
Visually verify ROI placement (femoral neck, total hip), edge detection, ischium-femur gap, and trochanter alignment; manually adjust if needed

Automated hip DXA analysis is a starting point. The technologist visually verifies: ROI placement (femoral neck and total hip well-positioned), bone edge detection (clear borders), ischium-to-femur spacing adequate, lesser trochanter not prominent (good rotation). Manual ROI adjustment is made when needed. The technologist's quality verification is essential for accurate BMD reporting.

front: A standard hip DXA report includes BMD values for:
back:
Femoral neck, total hip, trochanter, intertrochanteric, and Ward's regions (with diagnostic interpretation based on femoral neck and total hip per ISCD 2023)

A standard hip DXA report includes BMD for femoral neck, total hip, trochanter, intertrochanteric, and Ward's regions, plus T-scores and Z-scores. Per ISCD 2023, only femoral neck and total hip are used for WHO diagnostic classification; trochanter and Ward's are reported but not used for diagnosis. Total hip is preferred for monitoring response to therapy because it has the best precision (lowest CV).

front: Per ISCD 2023, the T-score reference database for hip DXA is:
back:
The young-adult Caucasian female NHANES III reference for femoral neck and total hip, regardless of patient sex

Per ISCD 2023, the T-score for hip (femoral neck and total hip) uses the uniform young-adult Caucasian female NHANES III reference database, regardless of the patient's sex. This standardizes T-scores across manufacturers and populations. The spine T-score uses the manufacturer's reference. Z-scores use age-, sex-, and (when available) ethnicity-matched populations.

front: A 70-year-old woman has total hip T-score of -2.6 and femoral neck T-score of -2.8. Per WHO 1994:
back:
Osteoporosis

Per WHO 1994, T-score -2.5 or below = osteoporosis. The patient has T-scores of -2.6 (total hip) and -2.8 (femoral neck), both in the osteoporosis range. The diagnostic T-score is the lowest of the relevant ROIs, so -2.8 (femoral neck) is used. No fragility fracture mentioned, so 'osteoporosis' rather than 'severe osteoporosis.'

front: Per NBHA 2014, a low-trauma hip fracture in a postmenopausal woman:
back:
Is diagnostic of osteoporosis with or without BMD measurement

Per NBHA 2014 (Siris ES et al. Osteoporos Int 2014;25:1439-1443), a low-trauma hip fracture in a postmenopausal woman is diagnostic of osteoporosis with or without BMD measurement. This is a clinical-diagnostic criterion that supplements the WHO BMD-based criteria. Fragility fractures of the vertebrae, proximal humerus, pelvis, and some distal forearm (wrist) sites are diagnostic when BMD is in the osteopenia range (T-score -1.0 to -2.5). NBHA also recognizes a FRAX-based diagnosis pathway when 10-year hip fracture probability is >=3% or major osteoporotic fracture probability is >=20% with osteopenia BMD. The hip fracture criterion reflects the high mortality and morbidity associated with hip fragility fracture.

front: FRAX 10-year fracture probability incorporates BMD from:
back:
Femoral neck only (NOT spine, NOT total hip)

FRAX is validated only with femoral neck BMD as the BMD input. Spine BMD, total hip BMD, and other site BMDs are not used in FRAX calculation. This limits FRAX use when femoral neck BMD is unavailable (hardware, replacement). Other limitations: validated ages 40-90 only; only in untreated patients; does not account for falls history; does not include lumbar spine BMD; does not adjust for dose-response of glucocorticoids.

front: A patient weighs 460 lb (208 kg) and exceeds the Hologic Horizon scan-table weight limit. The technologist should:
back:
Defer central DXA and consider forearm DXA (33% radius); the patient can be seated with the arm extended onto the scan table

When a patient exceeds the scanner table weight limit (Hologic Horizon ~450 lb / 204 kg), central DXA cannot be performed safely. The 33% radius can be measured by extending the patient's arm onto the scan table while the patient is seated; the rest of the body is off the table. Per ISCD 2023, the 33% radius is a valid diagnostic site when central sites are unavailable, including for body habitus exceeding scanner capacity.

front: For follow-up hip DXA, the compare-prior tool:
back:
Copies the baseline ROI placement to the follow-up scan, ensuring identical analysis and reducing artifactual BMD differences

The compare-prior (or copy-prior) tool in DXA software copies the baseline ROI placement to the follow-up scan. This ensures identical ROI placement, which is essential for valid serial monitoring. Without this tool, slight differences in auto-placement can introduce artifactual BMD differences. The technologist verifies the copied ROI fits the follow-up image; manual adjustment may be needed if patient positioning differs significantly.

front: A patient has hip DXA T-scores: total hip -1.7, femoral neck -2.2. Per WHO 1994:
back:
Low bone mass (osteopenia) - the lower of the two T-scores (-2.2) is in the osteopenia range

Per ISCD 2023, the diagnostic hip T-score is the lower of total hip and femoral neck. T-score -2.2 (femoral neck) is in the osteopenia range (-1.0 to -2.5 per WHO 1994). Diagnosis: low bone mass (osteopenia). FRAX should be calculated using the femoral neck BMD to estimate 10-year fracture probability and guide treatment.

front: On the patient history form, chronic glucocorticoid use is a particular hip-fracture risk factor because:
back:
Glucocorticoids preferentially affect trabecular bone first and accelerate cortical bone loss, increasing hip fracture risk; FRAX includes glucocorticoid use as an input

Chronic glucocorticoid use (prednisone-equivalent 5 mg/day or more for 3 months or more) preferentially affects trabecular bone first and accelerates cortical bone loss, increasing fracture risk at all sites including the hip. FRAX includes glucocorticoid use as a binary risk factor input. Per ACR Glucocorticoid-Induced Osteoporosis guidelines, patients on chronic glucocorticoids should be considered for prophylactic bone-protective therapy.

front: On a DXA report, percent of reference (or percent of young adult) is:
back:
An expression of the patient's BMD as a percentage of the reference mean BMD

Percent of reference (or percent of young adult, percent of mean) expresses the patient's BMD as a percentage of the reference mean BMD. For example, 80% means the patient's BMD is 80% of the reference mean. T-score gives the standardized difference (in standard deviations) which is more clinically standard. Per ISCD 2023, percent of reference is reported as a whole integer.

front: The trochanter ROI on hip DXA:
back:
Is reported but is NOT used for WHO diagnostic classification per ISCD 2023; only femoral neck and total hip are used for diagnosis

The trochanter ROI is reported on hip DXA but is NOT used for WHO diagnostic classification per ISCD 2023. Only femoral neck and total hip are diagnostic ROIs. The trochanter, intertrochanteric, and Ward's region are reported but not used for diagnosis. The trochanter contains a mix of cortical and trabecular bone.

front: The Ward's region (Ward's triangle) on hip DXA:
back:
Is a small region of low BMD between the femoral neck and trochanteric region; reported but NOT used for WHO diagnostic classification

The Ward's region (also called Ward's triangle) is a small region of relatively low BMD between the femoral neck and trochanteric region. It is reported on hip DXA but is NOT used for WHO diagnostic classification per ISCD 2023. Ward's region has high precision error (because it is small and ROI placement is variable) and is unreliable for monitoring. Only femoral neck and total hip are diagnostic; total hip is preferred for monitoring.

front: On the DXA patient history, a prior low-trauma hip fracture is:
back:
Highly significant; per NBHA 2014 it is independently diagnostic of osteoporosis and predicts future fracture risk

A prior low-trauma hip fracture is highly significant. Per NBHA 2014, it is independently diagnostic of osteoporosis with or without BMD measurement. It also predicts substantially elevated future fracture risk (recurrent hip fracture, vertebral fracture, mortality). FRAX includes prior fracture as a binary risk factor input. Document the fracture date, mechanism (low-trauma vs trauma), and any treatment history. The patient is automatically a high-risk osteoporosis patient.

front: Per ISCD 2023, the diagnostic forearm DXA ROI is:
back:
33% (one-third) radius of the non-dominant forearm

Per ISCD 2023, the 33% (one-third) radius of the non-dominant forearm is the diagnostic ROI. WHO classification can be applied at the 33% radius (along with femoral neck, total hip, and lumbar spine). Other forearm ROIs (ultradistal, mid-distal, total radius) are reported but NOT used for diagnosis. The 33% radius is approximately 95 percent cortical bone and is particularly informative in primary hyperparathyroidism, which preferentially affects cortical bone.

front: Per ISCD 2023, forearm DXA is indicated when:
back:
Hip and/or spine cannot be measured or interpreted, hyperparathyroidism (cortical bone loss preferentially affects 33% radius), or patient exceeds scanner table weight limit

Per ISCD 2023, forearm DXA (specifically 33% radius) is indicated when: (1) hip and/or spine cannot be measured or interpreted (hardware, severe artifact, body habitus), (2) primary hyperparathyroidism (PHPT preferentially resorbs cortical bone; the 33% radius is approximately 95% cortical), (3) patient exceeds scanner table weight limit (forearm DXA can be performed with patient seated, arm extended onto table, body off table). Standard practice is hip and spine first; forearm is the alternative central diagnostic site.

front: In primary hyperparathyroidism, the 33% radius is the recommended forearm site because:
back:
Primary hyperparathyroidism preferentially resorbs cortical bone, and the 33% radius is approximately 95 percent cortical, so this site shows the most pronounced BMD reduction

In primary hyperparathyroidism (PHPT), chronic continuous PTH excess preferentially activates osteoclast resorption of cortical bone. The 33% radius is approximately 95 percent cortical and shows the most pronounced BMD reduction (worst BMD of the typical sites). The lumbar spine is relatively preserved (mostly trabecular). After parathyroidectomy, BMD recovers largest and earliest at the lumbar spine and femoral neck; the 33% radius recovers slowly and often incompletely.
source: Bilezikian 2022 Fifth Workshop on PHPT; Silverberg 1995

front: WHO 1994 T-score classification can be applied at:
back:
Femoral neck, total hip, lumbar spine, and 33% radius (the WHO-validated and ISCD-endorsed sites)

WHO 1994 T-score classification can be applied at the femoral neck, total hip, lumbar spine, and 33% radius. The 33% radius is recognized as a valid diagnostic site by ISCD 2023, particularly when central sites (spine and hip) cannot be measured, in primary hyperparathyroidism, or when scanner table weight limits are exceeded. Peripheral DXA sites (heel, finger, wrist by peripheral devices) are NOT WHO-validated for diagnosis and are screening tools only.

front: Forearm DXA reports typically include BMD for:
back:
Ultradistal, mid-distal, 33% (one-third), and total radius (with 33% being the diagnostic site per ISCD 2023)

Forearm DXA reports typically include BMD for ultradistal radius, mid-distal radius, 33% (one-third) radius, and total radius. Per ISCD 2023, only the 33% radius is used for diagnostic WHO classification; the others are reported but not diagnostic. The 33% radius is approximately 95 percent cortical; the ultradistal radius is mostly trabecular. Document each ROI's BMD, T-score, and Z-score per facility report standard.

front: For Hologic forearm DXA, the patient can be positioned:
back:
Either seated (arm extended onto the scan table) or supine (arm at side); the forearm is positioned with the radius and ulna parallel to the scan axis in neutral pronation

For Hologic forearm DXA, the patient can be positioned either seated (arm extended onto the scan table) or supine (arm at side, depending on protocol). The forearm is positioned with the radius and ulna parallel to the scan axis in neutral pronation. Positioning aids hold the forearm in place. The seated position is useful for patients exceeding the scan-table weight limit because the body is off the table. Document the position used so follow-up scans can match.

front: Per the ARRT 2022 BD spec, significant adjacent structures for forearm DXA include:
back:
Carpal bones and soft tissue

Per the ARRT 2022 BD spec for forearm scan acquisition, significant adjacent structures include the carpal bones (which should be excluded from the radius ROIs) and surrounding soft tissue. The technologist verifies that the 33% radius ROI is properly positioned on the radius (not extending into soft tissue or carpal bones), with adequate air on the ulnar side. Manual ROI adjustment may be needed when auto-placement is suboptimal.

front: The 33% (one-third) radius is approximately:
back:
Approximately 95 percent cortical bone (the cortex of the radial diaphysis is dense; very little trabecular bone)

The 33% (one-third) radius is approximately 95 percent cortical bone (the cortex of the radial diaphysis is dense; very little trabecular bone). This makes the 33% radius the most cortical-rich DXA site and the recommended ROI for primary hyperparathyroidism evaluation. The ultradistal radius is mostly trabecular (approximately 75 percent trabecular); the mid-distal radius is mixed. Vertebral bodies are mostly trabecular; femoral neck is mostly cortical.

front: For valid serial 33% radius BMD comparison, the technologist must:
back:
Reproduce the same patient position (seated or supine), same arm (non-dominant unless otherwise documented), same forearm length entry, same ROI placement (using compare-prior tool)

Valid serial 33% radius BMD monitoring requires reproducing the same patient position (seated vs supine per facility protocol), same arm (non-dominant unless otherwise documented), same forearm length entry (because the 33% point is calculated from forearm length), and same ROI placement using the compare-prior tool. Documenting the side and position on the report is essential. Switching arms or positions introduces artifactual BMD differences.

front: In a patient with very low BMD, forearm DXA edge detection is:
back:
Generally robust because the cortical-rich 33% radius has clear bone-soft tissue contrast even at low BMD

In a patient with very low BMD, forearm DXA edge detection is generally robust at the cortical-rich 33% radius because the cortex provides clear bone-soft tissue contrast even when BMD is low. Spine and hip edge detection can be more challenging in severe osteoporosis (less contrast). However, the ultradistal radius (mostly trabecular) may have poorer edge detection in very low BMD. Verify edge detection visually and adjust manually if needed.

front: A patient weighing 480 lb (218 kg) exceeds the central DXA scan-table weight limit. The 33% radius is the diagnostic alternative because:
back:
Forearm DXA can be performed with the patient seated and the arm extended onto the scan table; the body weight is off the table and not on the scan-table mechanism

For patients exceeding the central DXA scan-table weight limit (Hologic Horizon ~450 lb / 204 kg), the 33% radius can be measured with the patient seated and only the arm extended onto the scan table. The body weight is off the table mechanism, so the table can support the much smaller weight of the arm. Per ISCD 2023, forearm DXA is the alternative diagnostic site in this scenario. Document the indication.

front: Per ISCD 2023, an in vivo precision study at the 33% radius:
back:
Should be performed separately for each site (spine, hip, forearm); each site has its own LSC; precision is technologist-dependent

Per ISCD 2023, in vivo precision studies must be performed separately for each anatomic site (spine, hip, forearm). Each site has its own LSC. Precision is technologist-dependent (each technologist must establish their own LSC). The forearm precision study uses 30 patients scanned twice (or 15 patients three times) with full repositioning between scans. Forearm precision is typically excellent due to the cortical-rich 33% radius (CV often well under 2 percent).

front: After parathyroidectomy for primary hyperparathyroidism, BMD recovery is typically:
back:
Largest and earliest at the lumbar spine and femoral neck; the 33% radius recovers slowly and often incompletely

After parathyroidectomy for primary hyperparathyroidism (Silverberg 1995; Bilezikian 2022 Fifth Workshop), BMD recovery is largest and earliest at the lumbar spine and femoral neck (typically 8 to 12 percent gain over 1 to 5 years). The 33% radius (highly cortical) recovers slowly and often incompletely; cortical bone has slower remodeling rates. The recovery pattern reflects underlying bone biology: trabecular sites with high turnover recover faster than cortical-dominant sites.

front: For 33% radius T-score calculation, the reference database is:
back:
Hologic's NHANES-aligned forearm reference or manufacturer-specific reference; 33% radius does not have a NHANES III reference equivalent to hip and femoral neck

The 33% radius does not have a NHANES III reference database equivalent to the hip/femoral neck. Hologic uses a NHANES-aligned forearm reference; GE Lunar uses manufacturer-specific data. The lack of a uniform reference can cause T-score discrepancies between manufacturers. Document the manufacturer and software version used. ISCD 2023 acknowledges this limitation but endorses 33% radius as a valid diagnostic site when central sites are unavailable.

front: A patient has a healed wrist fragility fracture from 5 years ago. For forearm DXA:
back:
Healed fractures from 5+ years ago typically do not preclude forearm DXA on that side; document the prior fracture and verify image clarity; if there is hardware or persistent bony deformity, scan the contralateral arm

A healed fracture from years ago typically does not preclude forearm DXA, particularly when fracture is well-healed and there is no hardware. Document the prior fracture and verify image clarity. If there is residual hardware (plate, screws, K-wire), bony deformity, or recent fracture (within 1 year), scan the contralateral arm. The fragility fracture history itself is clinically significant for osteoporosis diagnosis (per NBHA 2014 for hip; vertebral, proximal humerus, pelvis with osteopenia).

front: In primary hyperparathyroidism, the typical DXA pattern is:
back:
Lumbar spine relatively preserved (mostly trabecular), femoral neck mildly reduced, 33% radius preferentially reduced (most cortical site)

In primary hyperparathyroidism, the typical DXA pattern is: lumbar spine relatively preserved (high trabecular content; chronic continuous PTH preferentially affects cortical bone), femoral neck mildly reduced (mixed cortical-trabecular), 33% radius preferentially reduced (most cortical site, approximately 95 percent cortical). This pattern reflects the cortical-trabecular dichotomy of PTH effects. Post-parathyroidectomy recovery follows the inverse pattern: spine and femoral neck recover, 33% radius recovers slowly.

front: Before forearm DXA, the technologist should:
back:
Remove watches, bracelets, and rings if possible because metal jewelry can cause artifact in the scan field

Remove watches, bracelets, and rings from the forearm being scanned. Metal jewelry causes artifact (very high attenuation that the dual-energy algorithm cannot separate from bone) and can fall in the 33% radius ROI. The 33% radius is in the mid-radial diaphysis, distal to the elbow and proximal to the wrist; rings on fingers may not affect the 33% radius ROI but may affect ultradistal or mid-distal ROIs. Verify scan field clarity before starting.

front: FRAX 10-year fracture probability incorporates:
back:
Femoral neck BMD only (not 33% radius, not spine, not total hip)

FRAX is validated only with femoral neck BMD as the BMD input. The 33% radius BMD, spine BMD, and total hip BMD are NOT used in FRAX calculation. This means a patient diagnosed with osteoporosis at the 33% radius alone (e.g., in primary hyperparathyroidism) cannot have FRAX directly calculated unless femoral neck BMD is also available. The interpreting physician integrates the 33% radius result with clinical context.

front: A 65-year-old postmenopausal woman has 33% radius T-score of -2.7. The diagnosis per WHO 1994:
back:
Osteoporosis

Per WHO 1994 applied to the 33% radius (an ISCD-endorsed diagnostic site), T-score of -2.5 or below = osteoporosis. T-score -2.7 falls in the osteoporosis range without fragility fracture (severe requires fracture). Diagnosis: osteoporosis. The interpreting physician applies WHO classification to the lowest of available diagnostic sites (femoral neck, total hip, lumbar spine, 33% radius).

front: Per ISCD 2023, forearm BMD on a DXA report is reported to:
back:
3 decimal places (e.g., 0.781 g/cm^2)

Per ISCD 2023 Decimal Digits position, BMD at any site (forearm, hip, spine) is reported to 3 decimal places (e.g., 0.781 g/cm^2). BMC is reported to 2 decimal places. Area is reported to 2 decimal places. T-scores and Z-scores are reported to 1 decimal place (e.g., -2.5). Percent of reference is reported as a whole integer.

front: Forearm DXA radiation dose is:
back:
Lower than spine and hip DXA (the forearm has less overlying soft tissue)

Forearm DXA delivers lower radiation dose than spine or hip DXA because the forearm has much less overlying soft tissue and the scan field is smaller. Typical effective dose is well below 1 microSv. Apply ALARA: confirm pregnancy, use appropriate scan mode, no unnecessary repeats. The very low dose makes forearm DXA an attractive alternative when central DXA is precluded by body habitus.

front: The 33% radius (forearm DXA) is classified as:
back:
A site to which the WHO classification can be applied per ISCD 2023, along with femoral neck, total hip, and lumbar spine; diagnostic-capable on either central DXA or pDXA devices when a validated young-adult reference is used

Per ISCD 2023, the WHO osteoporosis classification can be applied at four sites: femoral neck, total hip, lumbar spine, and the one-third (33%) radius. The diagnostic distinction is anatomic (33% radius vs heel/phalanx), not based on central vs peripheral device class. ISCD 2023 states the WHO classification can be applied to the one-third radius ROI measured by DXA OR pDXA devices when a validated young-adult reference database is used. Peripheral DXA at the heel, finger, or phalanx (sites other than the 33% radius) is screening-only and not used for diagnosis. The 33% radius is approximately 95 percent cortical and is particularly useful in primary hyperparathyroidism.

front: For a very thin patient undergoing forearm DXA:
back:
Reduce dose by using a different scan mode if available; verify edge detection visually

For a very thin patient, less photon flux is needed because there is less soft tissue to penetrate. Use the manufacturer's recommended thin-patient or low-tissue scan mode if available; this reduces unnecessary dose (ALARA). Verify edge detection visually before accepting the analysis. Document the scan mode used; follow-up scans must use the same mode for valid comparison.

front: After auto-analysis of the forearm DXA, the technologist should:
back:
Visually verify ROI placement (33% point well-positioned on the radius), edge detection (clear bone borders), forearm length entry, and air on the ulnar side; manually adjust as needed

Automated forearm DXA analysis is a starting point. The technologist visually verifies: ROI placement (33% point well-positioned on the radius, ultradistal and mid-distal ROIs in correct positions), edge detection (clear bone borders), forearm length (consistent with the patient's anatomy), and air visible on the ulnar side. Manual ROI adjustment is made when needed. Document any deviations from default analysis.

front: For follow-up 33% radius DXA, the compare-prior tool:
back:
Copies the baseline ROI placement to the follow-up scan, ensuring identical analysis; the technologist verifies the copied ROI fits the new image

The compare-prior tool can be used at the forearm to copy the baseline ROI placement to the follow-up scan, ensuring identical analysis. The technologist verifies the copied ROI fits the new image; manual adjustment may be needed if forearm length or positioning differs. Reproducibility is essential for valid serial monitoring.

front: Per ISCD 2019 Pediatric, forearm DXA in children:
back:
Is a secondary site if PA spine and TBLH are unavailable and reference data exist

Per ISCD 2019 Pediatric Official Positions, the preferred sites for pediatric DXA are PA lumbar spine and total body less head (TBLH). The 33% radius is a secondary pediatric site if these primary sites are unavailable AND reference data exist for the child's age, sex, and ethnicity. Lateral distal femur (LDF) is recommended for non-ambulatory children. Pediatric DXA always uses Z-scores (not T-scores) and never diagnoses osteoporosis by Z-score alone (requires clinically significant fracture history).

front: A 60-year-old postmenopausal woman has 33% radius T-score of -0.8. Per WHO 1994:
back:
Normal

Per WHO 1994 applied to the 33% radius (an ISCD-endorsed diagnostic site), T-score -1.0 or above = normal. T-score -0.8 is in the normal range. The interpreting physician integrates this with other measured sites (lumbar spine, hip) to determine the overall diagnosis based on the lowest T-score. A normal forearm result alone does not exclude osteoporosis if other sites are below threshold.

front: For very obese patients, forearm DXA is reliable because:
back:
Body habitus does not affect forearm scanning since only the arm is in the scan field; the trunk weight is supported by chair (not table)

For very obese patients, forearm DXA is reliable because only the arm is in the scan field; the trunk weight is supported by the chair (not the scan table mechanism). This makes forearm DXA the alternative when patients exceed central scan-table weight limits. Body habitus does not affect forearm BMD measurement because there is little soft tissue overlying the radius. Per ISCD 2023, the 33% radius is the diagnostic alternative when central sites are unavailable.

front: On the patient history, prior wrist fragility fracture is:
back:
A clinically significant low-trauma fracture; consider whether to scan the contralateral arm if the wrist fracture occurred recently

A prior wrist fragility fracture (typically a Colles fracture from a fall on outstretched hand) is a clinically significant low-trauma fracture and a risk factor for future osteoporotic fractures. Document the fracture date and side. If the wrist fracture occurred recently (within 1 year) on the side scheduled for forearm DXA, consider scanning the contralateral arm to avoid measurement artifact during healing. Per WHO/ISCD/NBHA, a wrist fragility fracture with osteopenia (T -1.0 to -2.5) is diagnostic of osteoporosis.

front: For follow-up forearm DXA, the technologist should:
back:
Use the same arm scanned at baseline (non-dominant by default), document the side, and reproduce positioning

For follow-up forearm DXA, use the same arm scanned at baseline (non-dominant by default unless the contralateral was used due to contraindication). Document the side clearly on the report. Reproduce the same positioning (seated vs supine, neutral pronation, same forearm length entry) and ROI placement (compare-prior tool). Switching arms or positioning introduces artifactual BMD differences that may exceed the LSC.
source: ISCD 2023 Follow-up BMD; ARRT 2022 BD spec

front: A patient with body weight 425 lb / 193 kg has just exceeded the standard Hologic Horizon weight limit. The technologist:
back:
Performs forearm DXA (33% radius) per ISCD 2023 alternative diagnostic site indication; documents body weight and the indication

When a patient's body weight exceeds the central DXA scan-table weight limit (Hologic Horizon ~450 lb / 204 kg), the 33% radius is the alternative diagnostic site per ISCD 2023. The patient is positioned seated with the arm extended onto the scan table; only the arm is on the table. Document the body weight and the indication for forearm DXA. WHO classification can be applied at the 33% radius. Cross-calibration is not needed because the same scanner is used.

front: A patient has a relatively short radius. For forearm DXA:
back:
Verify forearm length measurement is accurate; manual ROI adjustment may be needed for the 33% point; document the variant

A short radius requires accurate forearm length measurement (ulnar styloid to olecranon) so the 33% point is calculated correctly. Manual ROI adjustment may be needed to ensure the 33% point falls on the radius (not on adjacent structures). Document the variant. Reproduce the same forearm length entry at follow-up scans. The 33% point is calculated from the forearm length, not from a fixed distance from a single landmark.

front: For initial osteoporosis evaluation, the standard diagnostic site priority is:
back:
Spine and hip first; 33% radius as alternative when spine or hip cannot be measured/interpreted, in primary hyperparathyroidism, or for patients exceeding scanner weight limit

Per ISCD 2023 and standard practice, the standard diagnostic site priority is: spine and hip first; the 33% radius is the alternative when spine or hip cannot be measured/interpreted (severe artifact, hardware), in primary hyperparathyroidism (PHPT preferentially affects cortical bone at 33% radius), or for patients exceeding scanner weight limit. Forearm-only osteoporosis evaluation is appropriate in these specific contexts. Peripheral DXA (heel, finger) is screening only and not used for diagnosis.

front: The ultradistal radius compared to the 33% radius:
back:
Is mostly trabecular bone (about 75 percent trabecular); shows earlier changes in postmenopausal bone loss but is reported, not used for WHO diagnosis

The ultradistal radius is approximately 75 percent trabecular bone, contrasting with the 33% radius (approximately 95 percent cortical). Trabecular bone has higher surface area and turnover, so the ultradistal radius can show earlier changes in postmenopausal bone loss. However, per ISCD 2023, the ultradistal radius is reported but NOT used for WHO diagnostic classification; only the 33% radius is the diagnostic forearm site. The mid-distal radius is intermediate.

front: The 33% (one-third) radius is approximately:
back:
Approximately 95 percent cortical bone

The 33% (one-third) radius is approximately 95 percent cortical bone (the cortex of the radial diaphysis is dense; very little trabecular bone). This makes the 33% radius the most cortical-rich DXA site and the recommended ROI for primary hyperparathyroidism evaluation. The ultradistal radius is predominantly trabecular (approximately 50-66 percent trabecular per Schlenker 1976 and Bonnick); the mid-distal radius is mixed cortical-trabecular.

front: For valid serial 33% radius BMD comparison, the technologist must:
back:
Reproduce the same patient position (seated or supine), same arm (non-dominant unless otherwise documented), same forearm length entry, same ROI placement

Valid serial 33% radius BMD monitoring requires reproducing the same patient position (seated vs supine per facility protocol), same arm (non-dominant unless otherwise documented), same forearm length entry (because the 33% point is calculated from forearm length), and same ROI placement using the compare-prior tool. Documenting the side and position on the report is essential.

front: A patient weighing 480 lb (218 kg) exceeds the central DXA scan-table weight limit. The 33% radius is the diagnostic alternative because:
back:
Forearm DXA can be performed with the patient seated and the arm extended onto the scan table; the body weight is off the table

For patients exceeding the central DXA scan-table weight limit (Hologic Horizon ~450 lb / 204 kg), the 33% radius can be measured with the patient seated and only the arm extended onto the scan table. The body weight is off the table mechanism, so the table can support the much smaller weight of the arm. Per ISCD 2023, forearm DXA is the alternative diagnostic site in this scenario.

front: Per ISCD 2023, an in vivo precision study at the 33% radius:
back:
Should be performed separately for each site (spine, hip, forearm); each site has its own LSC; precision is technologist-dependent

Per ISCD 2023, in vivo precision studies must be performed separately for each anatomic site (spine, hip, forearm). Each site has its own LSC. Precision is technologist-dependent (each technologist must establish their own LSC). The forearm precision study uses 30 patients scanned twice (or 15 patients three times) with full repositioning between scans.

front: After parathyroidectomy for primary hyperparathyroidism, BMD recovery is typically:
back:
Largest and earliest at the lumbar spine and femoral neck; the 33% radius recovers slowly and often incompletely

After parathyroidectomy for primary hyperparathyroidism (Silverberg 1995; Bilezikian 2022 Fifth Workshop), BMD recovery is largest and earliest at the lumbar spine and femoral neck (typically 8 to 12 percent gain over 1 to 5 years). The 33% radius (highly cortical) recovers slowly and often incompletely; cortical bone has slower remodeling rates.

front: For 33% radius T-score calculation, the reference database is:
back:
Hologic's NHANES-aligned forearm reference or manufacturer-specific reference; 33% radius does not have a NHANES III reference equivalent to hip and femoral neck

The 33% radius does not have a NHANES III reference database equivalent to the hip/femoral neck. Hologic uses a NHANES-aligned forearm reference; GE Lunar uses manufacturer-specific data. The lack of a uniform reference can cause T-score discrepancies between manufacturers. Document the manufacturer and software version used. ISCD 2023 acknowledges this limitation but endorses 33% radius as a valid diagnostic site when central sites are unavailable.

front: In primary hyperparathyroidism, the typical DXA pattern is:
back:
Lumbar spine relatively preserved, femoral neck mildly reduced, 33% radius preferentially reduced (most cortical site)

In primary hyperparathyroidism, the typical DXA pattern is: lumbar spine relatively preserved (high trabecular content; chronic continuous PTH preferentially affects cortical bone), femoral neck mildly reduced (mixed cortical-trabecular), 33% radius preferentially reduced (most cortical site, approximately 95 percent cortical). This pattern reflects the cortical-trabecular dichotomy of PTH effects.

front: Before forearm DXA, the technologist should:
back:
Remove watches, bracelets, and rings if possible because metal jewelry can cause artifact

Remove watches, bracelets, and rings from the forearm being scanned. Metal jewelry causes artifact (very high attenuation that the dual-energy algorithm cannot separate from bone) and can fall in the 33% radius ROI. Verify scan field clarity before starting.

front: FRAX 10-year fracture probability incorporates:
back:
Femoral neck BMD only (not 33% radius, not spine, not total hip)

FRAX is validated only with femoral neck BMD as the BMD input. The 33% radius BMD, spine BMD, and total hip BMD are NOT used in FRAX calculation. This means a patient diagnosed with osteoporosis at the 33% radius alone (e.g., in primary hyperparathyroidism) cannot have FRAX directly calculated unless femoral neck BMD is also available.

front: A 60-year-old postmenopausal woman has 33% radius T-score of -0.8. Per WHO 1994:
back:
Normal

Per WHO 1994 applied to the 33% radius (an ISCD-endorsed diagnostic site), T-score -1.0 or above = normal. T-score -0.8 is in the normal range. The interpreting physician integrates this with other measured sites (lumbar spine, hip) to determine the overall diagnosis based on the lowest T-score.

front: Per ISCD 2023, forearm BMD on a DXA report is reported to:
back:
3 decimal places (e.g., 0.781 g/cm^2)

Per ISCD 2023 Decimal Digits position, BMD at any site (forearm, hip, spine) is reported to 3 decimal places (e.g., 0.781 g/cm^2). BMC is reported to 2 decimal places. Area is reported to 2 decimal places. T-scores and Z-scores are reported to 1 decimal place. Percent of reference is reported as a whole integer.

front: Forearm DXA radiation dose is:
back:
Lower than spine and hip DXA (the forearm has less overlying soft tissue)

Forearm DXA delivers lower radiation dose than spine or hip DXA because the forearm has much less overlying soft tissue and the scan field is smaller. Typical effective dose is well below 1 microSv. Apply ALARA: confirm pregnancy, use appropriate scan mode, no unnecessary repeats. The very low dose makes forearm DXA an attractive alternative when central DXA is precluded by body habitus.

front: The 33% radius (forearm DXA) is classified as:
back:
A central-equivalent diagnostic site per ISCD 2023 (along with spine and hip)

The 33% radius (forearm DXA on a central DXA scanner) is classified as a central-equivalent diagnostic site per ISCD 2023, along with the lumbar spine and proximal femur. WHO 1994 classification can be applied. This is distinct from peripheral DXA performed on dedicated peripheral devices (heel, finger, wrist), which are screening tools only and not used for diagnosis.

front: For a very thin patient undergoing forearm DXA:
back:
Reduce dose by using a different scan mode if available; verify edge detection visually

For a very thin patient, less photon flux is needed because there is less soft tissue to penetrate. Use the manufacturer's recommended thin-patient or low-tissue scan mode if available; this reduces unnecessary dose (ALARA). Verify edge detection visually before accepting the analysis. Document the scan mode used; follow-up scans must use the same mode.

front: After auto-analysis of the forearm DXA, the technologist should:
back:
Visually verify ROI placement (33% point well-positioned on the radius), edge detection, forearm length entry, and air on the ulnar side

Automated forearm DXA analysis is a starting point. The technologist visually verifies: ROI placement (33% point well-positioned on the radius, ultradistal and mid-distal ROIs in correct positions), edge detection (clear bone borders), forearm length (consistent with the patient's anatomy), and air visible on the ulnar side. Manual ROI adjustment is made when needed.

front: For follow-up 33% radius DXA, the compare-prior tool:
back:
Copies the baseline ROI placement to the follow-up scan, ensuring identical analysis

The compare-prior tool can be used at the forearm to copy the baseline ROI placement to the follow-up scan, ensuring identical analysis. The technologist verifies the copied ROI fits the new image; manual adjustment may be needed if forearm length or positioning differs. Reproducibility is essential for valid serial monitoring.

front: Per ISCD 2019 Pediatric, forearm DXA in children:
back:
Is a secondary site if PA spine and TBLH are unavailable and reference data exist

Per ISCD 2019 Pediatric Official Positions, the preferred sites for pediatric DXA are PA lumbar spine and total body less head (TBLH). The 33% radius is a secondary pediatric site if these primary sites are unavailable AND reference data exist for the child's age, sex, and ethnicity. Lateral distal femur (LDF) is recommended for non-ambulatory children. Pediatric DXA always uses Z-scores (not T-scores) and never diagnoses osteoporosis by Z-score alone.

front: Vertebral Fracture Assessment (VFA) on DXA is performed to:
back:
Identify vertebral fractures using lateral DXA imaging without measuring BMD

VFA identifies vertebral fractures using lateral DXA imaging. It does NOT measure BMD; it is an imaging assessment for fracture morphology. VFA uses Genant semi-quantitative grading. Per ISCD 2023, VFA visualizes T7-L4; T1-T3 are typically not visualized. PA spine DXA still measures BMD; VFA is complementary, not a replacement. The two are typically performed together when VFA is indicated.

front: Per ISCD 2023, one of the indications for VFA is historical height loss:
back:
More than 4 cm (or more than 1.5 in) from peak adult height

Per ISCD 2023, historical height loss > 4 cm (> 1.5 in) from peak adult height is an indication for VFA. The loss suggests occult vertebral compression fractures, often asymptomatic and missed without imaging. Other ISCD 2023 indications: T-score below thresholds with risk factors (women 70+ or men 80+ if T-score < -1.0 anywhere; women 65-69 or men 70-79 if T-score < -1.5; younger postmenopausal/older men with significant risk factors and T-score < -1.5), self-reported but undocumented prior vertebral fracture, glucocorticoid use 5 mg/d for 3+ months.

front: VFA is indicated in patients on chronic glucocorticoid therapy when the dose and duration meet:
back:
Prednisone-equivalent 5 mg/day or more for 3 months or more (cumulative)

Per ISCD 2023, chronic glucocorticoid use at prednisone-equivalent 5 mg/day or more for 3 months or more is an indication for VFA. Glucocorticoids increase vertebral fracture risk preferentially (trabecular bone effect, suppression of osteoblast function, possible direct osteocyte apoptosis).
