The threshold is the same as for FRAX glucocorticoid input. Higher doses (7.5 mg/day or more) confer even higher risk. Document the dose and duration.

front: On Genant semi-quantitative VFA grading, Grade 1 (mild) vertebral fracture is defined as:
back:
20-25% height loss with 10-20% area reduction

Per Genant 1993, semi-quantitative VFA grading: Grade 0 = normal, Grade 1 (mild) = approximately 20-25% height loss with about 10-20% area reduction, Grade 2 (moderate) = 26-40% height loss with 21-40% area reduction, Grade 3 (severe) = more than 40% height loss with more than 40% area reduction. The grading combines anterior, middle, or posterior vertebral height reduction with the resulting area reduction. Grade 1 is the threshold for clinically significant fracture.

front: Genant Grade 3 (severe) vertebral fracture corresponds to:
back:
More than 40% height loss with more than 40% area reduction

Per Genant 1993, Grade 3 (severe) vertebral fracture corresponds to more than 40% height loss with more than 40% area reduction. Grade 3 represents the most severe morphologic change. The grades are: Grade 0 normal, Grade 1 (mild) 20-25%, Grade 2 (moderate) 26-40%, Grade 3 (severe) >40%. Clinical action: any Grade 1 or higher fracture diagnoses osteoporosis (when combined with osteopenia BMD or per NBHA criteria) and warrants antiresorptive treatment.

front: The most common location for osteoporotic vertebral fractures is:
back:
The thoracolumbar junction (T11-L2)

The most common location for osteoporotic vertebral fractures is the thoracolumbar junction (T11-L2). The biomechanical transition between the kyphotic thoracic and lordotic lumbar spine concentrates compressive stress at this junction, particularly during forward bending. Wedge fractures predominate at the thoracolumbar junction; biconcave fractures predominate at the mid-lumbar level. Knowing the typical fracture distribution helps the technologist focus visual review on the most likely sites.

front: VFA radiation dose compared to a standard lateral spine X-ray:
back:
Substantially lower (approximately 1-50 microSv vs ~600 microSv for lateral spine X-ray)

VFA delivers approximately 1-50 microSv (depending on scan mode and patient size), substantially lower than a standard lateral spine X-ray (approximately 600 microSv). VFA's lower dose makes it the preferred initial imaging tool for vertebral fracture assessment in osteoporosis. When VFA is non-diagnostic (poor visualization, equivocal findings), lateral spine X-ray can supplement at higher dose. The dose advantage is a key reason VFA has replaced spine X-ray for routine fracture screening in BMD-monitored patients.

front: Per ISCD 2023, the VFA report should include:
back:
Number of vertebrae visualized, any unevaluable vertebrae, deformed vertebrae and whether the deformity is consistent with fracture (with grade if applicable), unexplained pathology

Per ISCD 2023, the VFA report should include: number of vertebrae visualized (T7-L4 standard), any unevaluable vertebrae, deformed vertebrae and whether the deformity is consistent with fracture (with Genant grade if applicable), and unexplained pathology (when present, recommend additional imaging). The patient ID, indication for VFA, technical quality, and interpretation are required. The interpreting physician makes the determination of fracture vs variant.

front: A previously asymptomatic patient with normal BMD has a Genant Grade 2 vertebral fracture identified on VFA. Per NBHA 2014:
back:
With osteopenia BMD (T -1.0 to -2.5) the vertebral fragility fracture is diagnostic of osteoporosis; however with normal BMD the diagnosis is less clear and depends on clinical context

Per NBHA 2014 (Siris et al.), a low-trauma vertebral fragility fracture in a patient with osteopenia BMD (T-score -1.0 to -2.5) is diagnostic of osteoporosis. However, with normal BMD, the diagnosis is less clear; the interpreting physician evaluates clinical context (mechanism, secondary causes). A vertebral fracture in a patient with truly normal BMD raises the differential of secondary causes (multiple myeloma, malignancy, trauma history). VFA detects the fracture; clinical workup determines significance. Note: low-trauma HIP fracture alone is diagnostic of osteoporosis regardless of BMD.

front: On VFA, vertebral labeling uses:
back:
Identification of T12 by lowest pair of ribs (when visible), iliac crest landmark, and counting from these landmarks; document any anatomic variants

On VFA, vertebral labeling uses the same anatomic landmarks as PA spine DXA: T12 identified by the lowest pair of ribs (when visible on lateral image), iliac crest landmark crossing typically at L4 body or L4-L5 disc space, and counting from these landmarks. Document any anatomic variants (transitional vertebrae). Consistent labeling across baseline and follow-up is essential for valid serial monitoring.

front: On follow-up DXA, the technologist should also perform VFA when:
back:
The patient meets ISCD 2023 indications (continued risk factors, new height loss > 2 cm, new back pain, glucocorticoid use); the goal is to detect new vertebral fractures, which signal treatment failure

On follow-up DXA, VFA should be performed when the patient meets ISCD 2023 indications. Common follow-up VFA indications: continued risk factors, new height loss > 2 cm during observation (a clinical-practice trigger), new back pain, ongoing glucocorticoid use. The goal is to detect new vertebral fractures, which represent treatment failure and warrant clinical review (medication adherence, change of therapy). VFA is not indicated at every follow-up automatically; the clinical situation guides the decision.

front: During VFA review, the technologist:
back:
Reviews technical image quality, ensures all visualized vertebrae from T7-L4 are evaluable, documents any unevaluable vertebrae, and forwards the scan for interpreting physician review

During VFA review, the technologist's role is to: review technical image quality, ensure all visualized vertebrae from T7-L4 are evaluable, document any unevaluable vertebrae (e.g., due to motion, overlap, poor contrast), and forward the scan for interpreting physician review. The technologist does NOT diagnose fractures, provide treatment recommendations, or discuss findings with the patient; those are the interpreting physician's and ordering physician's roles.

front: VFA adds to BMD assessment by:
back:
Identifying prevalent vertebral fractures (~30-50% asymptomatic), which independently predict future fracture risk and meet diagnostic criteria when combined with BMD findings

VFA adds clinical value to BMD assessment by identifying prevalent vertebral fractures, which are asymptomatic in approximately 30-50% of cases. Prevalent vertebral fractures independently predict future fracture risk (vertebral fractures, hip fractures, mortality). Per NBHA 2014, a low-trauma vertebral fragility fracture with osteopenia BMD diagnoses osteoporosis; per the WHO criteria, vertebral fragility fracture plus T-score -2.5 or below diagnoses severe osteoporosis. Without VFA, many of these clinically important fractures would be missed.

front: A wedge fracture on VFA is characterized by:
back:
Anterior vertebral height loss with relatively preserved posterior height

A wedge fracture on VFA is characterized by anterior vertebral height loss with relatively preserved posterior height. The vertebra appears wedge-shaped on the lateral projection, with the anterior aspect shorter than the posterior. Per Genant 1993 and subsequent epidemiologic data, vertebral fractures show a BIMODAL distribution with peaks at the mid-thoracic spine (T7-T8) and at the thoracolumbar junction (T12-L1). L1 is the single most commonly fractured vertebra, closely followed by T12. The mid-thoracic peak reflects kyphotic curvature; the thoracolumbar junction peak reflects compression force concentration during forward bending. Genant grading combines the anterior height loss percentage with the resulting area reduction.

front: A biconcave fracture on VFA is characterized by:
back:
Mid-vertebral height loss with relatively preserved anterior and posterior heights (the vertebra has a 'fish-mouth' appearance on lateral projection)

A biconcave fracture on VFA is characterized by mid-vertebral height loss with relatively preserved anterior and posterior heights. The vertebra has a 'fish-mouth' appearance on the lateral projection (concave on top and bottom). Biconcave fractures are most common at the mid-lumbar level. Genant grading uses the mid-vertebral height loss percentage. Differentiate biconcave fractures from physiologic Cupid's bow variant (a normal mid-lumbar concavity that does NOT represent fracture).
